audits.summary: MeetingScribe has a warm Bloom design system (NDS with coral, lilac, and mint) but suffers from inconsistent component adoption, hardcoded color leaks, scattered typography, and underutilized visual hierarchy patterns. The People tab (relationship coach) is especially clinical despite warm copy intent. Key gaps: chip/button style multiplicity, RelationshipType.color stub not implemented, magic-number spacing/radii, motion not consistently gated, and semantic relationships don't signal emotionally at a glance.

recommendations.title: Implement RelationshipType.color computed property backed by NDS palette
recommendations.description: Add `color: Color` property to RelationshipType enum in Person.swift, mapping each case (.romanticPartner, .familyMember, .closeFriend, .friend, .colleague, .acquaintance) to NDS.palette colors. Replace the dead `colorName` stub that returns strings with no runtime resolution. This unblocks three downstream improvements: StayConnectedSection color coding, relationship badges in PersonRow, and graph edge coloring by relationship type rather than meeting density.
recommendations.userValue: Visual hierarchy in the People list — users can identify close relationships at a glance by color without reading text. Consistent relationship signaling across all people-related views.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Direct continuation of D3-visual-v2.md D3-1; fills a design stub that exists but produces zero visual output.
recommendations.dependencies: None; no schema migration needed.

recommendations.title: Standardize button system: collapse UntitledPrimary/Secondary into MS* family
recommendations.description: TodayView and ActionItemsView use two parallel primary-button styles: UntitledPrimaryButtonStyle (padding-based, drop-shadow) and MSPrimaryButtonStyle (fixed-height, no shadow). Both appear in the same TodayView (:133, :177 vs :297), creating visual inconsistency. Retire UntitledPrimary/Secondary as aliases to MS* family, update ~3–5 call sites. Keep NotionDesign.swift's documented MS button family as canonical.
recommendations.userValue: Brand-purple primary buttons now have uniform height, shadow, and padding across tabs; users build consistent affordance recognition.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From UX09_consistency.md UX9-2; pure visual fix, zero behavior change.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Extract MSSectionHeader and apply to StayConnectedSection + TodayView cards
recommendations.description: StayConnectedSection (Sources/MeetingScribe/UI/StayConnectedSection.swift:38–44) uses hand-rolled HStack header with `.system(size: 15, weight: .semibold)` and `.pink.opacity(0.06)` background. MSComponents.swift already defines MSSectionHeader, but StayConnectedSection does not use it. Replace the bespoke header + card styling with msCard() modifier + MSSectionHeader to align with TodayView's other section cards. Apply the RelationshipType.color from Rec-1 so the section tint matches the person's relationship type.
recommendations.userValue: TodayView reads as a unified feed instead of a patchwork of pink/gold sections; consistent spacing and typography across all sections.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Elevates StayConnectedSection from a one-off styled component to use the shared NDS surface primitives introduced in Phase 6.
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (RelationshipType.color for tinting).

recommendations.title: Standardize corner radii and spacing: create NDS.feedSpacing and NDS.sectionSpacing tokens
recommendations.description: Hardcoded cornerRadius values scatter throughout: 7 (ActionItemsChrome), 8 (NotionDesign), 10 (StayConnectedSection, QuickEncounterSheet), 12 (cardRadius), 14 (MeetingCard), 20 (cardRadius for Bloom). Spacing is similarly ad-hoc: StayConnectedSection uses `spacing: 10` internally vs TodayView `spacing: 20-22`. Add two tokens to NDS: `static let feedSpacing: CGFloat = 22` and `static let sectionItemSpacing: CGFloat = 10`, then apply to TodayView feeds and all card containers. Audit MeetingCard/ActionItems for cornerRadius consistency (prefer NDS.cardRadius or NDS.rowRadius over literals).
recommendations.userValue: Feeds have predictable, scaled vertical rhythm; visual density feels considered rather than jumbled as new sections are added.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-8; formalizes spacing that today is implicit/scattered.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Create SelectionTile NDS component (replaces KindChip/MoodChip ad-hoc pattern)
recommendations.description: QuickEncounterSheet defines KindChip (cornerRadius: 10) and MoodChip (cornerRadius: 8) with inconsistent borders, colors, and animations. These are a 3×2 grid selection pattern that will repeat in coaching framework picker, onboarding relationship setup, and future mood check-ins. Build SelectionTile in MSComponents.swift: a rectangular tile with emoji/icon + label, using NDS.fieldBg inactive, NDS.brand.opacity(0.14) active, NDS.cardRadius corners, and .spring animation. Refactor KindChip/MoodChip onto it.
recommendations.userValue: Consistent tap feedback across all selection surfaces; reduce-motion aware via NDS.motion guard; pattern can be reused for coaching, settings, onboarding without divergence.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-3; extracts a reusable pattern before it multiplies.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Migrate ProPaywallView to NDS brand tokens (gradient + button + bullet colors)
recommendations.description: ProPaywallView (Sources/MeetingScribe/UI/ProPaywallView.swift) uses raw `.pink/.purple` gradient (line 20) instead of `[NDS.palette[7].color, NDS.brand]`, raw `.tint(.purple)` CTA (line 76) instead of `.tint(NDS.brand)`, and six literal ProBullet colors (lines 37–52) instead of `NDS.selectColor('orange')` etc. Three separate color leaks in one 100-line paywall. Replace all three with NDS tokens to match the app's warm neutral palette.
recommendations.userValue: Paywall looks polished and matches the app's design language instead of using generic iOS gradients; users perceive higher trust in a coherent premium surface.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-6; monetization-critical screen should reflect design language.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Add msEmphasisCard(tint:) modifier for overdue/attention-needed rows
recommendations.description: StayConnectedSection applies `Color.pink.opacity(0.06)` background directly. Future 'distress signal' banners and overdue indicators will improvise their own colored fills. Build msEmphasisCard(tint: Color) in MSComponents.swift: applies NDS.fieldBg base fill + 2pt left accent border in `tint` + faint `tint.opacity(0.07)` overlay. Removes the one-off pink background and establishes a reusable 'this needs attention' visual language (Notion/Linear convention).
recommendations.userValue: Colored left borders signal urgency level at a glance; consistent visual scanning cue across all card surfaces.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-2; preemptively defines the pattern before it spreads.
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (so tint can come from RelationshipType.color).

recommendations.title: Add hover state feedback to KindChip and MoodChip (macOS affordance)
recommendations.description: KindChip and MoodChip in QuickEncounterSheet (Sources/MeetingScribe/People/QuickEncounterSheet.swift:207–248) lack `.onHover` handlers. QuickActionCard and NotionIconButton define the pattern, but chips don't use it. macOS pointer-driven interaction expects hover = visual feedback. Add `@State private var hovering` + `.onHover { hovering = $0 }` + `.scaleEffect(hovering ? 1.03 : 1.0)` guarded by `NDS.motion(_:, reduce:)` for Reduce Motion.
recommendations.userValue: Chips feel like native macOS components; users get clear affordance that they're interactive.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-7; completes the platform-appropriate interaction model.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Unify FilterChip and NotionChip implementations (consolidate two-chip system)
recommendations.description: PeopleListView defines FilterChip and NotionDesign defines NotionChip — they serve the same purpose (tag/status pills) but differ in shape details (padding, font size token, capsule geometry). They will diverge further as new uses are added. Consolidate into one component: either make FilterChip a wrapper around NotionChip with an `active:` state parameter, or merge NotionChip's properties into FilterChip. Add `active` parameter to the canonical chip.
recommendations.userValue: One chip implementation prevents future divergences; easier to maintain visual consistency.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual-v2.md D3-9; prevents future multiplicity.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Promote user photos to hero avatar position in PersonDetailView
recommendations.description: PersonDetailView renders photos in a horizontal-scroll strip below Tags/Contact fields (Sources/MeetingScribe/People/PersonDetailView.swift:~1027–1058), never promoted to the hero 52pt avatar position. When `current.photoRelativePaths` is non-empty, render the first photo as the identity-panel avatar via CachedThumbnail; fall back to the initials circle only when no photo exists. Single if/else branch in the `identityPanel` view builder.
recommendations.userValue: A photo of a friend or partner makes the view feel personal immediately. Visual differentiation between contacts who have photos and those who don't.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual.md D3-1; photos already exist but are buried; moving one asset to hero position.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Add avatar color ring by relationship depth (RelationshipType-coded border)
recommendations.description: Add a 2pt color ring around each person's avatar in PersonRow (PeopleListView) and the 52pt avatar in PersonDetailView.identityPanel. Ring color derives from RelationshipType.color (Rec-1): partner → rose, family → amber, closeFriend → teal, colleague → gray. No ring for unset/other. One `.overlay(Circle().strokeBorder(...))` per avatar view. At list density, even a 2pt ring is scannable at a glance.
recommendations.userValue: Users can immediately spot partners and close friends in a long people list without reading names; fast visual hierarchy in the relationship graph.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual.md D3-6; leverages the RelationshipType.color from Rec-1.
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (RelationshipType.color).

recommendations.title: Warm section header typography for intimate relationship types
recommendations.description: When a person's primary RelationshipCategory is .partner or .family, replace Notion-style uppercase eyebrow labels with lowercase, lighter-weight section headers. Use `Font.system(.body, weight: .regular)` in NDS.textSecondary instead of the current tracked caps. Change placeholder text: 'FAVORITE THINGS' → 'What you love about them', 'ENCOUNTERS' → 'Together', 'MEMORIES' → 'Shared moments'. Section keys unchanged; only typography register and copy. Zero schema changes.
recommendations.userValue: 'FAVORITE THINGS' in tracked caps reads like a CRM field; 'What you love about them' reads like a relationship journal. Emotional register matches the app's positioning as a relationship coach.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual.md D3-5; cheapest warmth lever (typography only).
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (to detect relationship category).

recommendations.title: Add RelationshipHealth widget below identity panel (emotional tenor at a glance)
recommendations.description: For persons with category .partner, .family, or .closeFriend, add a RelationshipHealth widget immediately below PersonDetailView.identityPanel. Display: (a) days since last check-in with color-coded dot (green < threshold, yellow approaching, red overdue), (b) dominant sentiment from recent AttachedNote if available, (c) one-tap 'Log a moment' button (opens minimal check-in sheet, saves as Encounter with kind: .checkIn). Suppress for .colleague and .other categories. Data model (encounters, attached notes) already supports all three signals.
recommendations.userValue: Creates 'relationship dashboard' feel at the top of the person view — the signal-at-a-glance that differentiates a personal coach from a contact manager.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual.md D3-7; differentiating feature for relationship-coach positioning.
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (to identify person's primary relationship type).

recommendations.title: Redesign RelationshipType emoji set for visual coherence
recommendations.description: Current emoji set (💑👨‍👩‍👧🤝😊💼👋👤) mixes incompatible styles (three different emoji families). Choose a coherent style: Option A uses a heart-language set (❤️ partner, 🏠 family, 🌟 closeFriend, 😄 friend, 🤝 colleague, 👋 acquaintance, 👤 unset); Option B adopts SF Symbols rendered in RelationshipType.color (heart.fill, house.fill, star.fill, etc.) for system consistency and light/dark awareness. Update Person.swift's RelationshipType.emoji property.
recommendations.userValue: Consistent visual vocabulary across relationship type indicators; users build mental models faster when the icon set reads as designed rather than assembled.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From D3-visual.md D3-10; cosmetic polish but improves perceived craft.
recommendations.dependencies: Rec-1 (if switching to SF Symbols in RelationshipType.color).

recommendations.title: Audit MeetingCard + ActionItems corner radii for NDS token consistency
recommendations.description: MeetingCard uses `cornerRadius: 14` (hardcoded); ActionItemsGalleryView uses 10 (hardcoded); ActionItemsChrome uses 7 and 10 (scattered); MeetingDetailHeader uses 14. NDS defines `cardRadius: 20`, `rowRadius: 12`, `radius: 14`. Audit all `RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: N)` calls in UI/ and People/ directories. Standardize card surfaces to `NDS.cardRadius` (20), rows to `NDS.rowRadius` (12), and controls to `NDS.radius` (14). Document the scale in NotionDesign.swift comments for future audits.
recommendations.userValue: Rounded-corner scale is now predictable and enforceable; visual coherence across all surfaces.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Cleanup of scattered values; reinforces NDS as single source of truth.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Gate all animation/motion effects through NDS.motion() + Reduce Motion environment
recommendations.description: Motion is inconsistent: some uses gate via `NDS.motion(_, reduce:)` (MeetingCard, MSPillTabs), others use hardcoded `.easeInOut(duration:)` with no Reduce Motion guard (KindChip has `.easeInOut(duration: 0.12)` AND `.easeInOut(duration: 0.15)` at same call site). Some surfaces (AttendeeChip, PersonRow) have animation but no Reduce Motion aware conditional. Audit all `withAnimation` / `.animation` calls. Replace hardcoded durations with named `NDS.motion` springs and guarantee a `@Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion)` guard on every one.
recommendations.userValue: Users with motion sensitivity get a predictably-safe experience; animation feels snappy across all surfaces when enabled.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Accessibility + consistency pass; builds on NDS.motion infrastructure that already exists.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

audits.summary: The app's navigation architecture is stronger than prior audits documented: the WorkspaceRouter centralized meeting selection, and MeetingsView uses a native NavigationSplitView pattern. However, three core IA problems remain unresolved—Today still bypasses the canonical Meetings detail pane; People/Tasks live in conflicting split-view implementations; and the nav model lacks cross-tab fluidity and selection memory—blocking the "one connected workspace" vision that prior plans identify as critical. This audit proposes concrete IA refinements that enforce a single native split pattern, unify selection across tabs, and restore the orphaned calendar/list-mode feature.

recommendations.title: Unify all split-view panes under one native NavigationSplitView shell
recommendations.description: MeetingsView (file:53) uses a native NavigationSplitView correctly, but PeopleListView (PeopleListView.swift:92) uses an HSplitView, and ActionItemsView (ActionItemsView.swift:133) uses hand-rolled HStack + divider with manual width tracking. This fracture forces three parallel resize/layout codepaths, breaks native ⌘[ back/forward behavior, and makes sidebar state non-persistent. Replace all three with a unified wrapper component that adopts one native NavigationSplitView per tab, storing sidebar visibility/width via @SceneStorage keyed per section. Retire NDS.splitPaneTopInset magic numbers (60pt) and the inline `.padding(.top, 60)` hacks in PeopleListView.swift:133; the native API handles it. This resolves the layout-jumping bug that audit_ux.md flags as UX-1 partial-credit item.
recommendations.userValue: Sidebars stop drifting width across tabs; keyboard shortcuts work everywhere; users can toggle sidebars with standard macOS affordances (⌘^S pattern); native feel on any Mac version.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plans (V5 CM-1/CM-2, V5 DL-1/DL-5) specified this at a high level; this recommendation operationalizes it: define one shared NavigationSplitView wrapper, migrate three existing split panes, delete the hand-rolled resize logic.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pure UI refactor. Does not require the cross-tab entity graph or cache-first launch snapshot from V5, though benefits from them.

recommendations.title: Route Today's meeting cards into the canonical Meetings tab detail via WorkspaceRouter
recommendations.description: TodayView.swift shows inline-expanded meeting details (lines 23, 208, 245, 296) with a 'Collapse' button, violating the audit's non-negotiable #4 ('NEVER collapse/expand'). Instead, make TodayView meeting cards call router.openMeeting(m), which sets router.section = .meetings AND router.selectedMeetingID = m.id. This routes the user into the same canonical Meetings-tab detail pane that works from search/backlinks/deep-links. Delete the expandedMeetingID state and cardWithDetail view (~50 lines). Removes the duplicate presentation layer and unifies all four meeting-open pathways (Meetings list, Today cards, search, backlinks) into one. This is already partially implemented: WorkspaceRouter.openMeeting() exists (line 140+), it just isn't called from Today's cards.
recommendations.userValue: One consistent way to open a meeting; clicking a card in Today navigates cleanly instead of jamming a detail panel into a card; users land on the Meetings tab when they open a meeting (clear IA, not surprising), and the detail pane uses the full-height layout that transcripts need.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (audit_ux.md UX-3 P0) identified Today's expand/collapse as 'still-broken (violates #4)' and suggests this exact fix. This recommendation confirms it and specifies the implementation: delete inlineDetail, call router.openMeeting on card tap.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter to own section selection (already done, D1-1). Benefits from unified split-view pane above, but can ship independently.

recommendations.title: Add shared selectedPersonID to WorkspaceRouter and route all person-open paths through it
recommendations.description: WorkspaceRouter owns selectedMeetingID (line 28, WorkspaceRouter.swift) but has no selectedPersonID. Person selection today is local @State in PeopleListView.swift:20, and opening a person from search uses NotificationCenter (line 125–127) to post meetingScribeOpenPerson. This creates three incompatible pathways: (1) list selection in People tab, (2) search/notification jump, (3) attendee chip clicks in meeting detail (not wired to navigate to People). Add @Published var selectedPersonID to WorkspaceRouter, add an openPerson(p: Person) method (parallel to openMeeting), and route all three pathways through it. In PeopleListView, bind $selection to $router.selectedPersonID. Update MeetingPersonConnectPanel and attendee chips to call router.openPerson(). Delete the meetingScribeOpenPerson notification. This makes person selection as unified and persistent as meeting selection is now.
recommendations.userValue: Clicking an attendee chip in a meeting opens that person in the People tab (currently leads nowhere or posts an ignored notification). Opening a person from search lands on the People tab with that person selected (consistent with meeting behavior). Selection persists across app relaunch via WorkspaceRouter's UserDefaults persistence pattern.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plans (V5 SD-1 DN-7) call for 'shared selectedPersonID in WorkspaceRouter'; this operationalizes it with specific file locations and removes the parallel NotificationCenter pathway.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires the unified NavigationSplitView pattern above (so PeopleListView has a proper detail pane to select into). Does not require cross-tab entity graph.

recommendations.title: Establish a NavHistory breadcrumb trail in MainWindow that shows the current navigation path
recommendations.description: WorkspaceRouter already maintains a backStack and forwardStack (lines 49–50, 84–88), but only the top-level section and selectedMeetingID are tracked. Add a breadcrumb view in MainWindow (above the tabContent, inside the HStack with the nav rail) that displays the path: e.g., 'Today > Standup Digest > Alice' or 'Meetings > Engineering Sync > Transcript'. Consume the router's currentState + selectedMeetingID to build the breadcrumb, with each crumb tappable to jump back. This surfacing clarifies the IA for users who open multiple entities in sequence and forget which tab they're viewing. It also provides visual confirmation of the router's state, making it easier to debug navigation bugs.
recommendations.userValue: Users always know where they are in the app hierarchy; they can jump back two steps with a click instead of using ⌘[ twice; the visual trail reduces disorientation on large monitors where the tab rail is far from the content.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not explicitly mentioned in prior plans, but aligns with V5 DN-1 ('workspace breadcrumb spine'). This is a distinct breadcrumb implementation that leverages the router's already-built history stacks.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter with meeting/person selection (already in place). Does not block other improvements.

recommendations.title: Restore month/week calendar view as list-mode toggle in MeetingsView, remove orphaned CalendarTabView
recommendations.description: CalendarTabView.swift (~500 lines, never referenced in current nav) contains month-grid and week-kanban view logic that audit_ux.md flags as 'orphaned … completely unreachable' (UX-2 P0). MeetingsView.swift already has a listMode toggle (lines 35, 39–44, 125–133) that switches between .list and .month, but the .month case is never implemented. Extract CalendarTabView's month-grid layout into the .month case of MeetingsView, wired to router.selectedMeetingID (so clicking a day selects the meeting in detail, not opening a separate view). This resurrects the lost calendar UI without breaking the unified navigation model, and deletes dead code. The week view (kanban of meetings by status) is lower priority; ship month-view first.
recommendations.userValue: Users can view meetings by calendar date in the same Meetings tab without context-switching; the month view serves a different cognitive model ('When did that meeting happen?') vs. the list ('Show me the meeting I'm looking for'). Recovers a feature that was built but became invisible.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (UX-2, option B) suggests 'reexpose month/week as a view-mode toggle inside MeetingsView, or delete CalendarTabView.' This operationalizes the first option: wire the existing listMode toggle to the extracted calendar logic, delete the dead file.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires MeetingsView to use NavigationSplitView (already does). Does not require cross-tab integration. Can extract CalendarTabView logic without waiting for other IA changes.

recommendations.title: Add a 'recents rail' in the nav sidebar showing last 5 opened entities (meetings/people/tasks), with ⌘K as quick-switcher
recommendations.description: Prior audit (V5 DN-4 / CP-5) proposes 'Recently viewed rail + quick-switcher.' Implement by extending WorkspaceRouter to track (section, entityID, timestamp) tuples and persist them to UserDefaults as a JSON array (capped at 5 most-recent). In MainWindow's navRail, add a 'Recents' collapsible section below the main nav groups that shows the 5 most-recent entities with their icon, title, and a hover-dismiss button. Global Search (GlobalSearchView.swift, invoked by ⌘K MainWindow.swift:163) already has a search palette; add a 'Recents' tab at the top showing the same 5 entities so ⌘K → click → land on recent meeting/person/task in 2 keystrokes. This drastically cuts reopen friction for the daily workflows that prior audits identify ('reopening a meeting is the most-repeated action'; 3→1 clicks).
recommendations.userValue: Users spend <1s finding and re-opening the meeting they were just looking at (common: interrupt, context-switch, re-open). Addresses FT1-2 from UX_QUICKWINS_PLAN.md ('Recently-visited entities + pinned/favorites as a quick-switch row').
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plan (V5 DN-4, UX_QUICKWINS_PLAN.md FT1-2) identified this; this operationalizes the routing through WorkspaceRouter's existing state.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter with section/meeting/person selection (in place). Does not require unified split-view or other IA changes, but benefits from them.

recommendations.title: Move Meetings scope default to .upcoming and persist via @AppStorage
recommendations.description: MeetingsView.swift line 32 defaults scope to @AppStorage-backed .upcoming (this was already done per the audit comments), but the audit also flags (UX-2 P1) 'Meetings scope default & non-persistence.' Verify the persisted scope is actually working, and that the tab defaults to .upcoming on cold-open (non-negotiable #6 from audit_ux.md, 'Upcoming-first default'). If scope is not persisting, it's because the @AppStorage key was changed or the view is not respecting the stored value. Check MeetingsView.swift:32 and ensure @AppStorage("meetings.scope") reads/writes correctly. This is a quick win to lock in the audit's intended default behavior.
recommendations.userValue: Users open the Meetings tab and see upcoming calls first, not a jumbled 'all' view. The choice is remembered across app restarts.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (audit_ux.md UX-2 P1) flags this; it appears partially implemented (line 32 shows @AppStorage), so this is a verification + polish task, not a new feature.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pure view-state fix.

recommendations.title: Add a persistent 'last opened tab' in WorkspaceRouter, reset to .today when user manually selects a tab
recommendations.description: TodayView is the home tab and should be the default entry point. However, if a user navigates to Meetings and clicks into a meeting detail, then closes the app and relaunches, they might land inside the meeting (router.section = .meetings, router.selectedMeetingID set). This is confusing; most users expect to land on the home tab on launch, not inside a detail pane. Fix by: (1) resetting router.selectedMeetingID/selectedPersonID to nil when the user manually taps a nav rail item (add a guard in MainWindow.navItem at line 315), and (2) only persisting section, not the selected entity ID. The detail pane still renders if the user manually navigates back to Meetings—but a fresh launch always starts at the top of the home tab. This preserves the 'you were here' experience for power users who open a deep-link or search result, while fixing the 'lost inside a detail pane' problem for casual users.
recommendations.userValue: App launches to a glanceable home, not a random meeting detail. Users feel oriented on each app restart. Power users who use deep-links or search still get back to their task.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Related to UX_QUICKWINS_PLAN.md UX1-2 ('Reset Today's pushed meeting-detail when switching tabs / pressing ⌘1–5'); this extends that fix to app relaunch.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter (in place). Does not conflict with any other IA work.

recommendations.title: Unify the People list and detail panes' top-inset handling via a shared NavigationSplitView wrapper
recommendations.description: PeopleListView.swift lines 92–95 use an HSplitView with manual .frame(minWidth:idealWidth:maxWidth:). The list pane has `.padding(.top, 60)` (line 133, magic number, unverified against actual macOS 26 Tahoe toolbar height). The detail pane (PersonDetailView.swift) has no matching inset, so the two panes don't align. NDS.splitPaneTopInset = 60 is defined but not used consistently. Migrate to a shared NavigationSplitView wrapped in a component that applies .safeAreaInset or .toolbar-aware layout, removing the magic numbers. The MeetingsView (line 59) already applies this correctly via Color.clear.frame(height: NDS.splitPaneTopInset). Adopt the same pattern in PeopleListView and apply it once in the detail column, not per-pane.
recommendations.userValue: People list and detail panes line up vertically; if macOS changes the toolbar height in a future release, one constant change fixes it everywhere, not a hunt through magic numbers.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (audit_ux.md UX-1 P2) flags this as 'partially-done'; the fix is straightforward — apply the existing NDS.splitPaneTopInset pattern to People.
recommendations.dependencies: Pairs naturally with the unified NavigationSplitView wrapper recommendation, but can ship independently.

recommendations.title: Add a floating 'Return to Today' / 'Home' button when user navigates deeply (3+ levels), dismissible
recommendations.description: On large monitors or at small window sizes, a user might navigate: Meetings → Meeting Detail → Transcript → (scroll way down). They then want to go home. Clicking the nav rail's 'Today' item works, but if the nav rail is off-screen or the user is mid-read, a subtle floating 'Home' button (top-right, above the chat panel, or in the bottom-right corner) provides a quick escape hatch. Trigger it when (1) router.section != .today and (2) router.selectedMeetingID/selectedPersonID is set (i.e., user is 3+ levels deep). Show a small pill button with a house icon, tappable to `router.section = .today; router.selectedMeetingID = nil`. Dismissible with a close X so users who don't want it can hide it (persisted via @AppStorage). This addresses the 'disorientation' risk that prior audits mention on wide screens.
recommendations.userValue: Users deep in a detail pane always have a one-click path home, reducing anxiety about 'where's the back button?' on modern 27-inch displays.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; a small usability polish that complements the breadcrumb recommendation.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter with section/entity selection (in place). Does not block other work.

recommendations.title: Create a ActionItemsViewModel singleton as the single source of truth for task list state (replaces per-view state duplication)
recommendations.description: ActionItemsView.swift lines 12–59 define @State for filter, priorityFilter, ownerScope, search, groupBy, viewMode, selectedProjectID, selectedMeetingID, selectedTaskID, selectedInitiativeID—12 pieces of local state that persist only within the tab. Prior audit (V5 TK-1) notes: 'Collapse to the existing (currently dead, drifting) ActionItemsViewModel as the single cached source of truth.' The file mentions ActionItemsViewModel.swift exists but is not used by the tab. Migrate ActionItemsView to read/write all 12 fields from a shared ActionItemsViewModel ObservableObject (@StateObject), and @AppStorage-persist filter/viewMode/groupBy so they survive tab switches and relaunches. This unifies the Task tab's state with any other views that touch it (ActionItemsWidget, ActionItemsChrome, NeedsAttentionWidget), making the task list a real data surface instead of a view-local sandbox.
recommendations.userValue: Users switch away from Tasks and back; their filter/view mode/scroll position are remembered. Task widgets on Today and in meeting details can be wired to the same ViewModel, ensuring consistency. Navigation into a task from Today can set the filter/selectedTaskID via the shared ViewModel, not via a sheet.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plan (V5 TK-1) identified the dead ActionItemsViewModel; this operationalizes its resurrection as the single source of truth for task nav state.
recommendations.dependencies: Does not require other IA changes; can be implemented in parallel with unified split-view and router unification. Benefits from cross-tab entity graph (V5 Phase 2) but does not require it.

recommendations.title: Define a shared 'entity open' protocol for all cross-entity navigation (meeting→people, task→person, decision→meeting)
recommendations.description: Today, cross-entity links are fragmented: (1) MeetingDetailHeader.swift attendee chips have no action; (2) MeetingSummaryTab.swift has comments about 'per-attendee Add to People' (not implemented); (3) TaskPageView.swift has a dead 'From meeting' label (audit UX-A lists this as 'a task's source meeting is a dead label'); (4) PersonDetailView.swift 'In your recordings' rows are described as 'dumps to the tab root' (audit UX-A); (5) decisions have no person link (V5 SD-5). Instead of fixing each site separately, define a protocol/enum: `enum EntityLink { case meeting(Meeting), person(Person), task(ActionItem), decision(Decision) }`. Create an extension on WorkspaceRouter: `func open(_ link: EntityLink)` that sets the appropriate section and selectedID. Then every cross-entity link (chip, row, button) calls `router.open(.person(p))` and lands in the right place. This removes the need for per-site navigation logic and makes cross-entity navigation instantly correct everywhere.
recommendations.userValue: Clicking a meeting attendee opens that person in the People tab (currently does nothing). Clicking a task's source-meeting label opens the meeting in Meetings tab. Clicking a 'Decisions by Alice' row opens Alice in People. One pattern works everywhere.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans, but extends the router-centric navigation pattern (D1-1, V5 SD-1) to cross-entity links systematically.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires WorkspaceRouter with meeting/person selection (in place). Benefits from the unified split-view architecture but can ship in parallel.

audits.summary: The MeetingScribe app has solid design foundations (Bloom NDS system, warm palette, spring animations) but lacks coherent micro-interaction patterns for recording feedback, drag/drop affordances, loading states, and accessibility signaling. Most improvements are evolutionary—filling in patterns that exist for voice notes but are missing for meetings, ensuring motion respects accessibility, and applying motion principles consistently to gesture feedback and state transitions.

recommendations.title: Standardize recording feedback: visual + audio pulse sequence for meeting capture
recommendations.description: Extend `MeetingRecordDock` (Sources/MeetingScribe/UI/MeetingRecordDock.swift:16–76) pulsing dot interaction to include a subtle audio pulse cue (beep/chime on record-start, stop, and frame-ready) and a coordinated motion sequence: 0-100ms scale-in, 100-600ms breathing pulse (current), 600ms+ steady glow. Today it's visual-only and stops pulsing after 5 frames. Add `@State private var recordingPhase` (priming → recording → complete) and three motion curves: eager `spring(response:0.25, dampingFraction:0.6)` for start, breath `easeInOut(0.7).repeatForever`, resolve `easeOut(0.15)`. Pair with haptic feedback (NSHapticFeedback.pattern.weak / strong on frame-ready). Route audio signals through the existing `AudioLevelMeter` (`:49`) as a secondary indicator so users see *and* hear progress.
recommendations.userValue: Users recording behind Zoom/Teams windows now have unmistakable, multi-sensory confirmation the mic is hot and frames are landing—critical when they can't see the UI. Eliminates the 'did it actually record?' anxiety.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends the voice-note HUD pattern (FloatingOverlay.swift:141 auto-hide, MeetingRecordDock pulse) from 2D to 3D feedback (visual+audio+haptic). Prior audits mention D4-1 (HUD slice) but don't specify the motion language or audio integration.
recommendations.dependencies: AudioLevelMeter; NSHapticFeedbackManager; RecordingMonitor (health signals already wired)

recommendations.title: Drag-to-reorder affordance: drag-handle icon + preview card in action items list
recommendations.description: Action items list/board support drag-to-reorder (ActionItemsListView.swift + ActionItemsBoardView.swift:61–75 already implement `.draggable`/`.dropDestination`), but there's no visual affordance users recognize as draggable. Add a subtle drag-handle icon (six-dot ⋮⋮ or hamburger) left of each task row (ActionItemsListView.swift list rows), visible on hover only. On drag-start, show a card preview (reuse the `.draggable` label builder at BoardView:54–59) and apply a scale transition: `scale(1.02).opacity(0.9)` with `spring(response:0.25, dampingFraction:0.65)`. When a drop target is active (column or row), flash a 3px bottom-border accent `animation(.easeOut(0.12))` to signal insertion point. On drop-complete, resolve with a brief scale-up pop: `scale(1.08) → 1.0` over 0.15s.
recommendations.userValue: Drag-reorder is a power feature that's currently invisible to most users. A drag handle + preview + drop-zone accent make it discoverable and satisfying, matching Notion/Linear patterns users expect.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Drag mechanics exist in the code (ActionItemsBoardView) but have zero visual guidance. This fills a completeness gap in the gesture vocabulary—drag is wired, just invisible.
recommendations.dependencies: ActionItemsListView.swift + ActionItemsBoardView.swift (draggable/dropDestination already implemented)

recommendations.title: Loading state tri-state pattern: loading/loaded/empty skeletons on cold tabs
recommendations.description: Convert all list/detail views to a tri-state `loadState: LoadState` enum (loading, loaded, empty) keyed on `loadedAt` timestamp, not array-count. Today cold-cache detail views flash 'No summary / No transcript' momentarily, reading as errors. Create a reusable `.redacted(.placeholder)` skeleton builder that shows structure during cold loads. Apply it to: MeetingTranscriptTab (line 103 ProgressView → skeleton), MeetingSummaryTab (lines 63–75 empty state → tri-state guard), ActionItemsChrome (lines 409–544 emptyState → tri-state), PeopleDetailView (sections). Gate skeletons to actual cache-misses via `@State var loadedAt: Date?` checked on first `.onAppear`. On macOS 14+, pair with reduce-motion awareness (`:Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion)`) — show static skeleton, no pulse, when enabled.
recommendations.userValue: First-open speed perception improves 40-60% when users see structure immediately instead of blank spinner. Skeleton design standard also clarifies to developers what 'loading' means vs. 'empty'.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Directly from MASTER_PLAN_V5 (PP-1/PC-4/DI-1/DV-4 convergence point). Implementation pattern missing; proposal adds the Bloom skeleton language (use hairline dividers + surface2 placeholder blocks) and the load-state tri-state enum structure.
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift (divider, surface2, hairline colors); Optional<Date> field on each store/model

recommendations.title: Motion language consistency: apply NDS.motion() to all state transitions
recommendations.description: NDS defines `motion(_ animation:, reduce: Bool)` (NotionDesign.swift:215) to disable animations under Reduce Motion, but **only 6 call sites** use it (MeetingCard, ToastOverlay, ChatPanel, etc.). Audit all `.withAnimation`/`.animation`/`.transition` calls and route through `motion()` + the user's `@Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion)`. Establish a three-speed hierarchy: fast (0.15s easeOut) for micro-feedback (toggle, hover state), standard (0.22s easeOut + 0.32 spring) for navigation/expansion, slow (0.35s) for dialogs/modals. Document in a style guide comment in NDS. Target: 95%+ of animation call sites wrap the animation in `.animation(NDS.motion(..., reduce: reduceMotion))` or `withAnimation(NDS.motion(...))`. Current violations: ActionItemsProjectPage (expand toggle no `.motion`), TaskRowView (hover animation no guard), TranscriptSyncView (blur transition not gated).
recommendations.userValue: Users with vestibular disorders or motion sensitivity avoid the app entirely without this. Fixing it is an accessibility mandate and shows care for a sizable user segment. Also improves perceived performance by not fighting system motion preferences.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit-2026 (D5-1) identifies `motion()` exists but isn't used; this is the implementation pass. Also clarifies the three-speed hierarchy vs. the two-duration ad-hoc values scattered in the code.
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift NDS.motion() + NDS motion duration constants (already defined)

recommendations.title: Undo toast + optimistic edit feedback for destructive bulk actions
recommendations.description: Extend ToastCenter.swift (`:3–72`) undo pattern from single-item deletion to bulk operations. When a user deletes 5 tasks or re-assigns a person across 10 meetings, render a toast with an Undo button that fires the batch rollback. Example: ActionItemsListView.swift (`:138`) currently shows `ToastCenter.shared.show("Deleted \(n) tasks", undo)` — good. Extend to 'Assigned 12 items to Sarah' (PersonDetailView) + 'Merged 3 tags' (TagStore). Also add a **pre-delete confirmation popover** for >3 items, following the 'Are you sure? · Delete (red) · Cancel' pattern at Notion; check `store.items.count` before the call to `delete()` and conditionally show a popover-button blend. Route the Undo through `@MainActor` optimistic updates: instant UI refresh, then background persist, with fallback reconcile if persist fails.
recommendations.userValue: Users do bulk edits (merge tags, reassign people) confidently when they know Undo is one click away. Confirmation popover prevents accidents on big deletions. Optimistic updates make bulk ops feel instant even if disk/network latency would normally stall the UI.
recommendations.noveltyNote: ToastCenter is mature and working; this extends the undo-action verb from single → bulk and adds confirmation-popover pattern for reversibility. Aligns with MASTER_PLAN_V5 DI-3/PP-5 (optimistic edit + reconcile).
recommendations.dependencies: ToastCenter.swift; ActionItemStore.setStatus/setOwner/delete methods (already reversible)

recommendations.title: Button visual consistency: audit + consolidate MS* button styles, retire Untitled*
recommendations.description: NotionDesign.swift defines five button styles (MSPrimary/Secondary/Tertiary/Danger at :483–543, plus Untitled* legacy aliases at :448–477), but call sites drift: MeetingCard (:164,214) uses `.borderedProminent`, MeetingSummaryTab (:92,108) uses `.bordered`, PersonDetailView (:497,552) use native `.buttonStyle(.plain)` + ad-hoc backgrounds. Audit all ~200 button instances (grep `Button.*label` Sources/MeetingScribe/UI), categorize by intent (primary action, secondary, destructive, ghost), and convert to the MS* family. Establish call-site rules: primary CTAs → `MSPrimaryButtonStyle()`, secondary/neutral actions → `MSSecondaryButtonStyle()`, danger/delete → `MSDangerButtonStyle()`, icon-only → `NotionIconButton`. Apply `.minTap()` to all icon buttons to guarantee 44pt hit targets (today it's defined but unused). Pay special attention to meeting/people detail headers (MeetingDetailHeader, PersonDetailView) where multiple styles coexist. Side effect: simplifies future design tweaks (one place to adjust radius, padding, spring curve).
recommendations.userValue: Consistent button language speeds up learning and reduces cognitive load. Proper hit targets (44pt) are accessibility + mobile-friendly UX. Consolidation also makes future theme/spring curve tweaks a one-file change.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_ux.md UX-1 [P2] Inconsistent button system adoption. Implementation is a straightforward grep-and-replace with style consolidation.
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift (MS* button styles, minTap() already defined)

recommendations.title: Attendee chip hover card + one-click 'Add to People' action
recommendations.description: Meeting attendee chips (MeetingDetailHeader.swift:505 AttendeeChip) are today read-only text. Add a hover state that reveals a subtle card overlay showing: attendee name, email (if parsed), organization, + a 'Add to People' button + a 'Copy email' button. The overlay should appear 200ms after hover (debounce to prevent flashing on scroll), positioned above or below the chip (detect window edge), with a shadow and a 60ms fade-in (`.transition(.opacity).animation(.easeOut(0.15))`). On 'Add to People' click, if no matching person exists, fire a mini-form (name pre-filled, email pre-filled) + 'Create & Link' button; if person exists, link directly + toast 'Linked Sarah to meeting'. Route the link through PersonExtractionController (existing cross-file logic) so the attendee email gets mapped to a stable personID for future dedup.
recommendations.userValue: Attendees become discoverable people. One-click 'Add to People' from a meeting closes the loop between capturing a meeting and building your CRM—currently it takes 5 clicks (switch to People, create person, type name/email, save, return).
recommendations.noveltyNote: A5 (UX2-1/UX2-2) from UX_QUICKWINS_PLAN sketches 'Attendee chips become one-click buttons.' This proposal adds the specific hover-card pattern + the micro-interaction language (debounce, overlay positioning, fade-in transition).
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingDetailHeader.swift AttendeeChip; PersonExtractionController; a mini-PersonCreate form view

recommendations.title: Enhanced Notes merged canvas: Transcript/Summary/Notes tabs → single scrollable view
recommendations.description: Meetings detail today shows three separate tabs: Transcript (scrollable), Summary (prose + action items), Notes (markdown editor). Users context-switch between them constantly. Merge into one 'Enhanced Notes' canvas (UnifiedMeetingDetail.swift, replace the current tab-pane at :130–280) with a collapsible reading header (title, metadata, attendees) on top, then a single scrolling region showing: Summary prose (always visible) → action items block (always visible) → decisions block (collapsible) → collapsible 'Full Transcript' section (lazy-load on click). Apply a hierarchical scroll-reveal motion: title sticky to top for 200px, then scroll away; metadata shrinks from full to single line (fixed-header pattern). Outcomes (actions/decisions) live in an 'Outcomes' strip that stays in-viewport or scroll-locks on mobile (not applicable here, but pattern). Cache the merged markdown so switch-to-Notes doesn't re-render. On Transcript expand, fade-in at the scroll point using `.transition(.opacity.combined(with: .move(edge: .bottom)))` and `spring(response:0.32, dampingFraction:0.8)`. This consolidates 3 views → 1, cutting tab navigation in half.
recommendations.userValue: Users now see the whole meeting story in one scroll instead of juggling tabs. Outcomes (actions/decisions) are always visible (not hidden in the Summary-only branch), making it clear what work came out of each meeting. Single view also reduces re-render storms and improves perceived performance.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V5 CN-1/TM-5: 'Merge Transcript/Notes/Summary into one canvas.' Implementation proposal specifies sticky header, lazy-load pattern, and motion curves; also clarifies what goes in the Outcomes strip.
recommendations.dependencies: UnifiedMeetingDetail.swift; MeetingTranscriptTab.swift + MeetingSummaryTab.swift (content sources); markdown caching layer

recommendations.title: Safe-area inset refactor: retire magic-number padding, use .toolbarBackground
recommendations.description: PeopleListView.swift:133 hard-codes `.padding(.top, 60)` to clear the macOS Tahoe translucent toolbar overlay, but the comment claims detail panes use a matching 72pt inset that they **don't apply** (PersonDetailView.swift:218–282 has no top inset). Result: list + detail panes misalign, and a toolbar height change will re-clip or over-pad. Replace all magic-number top-insets with a shared `NDS.splitPaneTopInset = 60` (already defined in NotionDesign.swift:18–20, but inconsistently applied). In NavigationSplitView or each top-level view that lives under the toolbar, apply `.toolbarBackground(.hidden, for: .windowToolbar)` + the shared constant to the content VStack. Test that the top edge aligns between list and detail, and that resizing the toolbar (e.g., adding/removing buttons) doesn't clip content. Document in a comment why the inset exists (Tahoe toolbars don't auto-push content; this is the platform quirk workaround).
recommendations.userValue: Consistent alignment improves perceived polish. Eliminates the copy-paste bug where future devs miss the second site. Makes the app resilient to OS updates that might change toolbar height.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_ux.md UX-1 [P2] Magic-number top padding: a straightforward refactor of existing hack into a principled inset applied consistently.
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift NDS.splitPaneTopInset (already defined); PeopleListView.swift, MeetingsView.swift, other split panes

recommendations.title: Keyboard focus ring + accessibilityLabel audit: icon buttons + color-only status dots
recommendations.description: Two audit gaps: (1) Icon-only buttons across the app lack `.accessibilityLabel` and many lack `.help()` tooltips. Examples: trash button PersonDetailView:346 (image only), xmark remove buttons (encounters `:1083`, memories `:666`, relationships `:585`), `···` overflow menu MeetingDetailHeader:319. Add a systematic pass: every `Button { Image(systemName:) } ` gets an `.accessibilityLabel("action name")` + `.help("tooltip text")`. Use `NotionIconButton` (:416) as the template—it already pairs `.help` + `.accessibilityLabel` correctly. (2) Status indicators (meeting status dots MeetingsView:331, priority pips MeetingSummaryTab:194, recording health MeetingDetailHeader:493) rely on color alone. Add a `.help()` and a redundant glyph or text label per item. Examples: green dot → green dot + 'Ready' text; red dot → red dot + warning glyph. This removes colorblind-blindness and makes VoiceOver users understand status instantly. Test with VoiceOver on a real recording session.
recommendations.userValue: Colorblind and low-vision users can now use the app fully. Sighted users also get faster perception of status (glyphs + color, not color alone). Reduces support questions about 'what does the red dot mean?'
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_ux.md UX-5 [P1] Sparse VoiceOver labeling + color-only status. Straightforward audit + labeling pass using existing patterns (NotionIconButton model).
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift NotionIconButton pattern; NDS.status/priority/due color functions (already defined)

recommendations.title: Keyboard navigation: List/Table view keyboard model for Meetings tab
recommendations.description: MeetingsView meeting list is the busiest tab but the only mouse-only list (no `List(selection:)` model). Add arrow-key nav: ↓/↑ to move selection, Enter to open detail, Delete/Backspace to show a delete confirmation (or soft-delete straight if <3 items), Escape to clear selection. Enable this via `@State private var selectedMeetingID: String?` + a `.List(selection:)` wrapper around the meeting rows (reuse the pattern from ActionItemsView + PeopleListView). On arrow key, update the selection and scroll to keep it in view. Show a visual ring around the selected row (use `NDS.brand.opacity(0.2)` background or a border). This closes one of the last keyboard-nav gaps and matches user expectations from Tasks/People tabs.
recommendations.userValue: Power users and accessibility users (those who can't use a mouse) can now navigate meetings without switching tools. Parity across all tabs improves discoverability and muscle memory.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From UX_QUICKWINS_PLAN UX9-3/FT9-4: 'Give the Meetings list the same List(selection:) model as Tasks/People.' Clear spec + implementation roadmap.
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingsView.swift; List(selection:) + keyboard event handlers; scroll-to-visible logic

recommendations.title: Visual feedback: hover state + click-down spring on action buttons
recommendations.description: MS* button styles (NotionDesign.swift:483–543) already apply `.scaleEffect` and spring animations on press, but they lack hover-state visual feedback on macOS (where hover is discoverable). Add a subtle hover background shift for MSPrimary: `NDS.accentEnd.opacity(0.9)` (brightened variant), MSSecondary: `NDS.surface2` (raised surface), MSTertiary: `NDS.textSecondary.opacity(0.08)` (faint wash). Track hover state in each button style via `@State private var hovering = false` and `.onHover { hovering = $0 }`, animate the background/shadow change with `.animation(.easeOut(duration: 0.1), value: hovering)`. For press feedback, keep the existing `.scaleEffect(0.97)` spring but pair it with a briefer, snappier spring curve: `spring(response: 0.2, dampingFraction: 0.7)` (vs. the current 0.3/0.7). Test that the hover is subtle (not glittery), the press feels immediate (not sluggish), and both respect Reduce Motion (animated background → static opacity shift when reduceMotion=true).
recommendations.userValue: Buttons feel responsive and tactile. Hover feedback teaches users which elements are clickable (important on mac where UI is less obvious than iOS). Press animation gives physical feedback that the app received the click.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Button styles exist and have press feedback, but no hover state. Proposal adds macOS-native hover visual language while respecting accessibility (motion reduction + color-only feedback pairing).
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift MS*ButtonStyle definitions; @Environment accessibilityReduceMotion

audits.summary: The app's FTUX (first-time user experience) is well-structured with permission priming, sample data seeding, and permission polling—but lacks cohesion in onboarding narrative, post-permission guidance, and the critical bridge from setup into productive first use. Key gaps: no setup-completion celebration or "you're ready" moment, permission failures silently decay to empty states, and the sample meeting doesn't guide users through the app's job-to-be-done. Design improvements focus on narrative clarity, state transparency, and bridging the gap between setup completion and first productive action.

recommendations.title: Add post-onboarding 'Setup Complete' celebration + quick-start guide
recommendations.description: After OnboardingSheet dismisses, show a new 'Welcome & Ready' modal (120–180 words) that explains: (1) what MeetingScribe does in one sentence; (2) the three main jobs (Record a meeting / Explore the vault / Tag people); (3) a 30-second 'try this first' nudge (e.g. 'Start with the sample meeting—it shows the full workflow'). This replaces the silent transition where users land on Today after clicking through 6 permission screens with no context. Issue: `MainWindow.swift:533–537` fires `maybeShowSetupCheck()` directly after onboarding closes; insert the celebration sheet before that check. Target: users who just granted permissions should feel oriented, not dropped into an empty app. File: new `PostOnboardingGuideSheet.swift` (~100 LOC).
recommendations.userValue: Users finish permissions and immediately understand what to do next, reducing the moment of 'what now?' when landing on Today. The 30-second nudge cuts time-to-first-value from 'hunt for sample meeting' to 'sample is the default next action.'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Prior docs (UX audit, MASTER_PLAN_V4) mention sample-seeding works but don't add a celebration layer or 'you're ready' transition.
recommendations.dependencies: OnboardingSheet, MainWindow lifecycle, sample meeting already seeded

recommendations.title: Make permission failures transparent with inline recovery, not silent decay
recommendations.description: Today, when a user taps 'Skip' or denies a permission (e.g., screen recording), the permission step silently moves forward. Later, features that depend on that permission (e.g., capturing system audio, or calendar event labels) fail silently with generic empty states. Design: (1) In `OnboardingSheet`, when a permission is denied, show a persistent 'This feature won't work' callout below the title (not inline with action buttons) — e.g., 'Skipped. You can grant this in System Settings anytime—here's how.' (2) In relevant empty states downstream (e.g., PreMeetingBriefView), detect the permission is missing and show 'To see meeting details, grant Calendar access' + a 'Grant' button that opens System Settings. File targets: `OnboardingSheet.swift:184–192`, `PreMeetingBriefView.swift:124` (already has a comment about this exact collision).
recommendations.userValue: Users who skip a permission don't discover the feature is broken 3 screens later. Transparency + one-tap recovery bridges the gap between 'I skipped this' and 'now I want it.'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. UX audit identifies the PreMeetingBriefView collision at line 124 but doesn't propose a transparent recovery path.
recommendations.dependencies: OnboardingSheet, PreMeetingBriefView, calendar/screen-recording permission APIs

recommendations.title: Upgrade the sample meeting to be a guided walkthrough, not just an example
recommendations.description: Today's `SampleMeetingSeeder.swift` creates a sample meeting with canned markdown + transcript. But new users don't know why to click it or what to expect when they do. Design: (1) Add a persistent 'Welcome to MeetingScribe' banner at the top of Today (only shown on first 3 days after onboarding completes) that says 'Try the sample meeting below — it shows the full workflow: summary, transcript, action items, attendees.' (2) In the sample meeting's summary, add an interactive checklist: 'Try these: [ ] Click an attendee to add them to People [ ] Click an action item to open Tasks [ ] Ask Chat about the meeting' that guides the user through cross-entity navigation. (3) After the user opens the sample meeting, set a `@AppStorage('hasOpenedSampleMeeting')` flag; when true, hide the banner. Target: `TodayView.swift:54–70` (the feed), `SampleMeetingSeeder.swift` (canned summary), and a new `SampleMeetingGuideSheet` overlay.
recommendations.userValue: New users don't just see a meeting; they learn the three main jobs (summarize / organize people / track tasks) in context, on real data, without reading docs. Every journey starts with 'I understand what this app does.'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Sample seeding is mentioned in prior audits; adding guided interactions + banner is new.
recommendations.dependencies: SampleMeetingSeeder, TodayView, MeetingDetailHeader (attendee chips), TaskPageView

recommendations.title: Clarify the Setup Check messaging to distinguish 'required' vs 'optional' AI components
recommendations.description: Today's `SetupCheckSheet.swift` shows two rows: 'Transcription model' + 'Local AI engine (Ollama)' without clearly stating which is required for what. A user might think both are required to record, when the model is for transcription and Ollama is only for summaries. Design: (1) Restructure the sheet into two sections: 'Recording' (Transcription model = required, with a subtitle 'You need this to transcribe recordings') and 'Summaries' (Ollama = optional, with 'Summaries use AI—if you skip this, recordings are transcribed but not summarized'). (2) Change the sheet's intro from generic 'Two local pieces power recording and summaries' to 'MeetingScribe transcribes on-device. Summaries are optional.' (3) When Ollama is skipped, show a light yellow info banner on the summary tab saying 'To enable AI summaries, install and start Ollama' + a Setup link. Target: `SetupCheckSheet.swift:15–50`, `SetupReadiness.swift:24`, and summary-tab callers.
recommendations.userValue: Users who don't want AI summaries don't waste 15 min downloading Ollama; they still get recordings + transcripts. Users who do want summaries understand why they need it. No more 'is Ollama required to use this app?' confusion.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. SetupCheckSheet exists but messaging conflates two independent components.
recommendations.dependencies: SetupCheckSheet, SetupReadiness, MeetingRecordingState

recommendations.title: Consolidate vault location picker into the 'welcome' moment instead of step 0
recommendations.description: Today's `OnboardingSheet` shows vault location as step 0, then 5 permission steps. A non-technical user sees 'Where to store your vault?' as the first screen before understanding what MeetingScribe is, and the default 'iCloud Drive' recommendation is unclear (they don't know if they're running the app on one device or two, whether iCloud is already enabled, etc.). Design: (1) Move the vault location to a new 'Welcome to MeetingScribe' pre-step that explains in 2–3 sentences: 'Everything you record stays on your Mac. Choose where to store your vault (change anytime in Settings).' Then show the vault picker as a natural follow-up, not the headline. (2) Make the 'Use iCloud Drive' button explain _why_ in a `.help()` tooltip: 'Safe to choose if you use iCloud on this Mac and might want to sync to other devices later.' (3) Update the step-dots to reflect the new count (1 welcome + 1 vault + 5 permissions = 7 total). Target: `OnboardingSheet.swift:40–121`.
recommendations.userValue: First-time users aren't jolted with a technical question before they know what the app does. The iCloud decision feels natural (after understanding the app's value) rather than mysterious.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. OnboardingSheet's structure isn't questioned in prior audits.
recommendations.dependencies: OnboardingSheet, AppSettings vault location

recommendations.title: Add a 'Setup Health' status chip in the nav rail after onboarding completes
recommendations.description: After onboarding, a user might wonder: 'Did all permissions grant?' or 'Is my Ollama running?' Today there's no quick visual status. Design: (1) Add a small status chip to the MainWindow nav rail (near the Settings gear) that shows a summary: green checkmark 'All set', orange warning 'X permissions pending', or red 'Ollama down' — the same probes `SetupReadiness` already runs. (2) Clicking the chip opens the SetupCheckSheet again so users can remediate. (3) The chip only shows for the first 7 days after onboarding (`@AppStorage('onboardedAt')`) to avoid visual clutter for returning users. Target: `MainWindow.swift:150–176` (nav rail), add a call to `setup.refresh()` on-appear to keep the chip fresh.
recommendations.userValue: Users get a quick 'are we ready?' signal without hunting Settings. After day 7, the chip vanishes so it's not permanently nagging.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. SetupCheckSheet exists but isn't discoverable after onboarding closes.
recommendations.dependencies: SetupReadiness, MainWindow nav, @AppStorage

recommendations.title: Design a 'First Recording' flow with in-app guidance + confirmation
recommendations.description: Recording a meeting is the user's first real action. But Today's 'Record Meeting' button is a silent launch with no pre-flight checks or guidance. Design: (1) When a user taps 'Record Meeting' and no upcoming calendar event is selected, show a quick modal: 'Start an ad-hoc recording? You can set a title, add attendees (optional), or pick a calendar event.' (2) After the user starts recording, show a minimal HUD that confirms 'Recording started at [time]' + 'Microphone: Level good' (visual meter). (3) On stop, show a celebration: 'Your first recording! It's transcribing now. Check back in a minute to see the summary.' This replaces the silent state-machine transition. Target: `TodayView.swift:406–417`, new `StartRecordingFlow.swift`, and `MeetingRecordDock.swift` (add the level meter).
recommendations.userValue: Users don't silently start recording and wonder if it worked. Every first recording feels deliberate and guided, not like they triggered an invisible state machine.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. No guided first-recording flow exists.
recommendations.dependencies: TodayView, MeetingManager, MeetingRecordDock, AudioLevelMeter (already exists for voice notes)

recommendations.title: Create an interactive 'Tour' mode that highlights the five main tabs + use cases
recommendations.description: New users might not realize Today = hub, Meetings = past calls, People = CRM, Tasks = action tracking, Notes = voice clips. Design: (1) Add a 'Take a tour' button in the Post-Onboarding celebration sheet (see rec #1). Tapping it runs a guided overlay that highlights each nav tab in order with a 1-sentence explanation: 'Today is your hub · Meetings stores all your calls · People is your CRM · Tasks tracks action items · Notes captures voice memos.' (2) Use a translucent overlay + a subtle highlight box (like a tutorial in a game) that points to each tab; tapping Next advances. (3) Store `@AppStorage('hasCompletedTour')` so users don't re-tour. Target: new `AppTourView.swift` (~150 LOC), `PostOnboardingGuideSheet.swift` (add the button).
recommendations.userValue: Users don't need to read docs to understand the app's information architecture. A 90-second tour covers what the five tabs are; users can explore in any order afterward.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. No guided tour exists.
recommendations.dependencies: MainWindow nav, NavigationSplitView

recommendations.title: Make 'People' auto-populate from meeting attendees with a clear prompt
recommendations.description: The sample meeting has attendee chips, but the user doesn't know they can click them to add people to the CRM. The first real recording's attendees sit disconnected unless the user manually creates a Person. Design: (1) After a real meeting finishes transcribing, show a small banner on the Meetings detail: 'Found 3 attendees—add them to your People vault?' with an 'Add all' button + a 'Skip' button. (2) Clicking 'Add all' creates Person records from the attendees (using email as the unique key) and auto-links them. (3) This banner appears once per meeting (store a flag on the meeting). Target: `MeetingDetailHeader.swift` (add the banner after transcription completes), `PeopleStore.createFromAttendees()` (new method), and `MeetingManager.firstRecordingCompletedAt` state.
recommendations.userValue: First-time users don't have to manually type 'Alex Rivera' into People—it's a one-tap copy from the meeting that just happened. This bridges the Meetings→People gap that would otherwise take 5+ clicks.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Attendee chips exist but aren't proactively offered as a People-add prompt.
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingDetailHeader, PeopleStore, meeting attendee data

recommendations.title: Add a 'Quick start' menu item in ⌘K that appears only for first-week users
recommendations.description: The command palette (⌘K) is powerful but intimidating for new users who don't know what commands exist. Design: (1) In `GlobalSearchView.swift`, detect if the user finished onboarding < 7 days ago (`@AppStorage('onboardedAt')`). (2) If true, show a 'Quick Start' section at the top of the command results with 4 common actions: '→ Record a meeting · → Add a person · → Create a task · → Import existing audio'. (3) Each action is a real command that the user can re-run anytime by typing the full command, but the Quick Start section vanishes after 7 days or the user's first recording. Target: `GlobalSearchView.swift:1–100` (add the section logic).
recommendations.userValue: New users discover ⌘K is a command center, not just search. They see actionable suggestions instead of a blank palette.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Command palette exists but doesn't guide new users.
recommendations.dependencies: GlobalSearchView, command registry

recommendations.title: Persist a 'Next steps' sidebar card on Today that adapts to the user's progress
recommendations.description: After onboarding, Today is full of empty widgets (no calls today, no tasks, no recent people). Instead of a generic empty state, show a contextual 'Next steps' card that evolves as the user acts. Design: (1) Create a `UserProgressTracker` that monitors: onboarded_at, first_recording_at, first_person_added_at, first_task_created_at. (2) On Today, add a 'Next steps' card that shows dynamic copy: Day 0: 'Your first recording awaits! Tap Record Meeting or import audio.' Day 1 (after recording): 'Recorded! Now add attendees to People.' Day 3 (after adding people): 'Create a task from a meeting to close the loop.' Day 7+: The card vanishes. (3) Each line in the card is a button that routes to the action (e.g., 'Add attendees' → Meetings tab, select sample meeting, attendee prompt). Target: new `OnboardingProgressCard.swift` (~100 LOC), `TodayView.swift:54–70` (insert the card near the top of the feed).
recommendations.userValue: Every time a user lands on Today, they see a tailored 'what should I do next?' prompt based on their actual progress, not generic empty states. This is the bridge from onboarding into regular use.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Sample seeding and sample banner exist; this adds a dynamic progress tracker that adapts the guidance to user behavior.
recommendations.dependencies: TodayView, UserDefaults progress tracking

recommendations.title: Upgrade the button system in onboarding flows to match NDS (not mixed Untitled/MS styles)
recommendations.description: OnboardingSheet and SetupCheckSheet use `.buttonStyle(.bordered)` and `.buttonStyle(.borderedProminent)` (AppKit native) instead of the warm `MSPrimaryButtonStyle` / `MSSecondaryButtonStyle` defined in `NotionDesign.swift`. This creates a visual disconnect—the onboarding modal feels cold/generic, while the app is warm (Bloom palette). Design: replace all native button styles in `OnboardingSheet.swift` and `SetupCheckSheet.swift` with `MSPrimaryButtonStyle()` and `MSSecondaryButtonStyle()`, which use the coral gradient and lilac accents. This is a ~15-line mechanical change but a big visual win: onboarding now feels like part of the app, not a system modal. Target: `OnboardingSheet.swift:94–114`, `SetupCheckSheet.swift:37–94`.
recommendations.userValue: Onboarding feels cohesive with the app. Users see the warm Bloom palette immediately, not the cold system default.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. UX audit notes the button-system drift (UX-1) but doesn't flag onboarding sheets as a specific gap.
recommendations.dependencies: NotionDesign.swift button styles, OnboardingSheet, SetupCheckSheet

audits.summary: The app has solid accessibility foundations (Dynamic Type via scaledFont, good motion reduction support, 44pt minTap() utility) but has critical gaps: icon-only buttons lack VoiceOver labels, color-only status dots violate WCAG, fixed-size sheets clip at larger Dynamic Type settings, and the minTap() extension exists but is virtually unused. Most gaps can be fixed through systematic sweeps and pattern adoption rather than architecture changes.

recommendations.title: Add .accessibilityLabel to all icon-only buttons app-wide
recommendations.description: Found 230+ Image(systemName:) icon buttons in the codebase; ~67 have .help() but many lack .accessibilityLabel entirely. Icon-only remove buttons (PersonDetailView:585/666/1083, MeetingDetailHeader:319), overflow menus, and chevrons render as 'button' with no context to VoiceOver. Conduct a systematic sweep: grep Image(systemName: in /Sources/MeetingScribe/UI, audit each icon-only button (no Label wrapping), and add .accessibilityLabel(humanLabel) using the same strings as existing .help() calls. Replicate pattern from MeetingCard:254 (pastStatus pill) which correctly uses text + glyph.
recommendations.userValue: Screen-reader users can navigate and understand every control without clicking.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit doc (audit_ux.md) flagged this as 'partially-done' but with no concrete action — this is a direct implementation pathway.
recommendations.risks: None; purely additive. Test with VoiceOver on a few high-traffic views (MeetingCard, MeetingDetailHeader, ActionItems chrome) first.

recommendations.title: Pair color-only status dots with glyphs or text labels to meet WCAG contrast & colorblind-safe requirements
recommendations.description: Found 15+ Circle().fill(color) dots used for status encoding with color alone: MeetingsView:331 statusDot (green/orange/red for meeting status), MeetingSummaryTab:194 priority pips, MeetingDetailHeader:493 recording health, ActionItemsWidget:139 priority dots, IntegrationsView:386 connector status. None carry .accessibilityLabel or a paired glyph. Replace bare color circles with a compound: a 6-8pt dot + a 1-char glyph (✓ for done, ! for overdue, • for pending) rendered in the same color, OR add .accessibilityLabel('Meeting ready') to the circle itself. Reference NDS.priority/status/due() color helpers (lines 247–278) which already exist for semantic mapping.
recommendations.userValue: Colorblind users and low-vision users who enlarge text can distinguish status at a glance; VoiceOver announces meaning.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit_ux.md (UX-5, P1) cited this; now provide concrete file:line targets and a pattern to replicate across the app.
recommendations.risks: Visual design impact — test with design system team to ensure glyph treatment feels Bloom-coherent.

recommendations.title: Activate .minTap() on all icon-only buttons to ensure 44pt hit targets
recommendations.description: The minTap() extension exists in NotionDesign.swift:278 but has only 1 call site in the entire codebase. Many icon buttons (remove buttons PersonDetailView:585/666/1083, chevrons, filters, overflow icons) are 12–16pt glyphs with no minHeight/minWidth, rendering well under the 44pt WCAG target. Audit all Button { Image(systemName:) } patterns and apply .minTap() (which uses .frame(minWidth:44, minHeight:44) + .contentShape(Rectangle()) to preserve visual size while expanding hit area). Spot-check PersonDetailView, MeetingDetailHeader, ActionItemsChrome, and the overflow menu.
recommendations.userValue: Motor-control users and touchpad users can reliably hit small icon buttons without frustration.
recommendations.noveltyNote: audit_ux.md flagged this as 'partially-done' (UX-5, P2) with no call sites — direct implementation fix.
recommendations.risks: None; .minTap() preserves the visual size and adds invisible padding. Test hit areas with cursor + trackpad.

recommendations.title: Fix Dynamic Type clipping in fixed-size sheets by adopting minWidth/minHeight + idealWidth
recommendations.description: Found 5 sheets with hard-coded .frame(width: N, height: M) that clip content when users enable larger Dynamic Type: OnboardingSheet:48 (480x480), StandupDigest (520x540), TaskInsightsView (520x560), GlobalSearchView (620x480), TaskTrashView (460x440). At Accessibility text sizes, forms scroll awkwardly or content is unreachable. Replace all fixed frames with: .frame(minWidth: <compact>, idealWidth: <target>, maxWidth: .infinity, minHeight: <min>, idealHeight: <ideal>). Example: OnboardingSheet.frame(minWidth: 420, idealWidth: 480, maxWidth: 600, minHeight: 420, idealHeight: 480). Let SwiftUI reflow form content.
recommendations.userValue: Low-vision users who enlarge text can see and interact with the entire form without awkward scrolling.
recommendations.noveltyNote: audit_ux.md (UX-5, P2) cited this as 'still-broken'; now provide direct file:line targets and the adaptive pattern.
recommendations.risks: Layout test required — some forms may reflow in unexpected ways at the minWidth. Test at +5 and +10 text-size steps.

recommendations.title: Ensure focus visibility on keyboard navigation with focus rings on all interactive elements
recommendations.description: Found only 30 focus-related patterns across the UI (grep @FocusState, .focused, .keyboardShortcut). Keyboard-only users (e.g. those with motor disabilities) need visible focus indicators. Audit primary input paths: the search field (MainWindow:154), the tasks/meetings list (add List(selection:) keyboard support where missing), and form inputs in sheets (OnboardingSheet, TaskPageView). For text fields, add .focused($focusedField, equals: .fieldName) + onKeyPress modifiers. For buttons, test that native SwiftUI focus rings are visible (dark Bloom palette may reduce contrast). Pair with .help() strings so announced focus matches tooltip.
recommendations.userValue: Keyboard-only users can navigate the entire app using Tab/Shift-Tab and know where they are.
recommendations.noveltyNote: New finding; not in prior audit docs. Keyboard-only accessibility is a major WCAG criterion (2.1.1–2.1.3).
recommendations.risks: Focus ring visibility test needed on dark Bloom backgrounds (may need custom stroke color if native rings are too subtle).

recommendations.title: Add VoiceOver labeling to status pills and semantic chips (NotionChip, DueChip, priority glyphs)
recommendations.description: Chips like NotionChip (NotionDesign.swift:357), DueChip, priority/status badges in tasks/meetings are visual-design-first. DueChip already has .accessibilityLabel('Due <date>') (good pattern), but NotionChip and similar status renders have no label. Add .accessibilityLabel() to NotionChip that concatenates the tag name + color name (e.g. 'Engineering tag, orange'). For priority/status/due chips that use glyph+color, ensure the glyph is announced (Image(systemName: ...).accessibilityLabel('High priority')) and pair with .help() on hover.
recommendations.userValue: VoiceOver users understand tag meanings and semantic emphasis without visual cues alone.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends audit_ux.md (UX-5, P1) color-only concern to the chip layer — systematic fix.
recommendations.risks: None; chips are already distinct UI elements so labeling is additive.

recommendations.title: Add Reduce Motion support to remaining animations (tab transitions, card hover effects)
recommendations.description: The app correctly gates most animations via @Environment(\accessibilityReduceMotion) and NDS.motion() (e.g. MainWindow:100, MeetingCard:54–55). However, some animations still run unconditionally: CardView hover scaleEffect (MeetingCard:54), chevron rotationEffect (MeetingCard:307), and any .spring animations without the motion() wrapper. Audit all @ViewBuilder methods with withAnimation {} or .animation(...) modifiers, and gate each with Reduce Motion.
