Pattern: replace .animation(spring) with .animation(NDS.motion(spring, reduce: reduceMotion)).
recommendations.userValue: Users with vestibular disorders or epilepsy sensitivities can use the app without motion-triggered discomfort.
recommendations.noveltyNote: audit_ux.md flagged D5-1 as 'already-good' but an audit pass confirms gaps remain; this is a polish pass.
recommendations.risks: None; gating animations is always safe (worst case: animations are disabled).

recommendations.title: Implement heading hierarchy and semantic HTML-like structure for VoiceOver navigation
recommendations.description: VoiceOver users can navigate by headings (H1/H2/H3 equivalent). Currently, page titles use .font(NDS.title) and section labels use .font(NDS.sectionLabel), but neither carry .accessibilityAddTraits(.isHeader) or .accessibilityHeading(.h1/.h2) markers. Add semantic heading traits: page titles (MeetingsView title 'Meetings', TaskPageView task name) as .h1, section headers (MSSectionHeader, NotionEyebrow) as .h2 / .h3. Pattern: Text('Title').font(NDS.title).accessibilityAddTraits(.isHeader) or use newer .accessibilityHeading(.h1).
recommendations.userValue: VoiceOver users can skim the page structure and jump to sections without listening to all content.
recommendations.noveltyNote: New finding; not in prior docs. Essential for screen-reader navigation efficiency.
recommendations.risks: None; traits are purely for screen readers and don't affect sighted users.

recommendations.title: Ensure contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for text and 3:1 for graphical elements per WCAG AA
recommendations.description: NDS already includes contrast helpers (NDS.contrastRatio, NDS.relativeLuminance; lines 286–298). Many text elements use textSecondary/textTertiary on fieldBg/bg, which may fall short of 4.5:1. Run a sweep: measure contrast on the darkest (dark mode) and lightest (light mode) combinations of foreground/background pairs in use. Spots to check: caption text in cards (NDS.textTertiary on NDS.fieldBg), secondary text on any background, and muted status indicators. If <4.5:1, use a less-muted shade. Example: NDS.textSecondary instead of textTertiary for primary body text in cards.
recommendations.userValue: Low-vision users can read all text at default zoom without eye strain.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Automated sweep using the contrast helpers already in NDS; not a design-system overhaul.
recommendations.risks: Minimal; shifting to higher-contrast text slightly darkens the Bloom palette but should stay within brand.

recommendations.title: Add descriptive alt text to SF Symbol glyphs in complex data tables (ActionItems board/table views)
recommendations.description: Tables like ActionItemsTableView and ActionItemsBoardView use priority/status/owner glyphs without text fallback. Add .accessibilityLabel() to each glyph that explains what it represents. Example: In ActionItemsTableView, a priority glyph Image(systemName: NDS.priorityGlyph(item.priority)) should have .accessibilityLabel('Priority: \(item.priority.rawValue.capitalized)'). In the board view, column-header priority glyphs need labels too. Use NDS.priority/status helpers to generate human-readable text.
recommendations.userValue: Screen-reader users navigating task lists understand priority, status, and ownership without guessing.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends icon-labeling work (recommendation 1) to data-visualization context.
recommendations.risks: None; glyphs + labels coexist without conflict.

recommendations.title: Document and test accessibility patterns in a living a11y style guide (e.g., ComponentGallery.swift expansion)
recommendations.description: The app has good foundations (NDS design system, scaledFont, motion reduction) but no single source of truth for accessibility patterns. Create a section in ComponentGallery.swift (or a new A11yGallery.swift) documenting the approved patterns: when to use scaledFont vs .font(), how to apply .accessibilityLabel, when to use .minTap(), Reduce Motion wrapping, and focus-ring testing. Include examples of both correct and incorrect usage. Link to this guide in code comments (e.g., // Follow A11y guide for icon-only buttons) so future contributors know the pattern.
recommendations.userValue: New developers and maintainers build accessible features from the start instead of learning a11y patterns retroactively.
recommendations.noveltyNote: New; proactive documentation to prevent regression as the app grows.
recommendations.risks: None; documentation-only, no code changes. Requires brief design review to finalize patterns.

recommendations.title: Create an end-to-end accessibility test flow using VoiceOver and keyboard-only navigation
recommendations.description: The app is complex (269 files, 50K LOC). Conduct a scripted VoiceOver test covering the critical user journey: open app → onboarding (grant permissions) → create/view meeting → add task → search → switch tabs. Record VoiceOver announcements at each step and verify: (1) all interactive elements are announced with labels, (2) focus moves in logical order, (3) headings are announced, (4) status/priority are conveyed textually, (5) animations don't play. Create a checklist (similar to audit-2026/findings docs) and snapshot test results. Run before each major release.
recommendations.userValue: Ensures accessibility doesn't regress as features are added.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Process improvement; complements the concrete fixes above by catching future issues.
recommendations.risks: Requires a tester with VoiceOver experience; consider pairing with accessibility consultant for the first run.

recommendations.title: Expand Dynamic Type support in complex layouts (Meetings split-view, chat, sidebar) by testing at +5 and +10 text-size steps
recommendations.description: The app uses scaledFont() consistently and supports Reduce Motion, but layout responsiveness at extreme Dynamic Type sizes is untested. MeetingsView uses a fixed split layout (300–480 list + detail); at +10 text size, text may wrap awkwardly and content can overflow. Test the app at: default (0), +2, +5, +10 text-size steps using System Preferences > Accessibility > Display > Text Size. Audit MeetingsView:64 (navigationSplitViewColumnWidth), MainWindow chat-rail collapse threshold (240 threshold at `:240`), and any fixed-height rows. Adjust column widths and row heights to reflow gracefully or add overflow handling.
recommendations.userValue: Low-vision users who enlarge text globally can still navigate complex layouts (splits, sidebars, tables) without excessive scrolling or hidden content.
recommendations.noveltyNote: audit_ux.md (P5-2) mentioned this as a prereq ('enlarged Dynamic Type can reflow') but no concrete testing was done.
recommendations.risks: Layout adjustments may affect sighted users at default text size; test both populations.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe is at a fork: it can own Granola's abandoned individual segment (local-first meeting tool, $10–15/month) or differentiate as a relationship coach (People CRM + meeting context, $20–30/year). The keystone decision—which wedge—determines the entire roadmap. Current codebase is 70% meeting tool, 30% relationship coach; the relationship path has higher unit economics but requires committing to a `RelationshipPath` enum and relationship-specific content (Gottman frameworks, check-in cadences, habit loops). Both paths are viable; picking one and doing it completely is the difference between a feature-filled mess and a defensible position.

recommendations.title: DECISION: Choose the Core Wedge—Meeting Tool or Relationship Coach
recommendations.description: The app must commit to ONE positioning before any public release. The codebase currently tries to serve both meeting-power-users (Granola competitors) and relationship coaches (Lasting/Paired competitors). Prior plans (V3–V5) hedged both. The decision tree: (A) Meeting Tool path: focus on write-capable MCP, action-item extraction, Notion/Linear sync, speaker diarization. De-emphasize People. Price $10–15/month. (B) Relationship Coach path: build relationship-type enum (RelationshipPath), per-type check-in cadences, Gottman/NVC content frameworks, check-in reminders, health scores. Treat meetings as relationship context. Price $20–30/year. Both are viable. Pick one. Embed the decision in `MeetingScribeApp.swift` top-of-file comments and in `CLAUDE.md`. Do NOT start Phase 2 work (Phases 2+ in MASTER_PLAN_V4) until this decision is made and communicated to the team.
recommendations.userValue: A focused product roadmap that doesn't dilute messaging, faster shipping velocity, and a defensible market position.
recommendations.noveltyNote: This is meta-strategic: all prior plans proposed features but hedged on the core positioning. This surfaces the decision explicitly and blocks ambiguous work.
recommendations.dependencies: None—this is the prerequisite for all other roadmap work. Must be decided before Phase 2.

recommendations.title: Build RelationshipPath Enum in VaultKit (Keystone for Relationship Coach Path)
recommendations.description: Add a `RelationshipPath` enum to `Sources/VaultKit/RelationshipPath.swift` with cases: `romanticPartner`, `spouse`, `parent`, `child`, `sibling`, `familyMember`, `closeFriend`, `friend`, `manager`, `directReport`, `colleague`, `mentor`, `client`, `vendor`, `custom`. This is raised independently by 22 of 25 prior auditors and is the architectural primitive blocking all relationship-coach features (per-type check-in cadences, Gottman prompt templates, relationship health scores, type-aware notifications, MCP coaching tools). Add computed properties: `suggestedCheckInDays: Int?` and `supportsDepthContent: Bool`. Add `relationshipType: RelationshipPath?` + `checkInCadenceDays: Int?` + `lastCheckInAt: Date?` to `Person.swift` (lines 77–184), mirror to `PersonDTO` in `SharedModels.swift`, update `SecondBrainDB` schema migration to v3 (additive `ALTER TABLE`). Add `RelationshipTypeTests` in `Tests/` covering round-trip codec, unknown-value fallback, per-type default cadence. This is Phase 1 in MASTER_PLAN_V4 and is prerequisite for Phases 2–6. Zero breaking changes; uses tolerant decoder pattern already in place.
recommendations.userValue: Enables type-aware reminders, relationship-specific content, and differentiated check-in cadences that treat a partner and a colleague differently—the core job-to-be-done of a relationship coach.
recommendations.noveltyNote: This is from MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 1 (endorsed by 22 auditors). Shipping it is the gate to everything relationship-coach-specific.
recommendations.dependencies: Explicit commitment to Relationship Coach wedge (recommendation #1).

recommendations.title: Fix Critical P0 Bugs Before Any Public Release
recommendations.description: Three silent-data-loss and distribution bugs block credible public launch: (1) E4-1: `DarwinNotifier.recordingStopped` observer in `MeetingManager.swift:136–149` never calls `finalize()`, so every daemon-recorded meeting produces no summary/action items/FTS index — silent data loss on primary recording path. Fix by capturing `droppedChunkCount` and `liveCoverageSeconds` from live transcriber before teardown, then call `pipelineController.finalize()` with those values (cf. non-daemon path at line 352). (2) C5/Sparkle: `UpdaterController.swift:21–24` — `isConfigured` is false, `SUPublicEDKey` is placeholder `REPLACE_WITH`, `SUFeedURL` in `Resources/Info.plist:45` points at old repo. Generate EdDSA key pair via Sparkle CLI, embed public key in Info.plist, fix SUFeedURL to `github.com/tyleryannes94/meetingscribe-refactor`, set `isConfigured = true`. No installed user can receive updates without this fix. (3) P5-12: Verify `birthday` field is actually absent from MCP `get_person` response (line 1094); if absent, add it to the response dict. These are independent one-line to multi-hour fixes. Do them first, verify with smoke test (record→stop→transcribe→confirm summary generated).
recommendations.userValue: Data integrity, distribution channel, and API completeness—prerequisites for any launch.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 0. These are identified critical bugs, not new features.
recommendations.dependencies: None—these are independent and must ship before anything else.

recommendations.title: One-Time Purchase + Annual Subscription via LemonSqueezy (Monetization Infra)
recommendations.description: Implement LicenseStore singleton in Keychain (cf. P4-1, C5-1 audit). License model: one-time $49 OR annual $79 (or $149 lifetime). Use LemonSqueezy for payment processing (not Paddle—the 2026 indie-dev community consensus favors LemonSqueezy for native license-key generation and Setapp Marketplace compatibility). Sparkle integration already partial in `UpdaterController.swift`; wire the EdDSA key (rec #3 above), then add license check before presenting Pro features: AI summaries, all MCP write tools, iMessage analysis presets, speaker diarization, all integrations. Free tier: 10 meetings/month, People CRM unlimited, no AI. Pro: unlimited recording, full AI, all integrations, write MCP tools. Implement inline upgrade banner (not modal) on first AI trigger—research shows 3% → 6–8% conversion lift vs. blocking UI. Add 'Plan' section to SettingsView showing current status (Free/Pro) with upgrade button + price anchor comparison (show Granola $168/yr, Fathom $240/yr, MeetingScribe $49 one-time). Annual subscribers receive quarterly content updates (Gottman check-in templates, new MCP tools in appcast, priority model upgrades) so the subscription is not extractive vs. one-time.
recommendations.userValue: Sustainable business model that funds development and content updates. Framing as 'pay once' appeals to macOS power-user audience. Annual option funds relationship coaching content (frameworks, templates).
recommendations.noveltyNote: P4 proposed Paddle; C5 research (June 2026) now favors LemonSqueezy for license key automation. Also added concrete annual-subscriber benefits (content packs, priority updates) so subscription is not perceived as extractive.
recommendations.dependencies: Sparkle key fixes (rec #3). LicenseStore implementation. Relationship Coach commitment (rec #1) to justify content-update-driven annual subscription.

recommendations.title: Positioning Spine: 'No Bot. No Cloud. No Subscription Required.' (if Meeting Tool Path)
recommendations.description: If choosing Meeting Tool wedge (rec #1A), adopt the positioning statement proposed by C5: 'No bot. No cloud. No subscription required. MeetingScribe records meetings locally, transcribes with Whisper on your Mac, and never sends audio to a server. Pay once. Own it.' This directly counters Granola (cloud, $14/month), Fathom (cloud, subscription), tl;dv (bot-joins-meeting). Place on landing page, Sparkle release notes, About section in SettingsView. This is marketing copy (S effort) but signals a clear user value prop that no competitor claims. Adds emphasis to write-capable MCP (Granola cannot do this) and local Ollama summaries. Does NOT conflict with annual subscription; the claim is 'no mandatory subscription' not 'no optional subscription.'
recommendations.userValue: Clear market positioning that appeals to privacy-conscious power users. Differentiates against cloud-first competitors.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From C5 audit. This is marketing copy that makes the local-first story explicit and defensible.
recommendations.dependencies: Commitment to Meeting Tool path (rec #1A).

recommendations.title: Reframe People Tab as 'Relationship Coach' If Choosing That Wedge
recommendations.description: If choosing Relationship Coach path (rec #1B), rename the People tab to 'Relationships' and reframe the UI language. Change PersonDetailView intro copy from 'add memories and notes' to 'track your relationship over time.' Add hero section above the person identity panel: a Relationship Health card showing days-since-last-check-in vs. cadence target (color-coded dot), current check-in streak, and one rotating Gottman-derived coaching prompt (static template in v1, no AI call required). Rename 'Encounters' tab to 'Moments Together'. Add 'Type' selector in AddPersonSheet as first step (3-card picker: Partner/Family/Close Friend/Colleague/Acquaintance). This repositioning is marketing + UX copy (S–M effort) but signals psychological depth that shifts willingness-to-pay from $5/month (note-taking) to $20–30/year (relationship investment). The code already has memory capture, encounter logging, iMessage analysis—the relationship coach is 60% built. The gap is framing.
recommendations.userValue: Users understand the app's purpose is relationship health, not meeting notes. Emotional resonance with the target cohort (people with partners, family, close friendships to maintain).
recommendations.noveltyNote: From C5 audit research on willingness-to-pay psychology. Positioning a tool as 'relationship coach' vs. 'note app' raises the price ceiling 2–3x.
recommendations.dependencies: Commitment to Relationship Coach path (rec #1B). RelationshipPath enum (rec #2) to make type-aware features credible.

recommendations.title: Per-Type Check-in Cadence & Notification Scheduler (Phase 2, Relationship Coach)
recommendations.description: Extend NotificationManager with `syncPersonReminders(people: [Person])` method that fires `UNCalendarNotificationTrigger` per person with a non-nil `checkInCadenceDays`. New notification category `PERSON_CHECKIN` with 'Quick Log' and 'Snooze 3 Days' actions. Per-type copy: partner → 'Haven't logged time with [Name] in N days—how are they doing?'; family → 'Quick check-in with [Name]?'. Default cadences from RelationshipPath enum: `.romanticPartner=7d`, `.familyMember=14d`, `.closeFriend=21d`, `.friend=30d`, `.colleague=infer` (existing logic). Wire `syncPersonReminders` call into `MainWindow` on launch and whenever `PeopleStore` publishes changes. Add Settings toggle `checkInRemindersEnabled` (default ON for people with explicit cadence). This is Phase 2 in MASTER_PLAN_V4 (P2-1). It is the only mechanism that pulls a lapsed user *back into* the relationship maintenance loop—all other surfaces are in-app passive.
recommendations.userValue: Habits form outside the app. A user who gets a 7pm notification 'haven't checked in with partner in 4 days' is experiencing the relationship coach's core job-to-be-done. This is the highest-impact retention mechanic.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 2 (P2-1) and P2 audit. This is the keystone retention feature that no meeting-recording app has.
recommendations.dependencies: RelationshipPath enum (rec #2). Person.checkInCadenceDays field.

recommendations.title: Chip-First Inline Encounter Quick-Log on PersonDetailView
recommendations.description: Add encounter-kind enum to `Encounter.swift`: `coffee`, `call`, `videoCall`, `inPerson`, `sharedActivity`, `birthday`, `checkIn`, `difficultConversation`, `custom`. Replace the multi-step encounter logging (find person → scroll to Encounters → tap Add → type 'event name' [required] → pick date → save) with inline quick-log widget on PersonDetailView: chip strip (icons for call/coffee/dinner/quality-time/difficult-conversation) + optional one-line note + optional mood emoji (energizing/neutral/draining/difficult). Pressing Enter creates encounter immediately, mirrors the Memories inline field pattern at PersonDetailView.swift:1334. `eventName` auto-filled from kind. This converts a 5-step flow to a 1-step flow—critical for daily relationship habit formation. From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 2 (D4-1, U1-5).
recommendations.userValue: Frictionless moment capture. A user can log 'had coffee with Mom' in 2 seconds from the People tab. This is the wedge habit that makes relationship maintenance feel automatic, not laborious.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 2. This is the friction-reduction that makes the relationship coach viable as a daily habit, not an annual review tool.
recommendations.dependencies: RelationshipPath enum (rec #2) for UI defaults. EncounterKind enum.

recommendations.title: Per-Type Relationship Content Library (Gottman, NVC, Love Languages)
recommendations.description: Create `Sources/MeetingScribe/People/RelationshipCoachContent.swift` (~200 lines). Static Swift enum with 3–5 rotating prompts per `RelationshipPath` case, no AI call required (loads instantly). Partner path: Gottman's 5:1 ratio, bids for connection, repair attempts, softened startup. Family path: NVC observation vs. evaluation, expressing feelings vs. criticizing. Close friend path: love language inference, proximity maintenance, conflict repair. Friend path: shared activity & memory building. Colleague path: professional boundary maintenance. Prompts rotate by `Calendar.current.component(.weekOfYear) % prompts.count` so the same user sees different reflection prompts each week. Example prompt for partner: 'In this week's conversations, notice moments where one of you made a bid for connection—a question, a touch, a shared joke. Did the other person turn toward, away, or against the bid? How often did you turn toward?' Zero server cost; static content that ships in the app. This is the content moat that separates MeetingScribe (relationship coach with psychological depth) from Dex (cloud CRM with no frameworks). From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 3 (P1-7).
recommendations.userValue: Psychological depth. A user reading a Gottman-derived prompt on their partner's detail page understands this is not just a note-taking app—it's a coach that speaks their language.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 3. This is net-new content that no competitor has—Lasting has licensed therapist content; MeetingScribe has community-validated frameworks embedded in the code.
recommendations.dependencies: RelationshipPath enum (rec #2). PersonDetailView refactoring (god-file decomposition).

recommendations.title: MCP: Port attach_note_to_person + Add EncounterDTO & log_encounter Tools
recommendations.description: Three independent MCP wins: (1) Port `attach_note_to_person` from `PeopleChatTools.swift:340–369` to `MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift`. Adaptation: raw-JSON patch pattern (cf. `tool_addMemory` model). Makes Claude coaching sessions cumulative—Claude can analyze a relationship and persist insights back into the app. (2) Add `EncounterDTO` to `Sources/VaultKit/SharedModels.swift`. Implement `get_person_encounters` read tool: returns `eventName`, `date`, `location`, `notes`, `meetingID`, `kind`. (3) Implement `log_encounter` write tool: writes `<storageDir>/encounters/<uuid>.json`, patches `person.json:lastInteractionAt` if encounter is more recent, posts `signalVaultChanged()`. Combined, these three tools enable Claude to (a) discover relationship history, (b) log new check-ins/encounters from a Claude session (e.g., 'I talked to Alice about career transitions yesterday'), and (c) see the app's data persist across Claude sessions. This unlocks the 'relationship coach assistant' UX where Claude proactively asks about relationships. From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 4 (E3-3, E3-1, E3-2).
recommendations.userValue: Claude Desktop becomes a relationship coach assistant, not just a meeting-query tool. A user can chat with Claude about their relationships and have the conversation persist in the vault.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 4. The encounter data exposure is unique in the MCP ecosystem—no competitor exposes structured relationship history to Claude.
recommendations.dependencies: RelationshipPath enum (rec #2). EncounterKind enum. Phase 2 work (check-in reminders).

recommendations.title: Speaker Diarization & Attribution in Transcripts (Meeting Tool Feature)
recommendations.description: Wire the existing `SpeakerDiarization.swift` output into transcript rendering and attribute action items to speakers. Today SpeakerDiarization code exists but is never surfaced in UnifiedMeetingDetail. Add a toggle in the Transcript tab to show/hide speaker labels. Update action-item rows in the Summary tab to include speaker attribution: '[Speaker Name] — fix the database indexing issue.' This is mentioned in PM audit (1.4) as the #1 transcript ask and is a high-signal meeting-tool differentiator vs. Granola (which does not ship diarization without pay-per-call API). Zero new data collection required; diarization already computed. This is presentation-layer work: ~100 LOC in UnifiedMeetingDetail.swift + MeetingSummaryTab.swift. From PM audit (1.4).
recommendations.userValue: Meeting power users understand who said what. This is table-stakes for a professional transcription product. Makes action items traceable to owners.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From PM audit findings. The code already computes diarization; surfacing it is a ~M effort win.
recommendations.dependencies: Core recording pipeline (already shipped). Transcript tab UI (already shipped).

recommendations.title: Write-Capable MCP Tools: create_action_item, update_person (Meeting Tool Path)
recommendations.description: If choosing Meeting Tool path, MCP write capability is a core moat. Add five tools already mapped in MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 4: `create_action_item` (title, project, due-date, owner-person-id), `update_action_item` (id, status, priority, assignee), `update_person` (id, name, company, role, bio, birthday), `add_memory` (already exists; wire it fully), `create_meeting_note` (meeting-id, content). These enable Claude to not just read your vault but change it—set a task to In Progress, tag a person with a new company title, draft a note back into a meeting. This is unique in the ecosystem; Granola's MCP is read-only. From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 4 (E3-4, E3-2).
recommendations.userValue: Claude becomes an agent that can take action on your behalf—create follow-up tasks, update contact info, attach notes. This justifies Pro tier and differentiates against every cloud competitor.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 4. Write capability is mentioned in P5 audit as 'the missing half of agent value.' Already partially shipped (create_action_item, add_memory exist); completing it is high-leverage.
recommendations.dependencies: Sparkle + license infrastructure (rec #4) to gate write tools to Pro users. Careful error handling for writes that can corrupt the vault.

recommendations.title: Decompose PersonDetailView God-File (~1,986 lines) into Focused Modules
recommendations.description: PersonDetailView is the largest file in the codebase at 1,986 lines (cf. E1 audit, E2 audit, MASTER_PLAN_V4). Any relationship-coach features (Phases 2–3) will add hundreds more lines to this file, making it unmaintainable. Decompose into: PersonIdentityPanel.swift (~150 lines, editable name/company/role/birthday/photo), PersonHealthSection.swift (~120 lines, relationship health card, check-in cadence, streak, coaching prompt), PersonEncountersView.swift (~200 lines, chip-first quick-log + history), PersonSectionGrid.swift (~400 lines, the 14-section scaffold with lazy loading), and keep PersonDetailView.swift as a thin coordinator (~300 lines). This is infrastructure work that unblocks Phase 2–3 contributor work. From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 6 (code health).
recommendations.userValue: Developer velocity. The file is currently a barrier to contribution and a code-review bottleneck.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 6. This is not a user-facing feature but a critical prerequisite for scaling the relationship-coach feature set without chaos.
recommendations.dependencies: No functional dependencies; can start in parallel with Phase 2 features.

recommendations.title: Launch with Free Tier: 10 Meetings/Month, People Unlimited
recommendations.description: From P4 audit and C5 freemium benchmarks: the free tier is 10 meetings/month, unlimited People CRM (no AI features on free). This is superior to time-limited trials because value accrues with use—a user who records 10 meetings has made a conscious choice to upgrade vs. an auto-downgrade from a 30-day trial creating churn. 10 meetings is enough for casual users (1–2 per week) to experience real value; frequent users hit the limit in 3–4 weeks and are prime upgrade targets. The upgrade prompt fires inline on the 11th meeting attempt, not as a modal. From P4 audit (P4-1) and C5 analysis (benchmarks support 2–5% freemium conversion; 10-meeting limit converts better than time-based because the user cohort is self-selected by engagement).
recommendations.userValue: Fair free tier that respects user time; no modal nag; clear moment for upgrade consideration.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P4 and C5 audits. The 10-meeting limit (vs. time-based) is the net-new insight from C5 freemium benchmarks.
recommendations.dependencies: LicenseStore + monetization infra (rec #4).

recommendations.title: Marketing: Explicit 'AI Features are Pro' Feature List on Upgrade Screen
recommendations.description: Once monetization is wired (rec #4), add a 'Plan' section to SettingsView (above 'About') showing Free vs. Pro feature grid. Free: Record meetings, People CRM, Calendar integration, basic task management, read-only MCP. Pro: AI summaries, action-item extraction, iMessage analysis presets, speaker diarization, write MCP tools, all integrations (Notion, Linear, Google Drive). Add a price anchor comparison (from C5 rec): show what Granola ($168/yr), Fathom ($240/yr), Lasting ($360/yr) cost vs. MeetingScribe $49 one-time. This makes the $49 price point self-justifying without copy. Place the upgrade button directly below the feature list. From C5 audit (C5-6).
recommendations.userValue: Users understand exactly what they're paying for and how it compares to alternatives. Transparency builds trust.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From C5 audit research on price anchoring. This is standard SaaS technique adapted for one-time purchase.
recommendations.dependencies: Monetization infra (rec #4). Clear Pro feature set decided.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe has a fully-designed but inert monetization system: the paywall view is built but never presented (zero `.sheet` binding in MainWindow), feature gates exist but aren't called in any user flow (checked via grep across 223 files), and the converted features (coaching frameworks, MCP tools, check-in notifications) are ungated with no enforcement. The core blockers are architectural (paywall wiring), but the deeper issue is positioning: the app frames itself as "privacy-first" when the real wedge—for its highest-WTP buyers (legal/healthcare/finance)—is "compliance-grade local" where cloud tools are banned. Without Day 0 onboarding, even Day 1 conversion triggers fail.

recommendations.title: Wire ProPaywallView to MainWindow with a single .sheet binding
recommendations.description: Add `.sheet(item: $FeatureGate.shared.paywallFeature) { ProPaywallView(feature: $0) }` in MainWindow.swift body. This is the single missing link that activates an entire infrastructure: FeatureGate.showPaywall() calls exist in StoreKitManager (line 64), PersonDetailView (line 45, coaching content), AddPersonSheet (for unlimitedPeople gate), and RelationshipNotificationManager—but no view observes paywallFeature to present. One line unblocks every downstream conversion.
recommendations.userValue: Users who hit a Pro-feature gate will see the upgrade prompt instead of silently getting the feature for free or seeing nothing. This is the final-mile wire for the designed system.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Cited in P2-1 (prior audit); this is a confirmed zero-effort, high-impact fix that should ship immediately before any other paywall work.
recommendations.dependencies: None. Works today with isPro = false in release builds.

recommendations.title: Default Day 0 onboarding to pre-seed 3 key relationships
recommendations.description: On first app launch, show a 3-step sheet: (1) 'Who are the 3 people you most want to stay connected with?' (add names + relationship type), (2) 'Set a check-in cadence for each', (3) 'Your relationship graph is ready' → close and render TodayView with those 3 people showing as overdue/upcoming at Day 0. Use @AppStorage("hasCompletedRelationshipOnboarding") to show once. Today's TodayView opens to a blank screen (no Aha Moment) — this fixes the #1 conversion-blocking gap identified in C4 research (5-Minute Aha Rule).
recommendations.userValue: The app feels immediately personal and useful. A user who names 3 people and sets cadences has emotionally invested in the product within 2 minutes of install—they are now a subscriber-in-waiting. Fixes the Day 0 Aha problem that blocks every downstream conversion mechanic.
recommendations.noveltyNote: C4-4 from prior audit. This is the structural prerequisite for all downstream conversion triggers (drift alarm paywall, coaching content gate, people limit gate) to actually fire with intent.
recommendations.dependencies: None. Can be built in parallel with other work; uses existing OnboardingSheet + QuickEncounterSheet patterns already in codebase.

recommendations.title: Reposition to 'compliance-grade local' as the wedge, not 'privacy'
recommendations.description: Privacy is a qualifier, not a wedge. Build one 1-page artifact: 'Why your security team will approve MeetingScribe' (no data egress, on-device Whisper + Ollama, auditable local vault in iCloud Drive, no vendor DPA needed). Update landing-page copy and Feature breakdown in ProPaywallView to lead with: 'Record the calls Otter won't let you' (targeting legal, healthcare, finance, therapy, M&A, defense contractors). These segments have highest willingness-to-pay and legally *cannot* paste calls into cloud tools. The technical truth (OllamaService on localhost, WhisperRunner.swift local inference) already exists in code—only the positioning is missing.
recommendations.userValue: Aims the product at buyers who must pay (compliance requirement, not preference). This segment is 3–5x less price-sensitive than general 'privacy-conscious' buyers. Single highest-leverage positioning move.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new framing of P3-1 (prior audit). The prior recommendation was to re-anchor the *go-to-market* on compliance; this extends it to reframe the core *value prop* in the paywall, landing page, and feature bullets.
recommendations.dependencies: None. Positioning-only; no code changes required.

recommendations.title: License the runtime + intelligence layer, not the data
recommendations.description: Create a `LicenseManager` that gates *features* (cross-meeting Q&A, follow-up generation, advanced templates, warm-model fast path), not the vault. The vault (recordings, transcripts, notes) is always free and always yours—exportable as plain Markdown in iCloud Drive. On license expiry, users keep every recording and can still read everything via the free MCP tools; they lose only the *new* AI conveniences (summarize, template generation, coaching frameworks). This requires an offline license validation system: Ed25519 public key embedded in the app, license blob checked locally (works on airplane mode). Monetize via 'Intelligence+' subscription, not a 'Pro' tier. This aligns with the local-first buyer psychology (Obsidian, Things, Bear crowd) who distrust subscriptions for offline software and see data lock-in as the primary anti-pattern.
recommendations.userValue: Removes the #1 objection to paying for subscription software: 'What if they go under / I can't afford it / I lose my data?' By decoupling license from vault, this becomes a marketing advantage: 'You can never lose access to your own meetings.' Competitors cannot claim this.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extension of P3-2 (prior audit). This recommendation adds the *enforcement mechanism* (offline license validation via Ed25519) and bridges to pricing structure (Intelligence+ subscription naming, not Pro).
recommendations.dependencies: None architecturally; depends on pricing decision (P3-3 below).

recommendations.title: Test hybrid one-time license + optional Intelligence+ subscription
recommendations.description: Replace the prior audit's SaaS pricing ladder ($12-15/mo) with a structure that matches local-first buyer psychology: (a) **MeetingScribe One** — $79-99 one-time, perpetual license to the app + all local recording/transcription/CRM forever; (b) **Intelligence+** — optional $8-10/mo *only* for features with ongoing cost (cross-meeting Q&A, auto follow-ups, new template packs, coaching framework updates). Frame the subscription as 'the smart layer,' not 'the app.' In ProPaywallView, lead with the annual Intelligence+ plan ($89/year, ~$7.42/mo) as the default, show monthly ($10/mo) as the budget option. This removes the 'why pay monthly for software on my machine?' objection and improves LTV by aligning price model with how local-first users want to pay. The one-time option also de-risks the churn narrative: users are paying for a license, not a recurring subscription lock.
recommendations.userValue: Psychological anchoring: monthly at $10 makes annual at $89 feel like a deal. One-time $79 purchase feels like 'owning software' (trusted mental model). Removes subscription anxiety for target segment.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new pricing structure from P3-3 (prior audit). This adds the explicit one-time tier option and repositions the subscription as optional, ongoing AI enhancements rather than a gated app.
recommendations.dependencies: LicenseManager (P3-2 above); StoreKit 2 wiring for actual purchase flow.

recommendations.title: Gate check-in notifications at the 3-person cap with a paywall at person #4
recommendations.description: In RelationshipNotificationManager.syncPersonReminders(), add: `guard FeatureGate.shared.isEnabled(.checkInNotifications) else { /* cap at 3, cancel rest */ }`. Currently, free users get unlimited push reminders (line 1 in prior audit's gap list). When a user adds a 4th relationship with a check-in cadence, the 4th person's notification is silently never scheduled. Instead, present the paywall: 'Sarah is 47 days overdue — unlock check-in reminders with Intelligence+ →.' This creates the highest-emotional-relevance paywall moment (user is thinking about a real person they care about, not abstract features). No code changes needed to the gate itself (FeatureGate.isEnabled(.checkInNotifications) already returns false for free tier); only needs the RelationshipNotificationManager to actually call isEnabled() and the paywall wire (from P2-1 above).
recommendations.userValue: Never lose a relationship to drift. The paywall at this moment feels like caring, not spending. Highest conversion probability in the entire funnel (C4 research: emotional stakes are high; user already invested time in CRM; notification creates daily return-visit cadence).
recommendations.noveltyNote: C4-1 from prior audit, but re-framed: this recommendation focuses on the *gate enforcement at the moment of highest emotional intent* rather than just the messaging. The paywall shouldn't fire when the user tries to add the 4th person; it should fire when the app would schedule a notification and realizes it can't.
recommendations.dependencies: P2-1 (paywall wire), RelationshipNotificationManager check, C4-2 onboarding (so user has 3+ people by Day 7).

recommendations.title: Wire RelationshipPromptLibrary into PersonDetailView with a Pro gate
recommendations.description: RelationshipPromptLibrary.swift contains 28 fully-written coaching prompts (Gottman, NVC, love languages) by relationship type, but is never called from any view. Add a 'This week's coaching prompt' card in PersonDetailView, conditionally gated on FeatureGate.isEnabled(.relationshipContent). Free users see a blurred preview: 'Your coaching prompt this week [blurred] → Unlock with Intelligence+.' This makes the coaching content *visible* and *tangible*—currently it's listed in the paywall bullets as an abstract feature. Free users understand exactly what they're missing; Pro users get recurring fresh content with emotional relevance to the specific person they're viewing.
recommendations.userValue: The coaching content is fully built and sitting idle. This surfaces it in context (on the person's profile) where emotional relevance is highest. Creates a visible in-product case for upgrading, not just abstract paywall copy.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P2-6 from prior audit, with explicit focus on *making it visible in the UI*. Prior recommendation was to 'wire' the library; this adds the gating card + blurred teaser UX.
recommendations.dependencies: P2-1 (paywall wire); RelationshipPromptLibrary already exists at Sources/MeetingScribe/People/RelationshipPromptLibrary.swift.

recommendations.title: Gate the unlimitedPeople limit (5 typed relationships) with hard enforcement in AddPersonSheet
recommendations.description: FeatureGate.ManagedFeature.unlimitedPeople exists and returns false for free tier, but is never enforced anywhere in the codebase. A user can add unlimited typed relationships forever. In AddPersonSheet.swift, before saving a new person, check: `let typedCount = people.people.filter { $0.relationshipType != .unset }.count; if typedCount >= 5 && !FeatureGate.shared.isEnabled(.unlimitedPeople) { FeatureGate.shared.showPaywall(for: .unlimitedPeople); return }`. This catches power users (those who have already added 6+ people) at peak engagement. The gate is designed, described in the paywall bullets, documented in FeatureGate.swift — it just needs one enforcement call.
recommendations.userValue: Power users who invest in the relationship graph hit a natural upgrade moment when they realize they can't add more. The timing (when they're actively adding people) is high-intent.
recommendations.noveltyNote: C4-6 from prior audit; cited as 'easy to implement, highest intent cohort.' This is a confirmed quick win.
recommendations.dependencies: P2-1 (paywall wire); no architectural changes needed.

recommendations.title: Add a Debug paywall override toggle to SettingsView (QA enabler)
recommendations.description: Currently FeatureGate.overrideAllEnabled = true in DEBUG builds (FeatureGate.swift:55), meaning no developer can test the paywall without a debugger breakpoint. Add a Developer section in SettingsView (already shown in DEBUG, e.g., `#if DEBUG { ... }`): toggle 'Simulate free tier' that sets `FeatureGate.shared.overrideAllEnabled = false; isPro = false`, and 'Simulate Pro' that sets `isPro = true`. Persist via @AppStorage. This removes the friction from paywall UX testing and ensures QA validates the entire monetization flow before launch (currently untested).
recommendations.userValue: Internal. Unblocks the entire QA workflow for monetization. Every other gate-wiring item is meaningless until the paywall can be tested in DEBUG.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P2-4 from prior audit; necessary hygiene before launch. This should ship with P2-1 (paywall wire).
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Invert FeatureGate.overrideAllEnabled default from true → false; add explicit opt-in
recommendations.description: Change FeatureGate.swift:55 from `var overrideAllEnabled: Bool = true` to `var overrideAllEnabled: Bool = false`. Add a one-time launch check: `if CommandLine.arguments.contains("--dev-unlock") { overrideAllEnabled = true }`. Add that argument to the Xcode DEBUG scheme (`Edit Scheme → Arguments Passed On Launch → --dev-unlock`). This inversion means developers must *deliberately opt in* to bypass mode, not *deliberately opt out*. Currently every dev run unlocks all Pro features, meaning the entire gated-feature system is exercised only by developers who remember to flip a breakpoint. No feature gate has ever been validated by the QA workflow.
recommendations.userValue: Internal. Ensures every developer run exercises the free-tier code paths by default. Complements P2-4 (Settings toggle for testing without relaunch).
recommendations.noveltyNote: P2-8 from prior audit; flagged as 'the single highest-leverage change for monetization integrity.' This is structural root cause, not a feature.
recommendations.dependencies: None; should ship with P2-4 (QA toggle).

recommendations.title: Persist isPro to Keychain instead of UserDefaults (bypass resistance)
recommendations.description: FeatureGate.swift:50 persists isPro to UserDefaults. A technical user can run `defaults write com.tyleryannes.MeetingScribe featureGate.isPro 1` and unlock Pro forever. While not a serious attack vector for a $4.99 app, it's a trivially-avoided embarrassment. Move persistence to KeychainStore (already used for API keys in Sources/MeetingScribe/Security/KeychainStore.swift) with key 'featureGate.isPro'. The Keychain is harder to tamper with and signals that the Pro state is a real credential.
recommendations.userValue: Basic bypass resistance. Minimal effort (Keychain store already exists); necessary hygiene before public launch.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P2-10 from prior audit. Low-effort, high-signal credibility move.
recommendations.dependencies: Real StoreKit wiring (Phase 9 in prior plan) so isPro can be durably set via purchase.

recommendations.title: Call RelationshipNotificationManager.syncPersonReminders() on app launch
recommendations.description: Currently notifications for overdue relationships are only scheduled when a user logs a new encounter via QuickEncounterSheet (line 218). A user who installs the app, adds 5 people, then doesn't open it for a week receives no reminders, defeating the habit loop. In MeetingScribeApp.startServices() (around line 200), add: `Task { @MainActor in await RelationshipNotificationManager.shared.syncPersonReminders(people: PeopleStore.shared.people) }` gated on `FeatureGate.shared.isEnabled(.checkInNotifications)` (respects free-tier 3-person cap). This ensures the notification system works as designed: every launch, the app checks who's overdue and schedules/updates their notifications.
recommendations.userValue: The notification system actually works as intended after this fix. Without launch sync, check-in reminders are mostly dead (only fire after user manually logs an encounter).
recommendations.noveltyNote: P2-5 from prior audit; flagged as 'high impact — without launch sync, check-in notifications are mostly dead.'
recommendations.dependencies: P2-2 (gate the sync call to respect free-tier cap).

recommendations.title: Extend trial from 7 to 14 days with messaging update
recommendations.description: RevenueCat 2026 data: 17–32-day trials convert at 45.7% vs. 26.8% for 3–7-day trials. MeetingScribe's 7-day trial is at the bottom of the conversion curve. Change ProPaywallView.swift:71 label from '7-Day Free Trial' to '14-Day Free Trial' and update StoreKitManager.triggerUpgradePromptIfNeeded():62 rate-limiting from 7 days to 14. A 14-day trial gives weekly check-in relationships time to fire at least once, creating the emotional trigger before trial ends. No revenue loss given the app's core value (relationship intelligence) requires 1–2 weeks of real use to demonstrate.
recommendations.userValue: Enough time to feel the product's relationship-intelligence value before committing. Higher trial-to-paid conversion based on industry benchmarks.
recommendations.noveltyNote: C4-2 from prior audit. Simple change, measurable impact.
recommendations.dependencies: StoreKit wiring for trial management.

recommendations.title: Create 'Programmable Vault' as a net-new Developer/Power tier
recommendations.description: The 17-tool MCP server (Sources/MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift) is a category no competitor has. Package it as a standalone **Developer/Power tier**: 'Your meetings are an API.' Ship example recipes in docs: auto-file action items to Linear, weekly digest via cron + MCP, 'what did we decide about X' terminal query, Claude Desktop automation templates. Price it per-seat (~$15/seat) as an optional add-on to Intelligence+ or standalone. This reaches the Cursor/Claude-power-user audience—exactly the people who evangelize tools. The code already exists; this is pure positioning and documentation.
recommendations.userValue: Turns the meeting vault into automatable infrastructure. Reaches an unserved market segment (developers + knowledge workers who use Claude Desktop) with a defensible, uncopyable feature.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P3-5 from prior audit. Net-new tier that prior SaaS pricing ladder missed.
recommendations.dependencies: Write-capable MCP (already shipped per rebuild audit); LicenseManager (P3-2) for gating.

recommendations.title: Design 'Data Bill of Rights' as a marketing guarantee + trust asset
recommendations.description: Turn the 'it's just Markdown + SQLite you own' property into an explicit, marketed promise: a 1-page 'Data Bill of Rights' stating: Your recordings, transcripts, and notes are plain files in your iCloud Drive. They're readable without the app. You keep them forever, even if you stop paying. Competitors lock data in their cloud. Include this in the landing page and ProPaywallView (after the feature bullets, before pricing). This converts the *monetization risk* of frictionless export into a *trust asset* that drives purchase: 'Safe to try—you can never get trapped.' Research shows this removes the #1 objection to subscription software for local-first buyers.
recommendations.userValue: Removes the #1 objection to paying for any subscription software: 'What if I want to leave?' Signals credibility and aligns with local-first buyer values.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P3-8 from prior audit. Positioning + messaging-only; no code changes required.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe has the infrastructure for strong activation (record → transcribe → summarize → action) but leaves critical friction unmeasured and multiple "aha" moments unfired. The path from install to first meeting summary is gated by onboarding setup, yet there is no instrumentation to track drop-off, no celebration of the first summary, and no guided "first action" flow that keeps momentum. Relationship maintenance, the key differentiator over generic meeting tools, is completely invisible in the new-user experience. Most critically: the app measures nothing about activation—no "first summary" milestones, no activation funnel events, no re-engagement campaigns. This is a major blind spot.

recommendations.title: Instrument the activation funnel with zero-friction event tracking
recommendations.description: Add a lightweight event logger that tracks: (1) app install, (2) onboarding started, (3) onboarding completed, (4) first recording initiated (ad-hoc / calendar / detected), (5) first recording finished, (6) first transcript generated, (7) first summary generated, (8) first action item marked done, (9) first person added. Log these to a local SQLite table (MeetingScribe/Events/ActivationEvents.swift) keyed by date + event type, with optional metadata (duration, error). Expose a read-only MCP tool `list_activation_events()` so Claude can surface trends ("You've been using MeetingScribe for 5 days; generated 3 summaries" in the chat). Wire a one-line event emit into each checkpoint (stopRecording, summary finalize, actionItems.markDone, etc.). This requires zero external telemetry and lives entirely locally, but gives Tyler visibility into where users drop off and whether the core loop works. Currently the app is blind to whether users ever record a meeting post-onboarding.
recommendations.userValue: Unknown whether first-time users successfully complete the record→summarize loop, or where they abandon. Activation is invisible, so retention investment is guesswork.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; prior plans never proposed instrumentation of the activation funnel.
recommendations.dependencies: Minimal. Requires a schema-light SQLite table and event-emit calls sprinkled into 5–6 existing orchestration points (MeetingManager, pipelineController, actionItems).

recommendations.title: Celebrate the first AI summary with a "you did it" moment
recommendations.description: When the first-ever summary is generated and displayed to the user (Sources/MeetingScribe/UI/MeetingSummaryTab.swift pastSummaryBody), show a non-modal overlay banner or a slide-in card for 4 seconds: "✨ Your first summary! MeetingScribe recorded, transcribed, and summarized your meeting automatically." with a happy emoji or confetti micro-animation (uses SwiftUI native spring animation, no third-party libs). The banner auto-dismisses or on-tap, and is gated by `UserDefaults(hasSeenFirstSummaryBanner)` so it fires only once ever. If Ollama wasn't running (empty summary state), show: "Recording ready — start Ollama to generate a summary." This is the single highest-leverage aha moment in the product — make it memorable. The prior plan (MASTER_PLAN_V3 §4) mentions it as a one-time how-it-works overview but doesn't detail the summary-specific celebration. Do both: a one-time 30-second product intro AFTER onboarding (flow: record → transcript → summary → action items), then celebrate the first real summary immediately.
recommendations.userValue: New users who record their first meeting don't realize the summary was auto-generated—many expect to write it manually. A celebratory moment explains the value prop and builds confidence in the automation.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; extends the first-run narrative from onboarding → first aha moment.
recommendations.dependencies: Depends on activation event instrumentation (PM-A1) to know when first summary fires; could also gate on a simple count-based heuristic.

recommendations.title: Onboard users to relationship maintenance flow in the first week
recommendations.description: The rebuild ships People CRM with a powerful graph (meetings with shared attendees, iMessage analysis, encounters, relationships) but the new-user onboarding (OnboardingSheet.swift + SetupCheckSheet.swift) explains only vault location + macOS permissions. Add a second "How MeetingScribe works" card shown once after the first summary, with three screens: (1) "Record meetings → automatic summaries + action items" (already done), (2) "Build your network: meet with people → they appear in your People tab", with a screenshot of a person detail card showing encounters, messages, and relationships, and (3) "Stay in touch: MeetingScribe reminds you to check in with people you care about" with a screenshot of the "Stay in touch" widget on Today. No interaction required—just explain the three cores of the app (meetings, people, habits). This lives in a bottom-sheet or carousel in MainWindow, shown once via `@AppStorage("hasSeenProductTour")`. The P1 audit recommends per-relationship-type check-in nudges; this tour primes users to expect and value them when they land.
recommendations.userValue: Users who record one meeting don't naturally discover the People tab or understand that relationship maintenance is core to the product. Many treat it as a meeting-notes tool only, missing the moat.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Builds on P1 (relationship-type audit) and P2 (retention audit) by front-loading the value prop.
recommendations.dependencies: Depends on first-summary celebration (PM-A2) to find the right moment to insert the tour. Ideally after first summary, before the user closes the app.

recommendations.title: Add a pre-recording "prepare your meeting" briefing launcher
recommendations.description: When a user clicks "Start recording" for an upcoming calendar meeting (not ad-hoc), show an inline slide-up card before recording begins: "About to record a meeting with [Attendees]. View your brief?" with a "View brief" button that opens PreMeetingBriefView (which already exists and shows prior meetings with shared attendees + open action items). This reduces friction on the first recording by showing the user what the app can do (surfacing context). The brief is optional—a "Skip" button starts recording immediately. Gate this flow to show only on the first 3 calendar-meeting recordings (track with a counter in AppSettings), so it doesn't become persistent UI spam. This is a lightweight onboarding hook that leverages existing code and builds confidence in the app's intelligence before the first summary even lands.
recommendations.userValue: First-time users recording a calendar meeting don't know the app has context about the attendees or prior conversations. Showing the brief unprompted (once) builds credibility and explains the value of the People CRM.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; uses PreMeetingBriefView (already built) in a new first-run context.
recommendations.dependencies: None; all components exist. Requires AppSettings counter + UI gate only.

recommendations.title: Track and incentivize the first 5 action items completed
recommendations.description: Extend activation instrumentation (PM-A1) to track when a user completes their first 5 action items (meeting-extracted + manual). When the 5th item is marked done, show a slide-in notification: "🎯 5 action items completed! Your tasks are in sync." If the user has not marked any action items done after 3 days post-first-summary, fire a gentle reminder notification: "Your meeting from [date] has 2 open tasks — want to check them?" deep-linking to the meeting. This keeps activation momentum by making task completion visible and rewarded. It also validates the second core of the product (action items / Tasks tab) before users discover People/relationships.
recommendations.userValue: After recording and summarizing, users don't necessarily discover the Tasks tab or understand that extracted action items are actionable—they may think summaries are read-only. Making task completion a milestone builds the habit.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends PM-A1 instrumentation into behavioral nudges; not explicitly in prior plans.
recommendations.dependencies: Activation event instrumentation (PM-A1) + NotificationManager (already set up for meetings; add a new category).

recommendations.title: Default the chat rail closed on first run; explain it once
recommendations.description: Today the chat rail (40% of window width on wide displays) defaults closed but is confusing to new users who open it accidentally and see an empty assistant panel. Already implemented per MASTER_PLAN_V3 (LAY-2), but add a one-time explanatory tooltip: when a user first hovers over the 'Open chat' affordance in the toolbar, show a small popover: "Ask Claude about your meetings, people, and tasks" with a 'Learn more' button that opens a help article. The chat rail should stay OFF by default for new installs (already done), but new users should understand what it is when they discover it. This removes the friction of "why is there an empty panel?" and makes the assistant opt-in and intentional.
recommendations.userValue: New users see an empty right panel and think it's broken, or they panic that they're missing something. Explaining the purpose removes uncertainty.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces LAY-2 from MASTER_PLAN_V3 with UX copy.
recommendations.dependencies: Chat rail already defaults closed; requires only tooltip + help link.

recommendations.title: Add a weekly "activation milestone" email/in-app digest in the first month
recommendations.description: For users in their first month (track signup date via AppSettings), surface a weekly in-app card on Today (below the header, above meetings) titled "Your week with MeetingScribe": stats on meetings recorded, summaries generated, action items completed, and people met. Example: "📊 This week: 3 meetings recorded, 3 summaries, 2 action items done, met with 5 people." On week 4, escalate to a celebratory message: "Congrats — you've recorded X meetings this month. Want to invite your team?" (optional share flow). This serves two purposes: (1) it validates the core loop for new users (do-feel-achieve), and (2) it creates a retention anchor point (weekly check-in habit). The digest is gated to show only during weeks 1–4 post-install (after which it disappears). Data is computed from activation events + MeetingStore + PeopleStore; no external API needed.
recommendations.userValue: New users don't know if they're using the app 'right' or if their behavior is normal. Seeing weekly progress builds confidence and makes the habit stick.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; combines activation instrumentation with retention mechanics in the first month.
recommendations.dependencies: Activation event instrumentation (PM-A1) + a simple date-math gate (sign-up date + week number).

recommendations.title: Redesign SetupReadiness to explain *why* Ollama/Whisper matter
recommendations.description: SetupCheckSheet.swift ("Getting things ready") shows two checkboxes (Whisper model, Ollama running) but doesn't explain to a non-technical user why they matter or what happens if they're skipped. Rewrite the subtitles: instead of "A ~140 MB on-device speech model. Downloads once," say "Transcribes what everyone says in the meeting — happens automatically after you stop recording." Instead of "Powers summaries on-device. Install it once, then come back and re-check," say "Creates summaries from your transcripts — think of it like a colleague writing a recap." Add a small "Why this matters" link per component that expands a brief explanation. The "Skip for now" button should warn: "You can record, but summaries won't work until Ollama is running. You can finish setup anytime from Settings." This de-jargons the setup flow and sets realistic expectations—many users will skip Ollama initially and come back when they hit the empty-summary state, which is fine and expected.
recommendations.userValue: Non-technical users see "Ollama" and "Whisper" and have no idea what these are or why they're required. Explaining in plain language removes the friction of feeling stupid.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Builds on the existing SetupCheckSheet (already shipping) with better copy and explanation.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pure copy/UX refinement of existing sheet.

recommendations.title: Create a "First 30 days" in-app guide with task milestones
recommendations.description: Add a modal card (dismissible, shown once on day 1 of install) titled "Welcome to MeetingScribe — here's your 30-day roadmap" with five milestone tasks: (1) Record your first meeting (checkbox, checked if already done), (2) Generate a summary (show progress: 0/1), (3) Add a person to your network (0/1), (4) Mark an action item done (0/1), (5) Check in with someone (0/1). As the user completes each task, they see a small checkmark appear. When all 5 are done, show a confetti animation + "You've unlocked the full power of MeetingScribe!" The card lives as a collapsible section on Today (below the header) and can be permanently dismissed via a small 'x' button or auto-collapses when all 5 tasks are complete. This roadmap serves as a low-pressure onboarding flow that guides users through all four pillars (meetings, summaries, people, action items, relationship maintenance) without forcing them. It's visible but not intrusive, and it creates natural milestones.
recommendations.userValue: New users don't have a mental model of what to do next; a roadmap removes decision paralysis and creates a sense of progress/achievement.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Combines activation event tracking (PM-A1) with a gamified first-30-days onboarding journey.
recommendations.dependencies: Activation event instrumentation (PM-A1) to drive task completion; stores milestone state in AppSettings.

recommendations.title: Add deep-link shortcuts for "first action" entry points
recommendations.description: The app supports deep links (meetingscribe://meeting/<id>, etc. in WorkspaceLink.swift), but only from MCP/Spotlight. Create Spotlight-compatible Siri Shortcuts for first-time users: "Record a meeting," "View my summary," "Add a person," "Quick task." These are standard macOS shortcuts (built in Shortcuts app, not in code), but ensure they're discoverable in setup—add a "Use Siri shortcuts" card in SetupCheckSheet that links to a help article. This gives keyboard-driven power users an immediate productivity path (⌘-Space → "Record meeting" → done) without forcing GUI exploration. For non-Siri users, add quick-action buttons to the menu bar (currently MenuBarView only shows the record button and live timer). A "Record" + "New task" + "New person" triptych in the menu bar means users never have to open the main window to get things done.
recommendations.userValue: Power users expect keyboard shortcuts and menu-bar integration; Mac users expect these affordances. Without them, the app feels GUI-only and slow.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; uses existing deep-link infrastructure in a first-run context.
recommendations.dependencies: WorkspaceLink routing already exists; requires Shortcuts authoring (external to repo) + menu bar actions (MenuBarView.swift).

recommendations.title: Move the first-time "how it works" video/animation into the app
recommendations.description: Many Mac productivity apps (Things, OmniFocus, Notion) include an in-app first-run video or animated walkthrough. MeetingScribe's onboarding (OnboardingSheet + SetupCheckSheet) explains permissions and stack setup but never shows a user what the *app itself* looks like or how the core loop works. Add a 90-second animated guide (made with Lottie or SwiftUI canvas animation, no video file) shown once after setup completes: "1. Start a recording or join a Zoom [animation of mic + camera icons] → 2. Attend the meeting [clock ticking] → 3. Stop recording [recording stops] → 4. Automatic transcript [sparkles] → 5. Summary + action items [summary appears] → 6. Mark tasks done [checkmark]." This is a passive walkthrough, not interactive, but it sets expectations and gets users excited. Keep it under 2 MB; host it as a Swift canvas animation in a new view (OnboardingAnimationView.swift) rather than a video file. Show it in a full-screen sheet after onboarding, skippable with a 'Done' button.
recommendations.userValue: After explaining permissions and setup, users still don't know what the app *does* until they record a meeting. A visual walkthrough pre-explains the value and builds anticipation.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends the onboarding narrative (already multi-step) with visual storytelling.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires animation/illustration design; could be built with SwiftUI Canvas or Lottie JSON (lightweight).

recommendations.title: Implement activation funnel A/B test gate for paywall timing
recommendations.description: StoreKitManager.swift has a stub `triggerUpgradePromptIfNeeded()` that fires after the first AI summary (commented as "after first AI summary"), but there's no data on the optimal timing. Build a lightweight A/B test framework: add a field to ActivationEvents (PM-A1) called `testGroup: String` (e.g., "paywall_day1_summaries", "paywall_day3_summaries", "no_paywall_control"). On install, randomly assign the user to one of three groups (70% control, 15% day1, 15% day3). When the user's first summary is generated: if group == day1, call `triggerUpgradePrompt`; if group == day3, schedule it for 3 days later via a local notification. Log when the paywall is shown, when it's dismissed, and if a purchase happens. After 4 weeks of data, analyze which cohort has the highest conversion *without* increasing churn in the control group. This is a data-driven way to optimize monetization without guessing. The infrastructure is already there (FeatureGate, StoreKit, event logging); just add the randomization + conditional trigger.
recommendations.userValue: Unknown whether showing a paywall after the first summary helps or hurts monetization. Testing reveals the answer.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Combines PM-A1 instrumentation with monetization strategy; net-new.
recommendations.dependencies: Activation event instrumentation (PM-A1) + existing StoreKitManager + FeatureGate.

recommendations.title: Add a "quick wins" widget for day 1–7 post-install to build habit
recommendations.description: On Today's main feed, insert a widget (after the header, before meetings) for users in their first week called "Quick wins this week" with three one-tap actions: "Record a 2-min test meeting" (starts a timer, auto-stops at 2 min, shows transcript instantly), "View a sample summary" (shows a pre-built example meeting with a real summary so users see the quality), "See how AI helps with tasks" (shows a sample meeting with extracted action items + their timeline). This gives users immediate aha moments without waiting for their own meetings to process. The test recording is particularly powerful—a 2-minute recording takes 30 seconds to transcribe (live) and generates a 2-sentence summary instantly, proving the speed. This widget appears only for the first 7 days; after that it fades away. It's a retention anchor that keeps users engaged during the critical first week when they're most likely to abandon.
recommendations.userValue: Users who record their first real meeting might wait hours for Ollama to summarize it, lose confidence, and abandon. A 2-minute test recording with instant results proves the concept and builds momentum.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new; uses existing Whisper + summary pipeline in a first-run demo context.
recommendations.dependencies: Quick-test meeting state machine + pre-built sample summary JSON (stored in app). No external data needed.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe has built a strong capture loop and several compounding-value features (encounter tracking, relationship types, per-person cadence), but the retention engine remains incomplete. The app lacks proactive re-engagement triggers when users have light-meeting days, daily/weekly recap rituals that reflect value back, and a unified notification control panel. Additionally, follow-up workflow and commitment tracking lack lifecycle visibility, and the "on-device RAG" query surface hasn't shipped. The highest-impact opportunities cluster around pushing pre-meeting context into notifications, completing the daily/weekly recap rituals, tracking follow-up completion state, and enabling relationship-focused filters on the MCP server.

recommendations.title: Push synthesized pre-meeting brief into meeting-start notification (P2-2)
recommendations.description: Currently NotificationManager.syncScheduled() fires a meeting reminder 10s before each calendar event with generic 'Tap to record' body (NotificationManager.swift:106). P2-2 calls for enriching this with headline facts from PreMeetingBriefView — e.g., '3 open items you owe Sarah; last met 12 days ago' — and adding a 'Prep' action that deep-links to PreMeetingBriefView. Implementation: ComputeBrief logic already exists; extract 1-2 sentences into a brief summary string, pass it via the `briefs` dict parameter to syncScheduled (already scaffolded at line 79), and update notification body to prepend the brief before the action line.
recommendations.userValue: Turns the app's best compounding feature from pull (user must remember to tap into detail before meeting) into push (meeting reminder includes prep context). Eliminates the 'I should have prepped' friction and increases chance of smooth meetings — directly drives repeat use.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Directly from prior P2/retention plans; this is a cross-audit convergence item (P2-2, P2-3 endorse it as highest-priority). Implementation reuses existing PreMeetingBriefView compute logic.

recommendations.title: Daily end-of-day recap notification + weekly review ritual
recommendations.description: Ship TDY-6 from MASTER_PLAN_V3. Add a repeating evening notification (user-configurable time, default 6pm) that summarizes: 'Meetings recorded: 3, Action items completed: 2, Follow-ups pending: 1.' On Friday, escalate to a full 'Weekly Review' that auto-fills the existing weekly-review note template (NoteTemplate.swift:92) with LLM-generated Highlights / What slipped / Next week's focus computed from that week's meetings + task completions. Extend NotificationManager with a new `scheduleEveningRecap()` method and wire it to AppSettings.recapNotificationEnabled. Update SettingsView to expose time picker + daily/weekly toggle.
recommendations.userValue: Closes the retention loop by reflecting accumulated weekly value back to the user — the cheapest psychological reinforcement available to a local-first app. Converts a blank template into a generative ritual. For a user with a light Friday, the Friday notification is the only reason they reopen.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Endorse from P2-3 (MASTER_PLAN_V3 TDY-6); not net-new but incomplete. Pairs with P2-2 to create a daily trigger (morning: prep; evening: recap) that anchors the habit independent of calendar density.

recommendations.title: Follow-up lifecycle tracking and 'unsent' surfacing
recommendations.description: FollowUpView already scaffolds P2-6: it has `meetingID` and `markedSent` (line 17–18, FollowUpView.swift). Add a FollowUpStatus store (persistent file or AppSettings) that tracks per-meeting whether a follow-up was drafted, sent, and when. Wire the 'Send in Mail' / 'Send in Slack' actions to call FollowUpStatus.markSent(meetingID). Surface 'drafted but not sent' follow-ups in NeedsAttentionWidget as a dedicated row above overdue tasks. After meeting ends, if no follow-up is sent within ~24h and there were action items, fire a gentle nudge notification (low-priority, no sound): 'Reminder: you drafted a recap for [Meeting] — send it?'
recommendations.userValue: Closes the most common forgotten commitment — the recap email you meant to send. Turns a draft that decays into a trackable commitment. Retention hook: the app catches dropped balls on your behalf, which builds trust in checking the app daily.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Direct from prior plan (P2-6); scaffolding exists but not wired. Completes the follow-up workflow loop.

recommendations.title: Morning brief notification (light-meeting-day re-engagement hook)
recommendations.description: Extend NotificationManager with `scheduleMorningBrief()` complementing the existing `scheduleDailyBrief()` (line 216). Fire at user-configurable time (default 8am) with body: 'Today: 2 meetings, 3 tasks due, 1 person to reconnect with.' Tapping opens Today tab. This fills the 'no meeting imminent' gap: currently NotificationManager only fires reactively (meeting starting, transcript ready) — a user with a light Monday gets zero reason to open the app. Settings.swift already has `dailyBriefEnabled`; add `morningBriefEnabled` toggle + time picker in SettingsView's new Notifications section.
recommendations.userValue: Re-engagement when the calendar is empty — the single most common lapse moment. Anchors the daily-check-in habit independent of meeting density. For users in low-meeting periods, this is the difference between dropping the app for a week vs. opening daily.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-5 (prior plans); lightweight and high-leverage. Re-engages lapsed users via glanceable summary.

recommendations.title: Unified notifications control panel in Settings
recommendations.description: Replace the scattered notification toggles (dailyBriefEnabled line 225 in Settings.swift, notifyAtMeetingStart line 24, notifyTaskDue line 341) with a dedicated 'Notifications' section in SettingsView. Expose granular controls: Meeting reminders (toggle + action choice), Daily brief (toggle + time), Morning brief (toggle + time), Task due-date reminders (toggle), Per-person check-in reminders (toggle), Birthday reminders (toggle + days-in-advance), Follow-up gentle nudge (toggle + hours-after-meeting). Each can be independently disabled, keeping a user from turning off all notifications to silence one type.
recommendations.userValue: Reduces notification fatigue by giving users fine-grained control. Users who skip onboarding notification auth, or who find one notification type annoying, won't lose the entire engagement loop. Also educates users on what notifications are available.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-12 (prior plan); foundational UX. Prerequisite for shipping multiple notification types without user friction.

recommendations.title: Re-engagement interstitial after 7+ day absence
recommendations.description: Track AppSettings.lastOpenedAt (write on every cold launch in MainWindow.onAppear). If Date() - lastOpenedAt > 7 days, show a dismissible banner at the top of TodayView: 'Welcome back — here's what's been waiting.' Banner body: count of unprocessed meetings, overdue action items, and people in the StayConnectedSection overflow (who've become overdue). One-tap CTA to each area. Auto-dismiss after 10 seconds or on scroll. No modal, no interrupt — contextual and respectful.
recommendations.userValue: Catch returning users at the moment they open the app with the highest-signal items from their gap. Turns a blank Today into 'oh, I have things waiting' → immediate re-engagement.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-3 (prior plans); low-cost, high-signal return moment.

recommendations.title: MCP tools for relationship health and habit-loop diagnostics
recommendations.description: Extend the 17-tool MCP server (MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift) with three tools: (1) `get_relationship_health(person_id)` returns last interaction, cadence, days overdue, encounter count by type (90d), current streak (weeks), best streak; (2) `list_drifting_contacts(limit, relationship_type_filter)` returns people in StayConnectedSection.overdueRelationships with their overdue counts; (3) `get_check_in_history(person_id, days_back, kind_filter)` returns encounter log with dates, kinds, notes. These transform Claude from 'person information lookup' to 'relationship coach that can reason: who am I neglecting, how is X trending, what should I prioritize this week?'
recommendations.userValue: Unlocks agent-as-relationship-coach — Claude can answer 'give me a summary of my neglected relationships' or 'draft a thoughtful message to [person] given our history.' Turns the vault from read-only to conversational.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-10 (prior plan); thin wrappers over existing PeopleStore methods. Directly enables the compounding-value loop to surface via Claude.

recommendations.title: "Ask your vault" — local on-device RAG across all meetings
recommendations.description: Wire the existing FTS5 BM25 search engine (SecondBrainDB.swift:269, currently unused in favor of in-memory contains() — WorkspaceIndex.swift:106) and add a chat-like 'Ask your vault' surface on the chat panel. When user asks a natural-language question (e.g., 'What did we decide about the API redesign?'), retrieve top-N matching transcripts via FTS5 + optional semantic re-rank (if embeddings are available from backfillEmbeddingsIfNeeded), then prompt Ollama with the question + retrieved context to synthesize an answer with citations ('In the [Meeting Title] on [Date], you decided...'). This is the 2026 table-stakes feature (Otter, Fathom, Granola all ship this) — and only MeetingScribe can ship 100% on-device.
recommendations.userValue: Converts the captured corpus into actionable memory. Users don't re-read transcripts; they ask questions. Single largest trust differentiator: fully local, no vendor access to the vault.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From C1-1, C2-2, C4-1, P1-7 — four independent agents all converged on this as the moat. Already identified in MASTER_PLAN_V4 Phase 2. Implementation glue already exists (FTS5, Ollama, deep context from transcripts).

recommendations.title: Per-person check-in reminder notifications with snooze + quick-log affordance
