recommendations.description: RelationshipNotificationManager.swift already scaffolds this (lines 1–46 register the category, lines 48–117 syncPersonReminders). It fires notifications when lastInteractionAt + effectiveCheckInDays is overdue. Gaps: (1) snooze action missing from the notification (only 'Log now' exists) — add a 'Snooze 3 days' UNNotificationAction; (2) quick-log deep-link (meetingscribe://person/<id>/quickLog) not wired in AppDelegate / MainWindow onOpenURL; (3) notification not called on launch or when PeopleStore changes. Wire syncPersonReminders calls into MeetingManager (call on launch + whenever a person is updated). Ensure the deep-link route opens QuickEncounterSheet directly.
recommendations.userValue: Highest-priority retention item per P2-1. Every other engagement surface is passive (only works if user opens app); this is the only mechanism that pulls lapsed users back on behalf of their relationships. P2-1 identified it as the flagship habit loop.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Heavy scaffolding exists in RelationshipNotificationManager (lines 48–117); this is wiring it end-to-end. From P2-1 (prior plans).

recommendations.title: Menu bar summary line: today's glance + weekly rollup
recommendations.description: MenuBarView.swift already shows upcoming meetings. Add two context lines: (1) 'Today: 2 tasks, 1 follow-up pending, 1 person overdue' (sampled from NeedsAttentionWidget, FollowUpStatus, StayConnectedSection); (2) 'This week: 5 meetings, 8 tasks done' (aggregated from ActionItemStore.recentlyCompletedCount + calendar.meetings.count for the week). The menu bar is the always-visible surface a closed-window user sees most — ambient re-engagement without opening the main window.
recommendations.userValue: Re-engagement via glance. Even if a user doesn't open the app, they see evidence of accumulated value (meetings, tasks closed) — reinforcement that opens the app.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-9 (prior plan); depends on recap logic (P2-3) for the data.

recommendations.title: Auto-bump lastInteractionAt from calendar attendance + message history
recommendations.description: Currently lastInteractionAt is only bumped via manual Encounter logging (PeopleStore.recordEncounter line 449–450). This breaks trust in the 'you haven't talked to X in 30 days' nudge — a manager who has a weekly 1:1 with someone but never manually logs it sees a false-positive drift alert. Gap: (1) when a meeting finalizes with extracted attendees, bump lastInteractionAt for each person (add a call in MeetingManager.finalize after PersonExtractionController runs); (2) integrate message history from get_person_messages MCP tool — during daily backfill, scan the most recent message date per person and bump lastInteractionAt if it's newer than the current value. This makes the signal truthful.
recommendations.userValue: Restores trust in the stay-in-touch nudge. Users who meet regularly but don't log encounters see accurate cadence, not crying-wolf alerts. Foundation for all relationship-health features.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-1 (prior plan); critical correctness issue. Currently a blocker on the compounding-value loop.

recommendations.title: Encounter heat map + goal-setting on person detail
recommendations.description: Design (D4-6, P2-4, P2-8 converge) calls for a 13-week contribution-style grid on PersonDetailView above the encounters list — one cell = one week, color intensity = encounter count that week (max 3 = full saturation). Show 'Current streak: N weeks' + 'Best streak: M weeks' below. Add a goal-setting UI row in PersonDetailView: 'I want to check in [weekly / every 2 weeks / monthly]' — stores as checkInGoalDays on Person. Heat map renders the goal as a ghost outline so users see intention vs. behavior. Implementation: LazyHGrid of 91 RoundedRectangles, data from PeopleStore.encounters(for:), no third-party library. This is the habit-formation visualization that makes consistency visible — the intrinsic-motivation loop.
recommendations.userValue: Behavioral reinforcement: setting a goal + seeing your streak makes the relationship a conscious practice, not a passive CRM list. Users who set goals with their closest people are significantly more likely to follow through than those relying on inferred cadences alone.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-4 + P2-8 (prior plan); doubly validated. Pure view code on top of existing data.

recommendations.title: Decision & Commitment Ledger — structured cross-meeting memory
recommendations.description: P1-1 + C1-11 + C2-8 converge: extract a queryable 'commitment ledger' from transcripts. For each meeting, the extraction pipeline (PersonExtractionController, ActionItemBackfillController) already identifies action items; extend it to surface commitments in a structured form: 'Who owes what, by when' — e.g., 'Tyler owes Sarah the API spec by June 15' (from transcript 'I'll get you the spec by Friday'). Store as a Commitment entity (meetingID, actor, recipient, commitment_text, due_date, status) in the SecondBrainStore. Surface a dedicated 'Commitments' tab on the person detail (not mixed with action items) showing 'Owed to [person]' vs. '[Person] owes you.' This is the clearest differentiator vs. competitors: a CRM that doesn't just track meetings but tracks mutual obligation across them.
recommendations.userValue: Turns meeting notes into a relationship contract. Users can answer 'what did I promise Sarah' and 'what does Marcus owe me' without re-reading transcripts. Uniquely defensible feature.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P1-1, C1-11, C2-8 (prior plans); 4-way convergence. Requires extraction + storage; UI is straightforward.

recommendations.title: Streaks + consistency visualization in daily recap
recommendations.description: Extend the daily/weekly recap (P2-3) with a subtle consistency metric: 'Recorded meetings 4 days running' + 'Reviewed all action items 3 weeks straight.' Tie it to behaviors that produce value (reviewing tasks, sending follow-ups, logging check-ins with relationships), not raw app-opens. Render as a muted badge in the recap card and the menu-bar summary. No gamification — frame it as 'you've built a habit, keep it going.' Implementation: compute streaks from ActionItem.completedAt dates + Meeting.recordedAt dates within the recap period, check for gaps < 1 day.
recommendations.userValue: Loss-aversion nudge to maintain the habit. Cheap to build on recap logic. Psychological reinforcement without guilt-tripping.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From P2-8 (prior plan); depends on recap ritual (P2-3). Pure computed property on existing data.

recommendations.title: Verify & wire per-person check-in cadence defaults by relationship type
recommendations.description: Person.swift (lines 77–87) defines relationshipType.defaultCheckInDays: partner=1, family=7, closeFriend=14, friend=21, colleague=30, acquaintance=60. RelationshipNotificationManager.syncPersonReminders (line 75) correctly uses effectiveCheckInDays. But: (1) UI for setting/editing relationshipType on Person only lives in PersonDetailView (needs verification that it's wired in the two-column detail pane); (2) new people extracted from meetings have relationshipType = .unset by default (PersonExtractionController doesn't infer it) — so they never trigger notifications. When user sets relationshipType, immediately call RelationshipNotificationManager.syncPersonReminders to schedule. Ensure extracted people have a path to infer relationship type from context (colleague if extracted from a work domain, etc.) or at least prompt the user on first check-in-reminder trigger.
recommendations.userValue: Ensures the relationship-type defaults actually drive check-in cadence. Currently the feature is half-wired: relationship types exist but don't auto-drive notification frequency because extracted people stay .unset.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Completion of existing feature (D4-7 + P2-1 prerequisite). Data layer exists; UI wiring needs verification.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe has strong diagnostics plumbing (ErrorReporter, MetricsStore, HealthCheckSheet) but lacks the measurement infrastructure to answer "are we hitting our KPIs?" and "is the local LLM actually getting better?". The app is feature-rich and privacy-first; the gap is measurement and value reflection—analytics that stay local and show users and developers concrete signal on core workflows, quality, and reliability.

recommendations.title: Complete P5-1 cold-launch instrumentation: frame 0 → usable UI timing
recommendations.description: Wire startup timing into MetricsStore so V2 KPI #1 (cold launch < 1s) becomes measurable. Timestamp app init in MeetingScribeApp.body, record frame-paint via SwiftUI paint tracking, and compute delta. Store in MetricsStore as 'coldLaunchMs'. Add a lightweight Diagnostics panel showing cold-launch trend over last 20 launches. This directly closes PC-7 from V5 plan.
recommendations.userValue: Developer can validate startup optimizations (launch-snapshot caching, off-main initialization) actually improve the headline metric users care about. User sees evidence the app is snappy on their Mac.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Direct implementation of PC-7 from MASTER_PLAN_V5; pushes planned but unbuilt perf KPI into measurable form.
recommendations.dependencies: MetricsStore already exists; AppSettings.collectMetrics gating already in place.

recommendations.title: Real-Time Factor (RTF) dashboard for transcription lag (P5-2)
recommendations.description: Add a Diagnostics panel showing rolling transcription performance: parse TranscriptionLog (Sources/MeetingScribe/Transcription/TranscriptionLog.swift) to compute audio-seconds ÷ wall-seconds (RTF), split by LiveTranscriber vs final-pass vs QuickTranscribe. Show RTF trend + current, target line at RTF ≥ 1.0 (real-time is 1.0, faster is >1). Store RTF samples in MetricsStore so the V2 KPI #3 (transcription lag < 30s/min = RTF > 2.0) becomes dashboarded and trendable.
recommendations.userValue: Power users see if their GPU/Flash-Attention settings actually help; developer gets real perf data to decide model/inference optimizations. Currently this signal exists in logs but is write-only forensics.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Surfaces P5-2 from prior plan; turns forensic TranscriptionLog data into actionable UI + metrics.
recommendations.dependencies: TranscriptionLog parser already accessible; MetricsStore ready.

recommendations.title: Aggregate reliability dashboard: 30/90-day health trends (P5-4)
recommendations.description: In Settings/Diagnostics, add a 'Recording health' card showing aggregated MeetingHealthDTO stats across last 30/90 days: counts of ok/partial/noTranscript/fallbackUsed + most-common warning patterns (e.g., 'system audio silent 6×'). This surfaces trends the per-meeting badge (MeetingHealthBadge.swift) can't—e.g., a permission issue affecting 6 recordings becomes diagnosable instead of ignored one-offs. Use the existing health data (already persisted per meeting in Meeting.health) and compute aggregates on view load.
recommendations.userValue: User sees 'your setup is mostly healthy, but screen recording failed 3× — check Settings > Privacy > Screen Recording' instead of mysterious single badges. Converts support loops into self-service.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Direct implementation of P5-4; aggregates signals already in VaultKit/SharedModels.swift:64.
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingHealthDTO already persisted; just needs aggregation + UI.

recommendations.title: Count recovered crashes as crashes (closes V2 KPI #4)
recommendations.description: The crash-recovery path (AudioRecovery.meetingsWithInterruptedRecordings, MeetingManager.swift:502) detects interrupted recordings on launch — that IS a crash signal. Increment a durable crash counter in MetricsStore when recovery path runs; log active-recording-hours so V2 KPI #4 (crashes/hour active) becomes measurable. Add optional ultra-minimal crash breadcrumb (last ErrorReporter category at time of crash) written on recovery path. Store crash history in MetricsStore (count + timestamp) and surface in Diagnostics as 'Reliability / Crashes recovered in last 30 days: N'.
recommendations.userValue: Honest crash rate for developer enables monitoring reliability regressions. User sees 'MeetingScribe recovered N interrupted recordings' instead of silent loss.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Closes P5-5 from prior plan; makes the crash-detection signal quantifiable.
recommendations.dependencies: AudioRecovery path already exists; MetricsStore ready for counters.

recommendations.title: Action-item extraction quality proxy: yield tracking (P5-9)
recommendations.description: Wire ActionItemStore edit/delete signals: for each meeting, track (AI-extracted count) vs (user edited/deleted/added count) to compute extraction precision. Store per-meeting as 'extractionYield' in MetricsStore. On summary regenerate with a new LLM prompt, surface the yield before/after so prompt changes can be A/B'd locally without telemetry. Display in TaskInsightsView as 'Avg extraction accuracy: 78% (deleted 22% of AI items)' so users get intuitive feedback on quality.
recommendations.userValue: If a prompt change makes extraction worse, developer sees it immediately in the dashboard. User sees transparent signal: 'AI got this right 78% of the time on your meetings.'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Implements P5-9; pairs with P5-3 (summary feedback) to close both quality-loop gaps.
recommendations.dependencies: ActionItemStore edit/delete/add signals already exist; MetricsStore aggregation required.

recommendations.title: 'Your Year in Meetings' local value-reflection view (P5-7)
recommendations.description: A personal reflection view (accessible from Today or the dashboard) built entirely from the vault: total meetings recorded, hours captured, words transcribed, top 10 people by meeting-time, busiest day/week, action-items created vs completed, longest 'stay-in-touch' gap closed, relationship graph size. No network, 100% local aggregation. Render as a scrollable card deck or single infographic. Add 'Share as screenshot' so users can optionally share their year (no analytics, just local generation → screenshot).
recommendations.userValue: Retention hook: turns the uniquely rich local dataset (the moat) into something users *feel*. A shareable moment ('I had 87 meetings and connected with 42 people') that respects privacy. Drives engagement on a slow Monday.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Implements P5-7; pairs with TaskInsightsView (existing) to give users concrete value reflection across the whole app.
recommendations.dependencies: Vault data already aggregatable; needs simple views + aggregation queries on MeetingStore, PeopleStore.

recommendations.title: Durable 'Recent Issues' list: persist ErrorReporter and surface in Settings (P5-10)
recommendations.description: Today ErrorReporter.recent is in-memory only (Diagnostics/ErrorReporter.swift:28), lost on quit. Persist the last-100 ring buffer to disk as append-only log (ndjson, like DiagnosticsExporter), add a 'Recent issues' list in Settings that shows category/message/time with 'copy' and 'export-bundle' actions inline. This makes error history durable and human-readable without grepping app.log. Errors persisted are redacted per DiagnosticsExporter allowlist.
recommendations.userValue: User can see what's been failing without opening a text file. Lowers the bar to filing a good bug report ('I've had 3 summary generation failures in the last hour').
recommendations.noveltyNote: Implements P5-10; closes the gap where errors are logged but not discoverable by the user.
recommendations.dependencies: ErrorReporter and AppLog infrastructure already exist; just needs disk persistence + UI.

recommendations.title: Define north-star metric and tracking framework
recommendations.description: The app has four V2 KPIs (cold launch, start latency, transcription lag, crash rate) but no north-star metric. Propose: 'Capture rate: % of user's daily meetings that are recorded and have a usable summary.' This is both a quality indicator (summaries must be >70% complete to count) and an engagement indicator. Instrument: on app launch, count all user's calendar meetings yesterday; count all meetings recorded + summarized. Compute ratio. Store in MetricsStore weekly. A user with a 60% capture rate will likely return; one with 20% won't. This metric also guides the feature roadmap (better calendar integration → higher capture; faster transcription → fewer missed meetings).
recommendations.userValue: Developer has a single metric to optimize around. User sees 'you captured 14 of 18 meetings this week' which motivates recording the remaining 4.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: prior plans defined KPIs but not a north-star. This is the strategic anchor metric.
recommendations.dependencies: EventKit calendar access already present; MetricsStore aggregation ready.

recommendations.title: Local A/B testing harness for prompt/model variations
recommendations.description: Add a Settings panel 'Experimental Settings' where the user (or dev) can toggle between 2–3 LLM prompt variants (e.g., 'Summary style: Bulleted vs Prose vs Executive') or model snapshots (e.g., 'Local LLM: llama3.1:8b vs llama2:7b'). On summary regenerate, record which variant was used + the SummaryFeedback rating. After 5 regenerates per variant, display a simple card: 'Bulleted summaries got 👍 4/5 times; Prose got 👍 2/5.' No network, pure local comparison. This lets power users and the dev validate prompt changes in real meetings without ever touching telemetry.
recommendations.userValue: Developer can confidently ship a prompt change because they tested it locally on real user data and saw quality improve. User can tune the app to their preference.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: enables local experimentation on the most variable part of the system (LLM output), closing the quality-loop gap completely.
recommendations.dependencies: SummaryFeedback (P5-3) and TaskInsightsView pattern already exist; needs variant picker + aggregation.

recommendations.title: Transcription confidence surface: de-emphasize low-confidence spans (P5-8)
recommendations.description: Parse whisper.cpp's per-segment no_speech_prob / avg logprob from JSON output (WhisperRunner.swift already invokes this; currently discarded). Fold into MeetingHealthDTO as a confidence score. Visually de-emphasize (gray out, reduce opacity) low-confidence transcript spans so users know which parts to re-listen to. In Diagnostics, add trend: 'Last 30 recordings: X% high-confidence, Y% medium, Z% low'—this is a real accuracy proxy without any ground-truth annotation.
recommendations.userValue: User can trust the transcript at a glance ('this part is accurate; that one I should double-check') and knows which segments to re-listen to. Developer sees low-confidence trend as a signal the mic/audio setup is degrading.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Implements P5-8; surfaces confidence signal already computed by whisper but thrown away.
recommendations.dependencies: WhisperRunner parsing + MeetingHealthDTO extension; TranscriptView rendering changes.

recommendations.title: Meeting-to-outcome funnel: record → transcribe → summary → action (core funnel instrumentation)
recommendations.description: Instrument the core workflow as a funnel in MetricsStore: count meetings started / stopped / with final transcript / with summary / with ≥1 action item. This tells the developer whether the bottleneck is recording attrition, transcription failures, summary generation failures, or action extraction. Store as counters; render in Diagnostics/Insights as a waterfall: 'Started: 47 → Stopped: 45 → Transcript: 43 → Summary: 41 → Actions: 38.' Pair with ErrorReporter to tag which stage failed for each lost meeting. This is the core business funnel.
recommendations.userValue: Indirect: developer can identify and fix the stage where most value is lost. User indirectly benefits from faster debugging of 'why aren't my summaries being generated?'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: no prior plan instrumented the core workflow as a funnel; this is critical PM data.
recommendations.dependencies: MetricsStore + ErrorReporter already in place; needs instrumentation points in MeetingManager, MeetingPipelineController.

recommendations.title: Privacy-preserving opt-in aggregate metrics export (P5-11)
recommendations.description: Extend DiagnosticsExporter (Diagnostics/DiagnosticsExporter.swift) with an optional metrics-aggregate.json: counts and percentiles only (no titles, names, transcript content). User can choose to attach to a bug report or GitHub issue. Reuses existing allowlist/redaction discipline. Include: meetings/month, transcription RTF, summary feedback ratio, action-item completion rate, health status breakdown, crash rate. This is how developer gets opt-in, user-initiated field data without building a telemetry pipeline.
recommendations.userValue: Lets engaged users help improve the app on their terms; preserves the 'nothing leaves automatically' promise exactly. Engaged beta testers can say 'my setup has RTF=1.8' when reporting a lag issue.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Implements P5-11; extends existing DiagnosticsExporter pattern to metrics.
recommendations.dependencies: DiagnosticsExporter structure already present; MetricsStore data ready to aggregate.

recommendations.title: People engagement funnel: added → encountered → stay-in-touch reminder actioned
recommendations.description: Instrument the People CRM as a funnel (parallel to the meeting funnel): count people added to vault / with ≥1 meeting / with ≥1 encounter / with stay-in-touch reminders sent. Store in MetricsStore; render in Diagnostics. Pair with SuggestedPeopleView logic (PeopleListView.swift cadence) to flag people overdue for outreach and measure if 'stay in touch' nudges drive re-engagement. This tells the developer whether the relationship-graph moat is actually *engaged* (people are being maintained, not just recorded).
recommendations.userValue: Indirect: developer knows if the people CRM is actually driving behavior change. User indirectly benefits as the app gets smarter about who needs attention.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: no prior plan instrumented people lifecycle as a funnel; critical for understanding the moat's actual value.
recommendations.dependencies: PeopleStore + SuggestedPeopleView already exist; needs encounter logging + stay-in-touch event tracking.

recommendations.title: Extended SummaryFeedback: capture true/false intent reasons (expand P5-3)
recommendations.description: SummaryFeedback (AI/SummaryFeedback.swift) currently captures why on 👎 only. Expand to capture reasons on both 👍 and 👎: 'thorough', 'concise', 'missed_items', 'hallucinated', 'wrong_attendees', 'too_detailed', 'too_vague'. Store reason multiset per meeting. On regenerate, use reason distribution to steer the prompt: 'Last summary was 👎 for missed_items and hallucinated (2×); regenerate with emphasis on accuracy.' This closes the quality-loop: explicit user feedback + prompt steering + measurable improvement.
recommendations.userValue: Summary quality improves the more user corrects it—the LLM learns user's preferences locally. User sees 'this style of summary works best for your meetings'.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends P5-3 (already partially built) to be a true quality loop with bidirectional feedback.
recommendations.dependencies: SummaryFeedback already wired; needs UI for reason selection + prompt template steering.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe is a 269-file, 59K-LOC macOS app with critical architectural debt: a 25-file duplication between the app and daemon targets (already drifting in 12 files including the core MeetingPipelineController), inverted dependencies via 323+ global singleton accesses, a 1,894-line god-file MCP server with duplicated iMessage parsing, and a two-binary split that is scaffolding without substance—the daemon code is never run in production while the duplication cost is fully paid. Three foundational items unlock the entire roadmap: constructor injection (no seam to test recording logic today), the unimplemented SecondBrainStore protocol (orphaned scaffolding), and extraction of the Audio/Transcription stack into a shared CaptureKit library before Phase 2 daemon activation diverges the two copies further.

recommendations.title: E1-1 — Introduce a Services domain layer with constructor injection + protocol seams
recommendations.description: Define protocol-fronted services (SettingsReading, TranscriptionService, SummarizationService, PeopleReading, MeetingStoring) in VaultKit. Have concrete implementations conform. Inject through MeetingManager/MeetingPipelineController initializers with default args to keep call-sites terse. Kills 323+ .shared singleton accesses and opens a unit-test seam around the recording pipeline that does not exist today.
recommendations.userValue: Unblocks deterministic unit tests for recording/finalize/extraction logic that currently has zero test coverage; enables daemon and app to share one implementation with target-specific injections (kills CompatShim).
recommendations.noveltyNote: Echoes audit_eng.md E1-1, strongly endorsed. This is the highest-leverage net-new unlock—without injection there is no seam to test, no way to unify code across binaries, and Phase 2 will compound the divergence.
recommendations.dependencies: None; enables E1-2, E1-5, ARCH-1 extraction.
recommendations.risks: Migrating 300+ call-sites requires discipline; default args and smart refactoring keep noise low. Pair with E1-6 (dependency policy guard) to prevent re-coupling.

recommendations.title: E1-2 — Implement VaultFileStore and unify app + MCP persistence logic
recommendations.description: VaultKit.SecondBrainStore is a protocol with only an in-memory conformer; the production stores (PeopleStore, MeetingStore) do not conform to it. Implement VaultFileStore: SecondBrainStore (NSFileCoordinator-backed) in VaultKit. Route both app and MCP through it instead of MCP's 50 free-function disk readers. One file-format owner, one path-resolution policy, one place to fix corruption bugs.
recommendations.userValue: Eliminates the class of silent bugs where app and MCP server disagree about what's on disk. Today the promise of 'identical numbers' is maintained by hand.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Echoes audit_eng.md E1-2, strongly endorsed. Turns VaultKit from a DTO bag into a real shared domain layer.
recommendations.dependencies: E1-1 (protocol seam); pairs with E1-9 (unify tool dispatch).
recommendations.risks: NSFileCoordinator introduces new concurrency; test thoroughly on iCloud-synced vaults.

recommendations.title: ARCH-1 — Extract CaptureKit library: retire app↔daemon duplication before divergence worsens
recommendations.description: 25 files are physically duplicated between Sources/MeetingScribe/{Audio,Transcription,Detection,AI} and Sources/ScribeCore/. Audit_eng.md now shows 12 files have diverged (WhisperRunner 85 diff lines, MeetingPipelineController 135, NotificationManager 83, LiveTranscriber 37, OllamaService 25, AudioRecorder 11, others). Create CaptureKit library target in Package.swift; move the 22 byte-identical files there; abstract the 2 diverged files (AudioRecorder via DisplaySleepObserver protocol; NotificationManager via isPrimaryPoster flag); have both app and daemon depend on CaptureKit.
recommendations.userValue: Every recording/transcription fix lands once instead of twice. Prevents ENG-A live-transcript repair gate and ENG-D model-checksum logic from diverging across binaries. Eliminates 25-file maintenance hazard.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From REMAINING_WORK.md §1, validated and escalated by audit_eng.md—the divergence is now real (12/25 files), not hypothetical. This was 'byte-identical' in REMAINING_WORK but is already mid-drift.
recommendations.dependencies: E1-1 (dependency injection seam so shared code can read target-specific settings). Enables and gates Phase 2.
recommendations.risks: Moving code across targets risks a build breakage; verify build after each step. The CompatShim abstraction for logging may be deleted post-E1-1 instead of carried into CaptureKit.

recommendations.title: E1-7 — Define a typed, versioned ScribeBridge IPC contract (file-command + future XPC envelope)
recommendations.description: Today VaultCommandWatcher polls JSON files with an unversioned struct, and ScribeCoreXPC.swift defines a separate protocol (never wired). When Phase 2 switches to NSXPCConnection, the two definitions will drift. Define one Codable, versioned command/response envelope in VaultKit (e.g., ScribeBridgeMessage{version, command: Recording.Start{...}, response: ...}). File bridge serializes to JSON; XPC passes as Data. ENG-A's liveDroppedChunks/liveCoverageSeconds become typed fields, not ad-hoc plumbing.
recommendations.userValue: Phase-2 XPC cutover becomes a transport swap, not a re-implementation. ENG-A repair gate can't silently regress in daemon path. Versioning lets the daemon and app run different builds without IPC breakage.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-7, strongly endorsed. REMAINING_WORK.md §2.1 warns 'liveDroppedChunks must cross boundary or gate silently regresses'—this envelope is the answer.
recommendations.dependencies: ARCH-1 (defines the services being commanded). Gates Phase 2 safely.
recommendations.risks: Versioning adds complexity; keep the envelope flat (no nested discriminated unions) to minimize decode hazards.

recommendations.title: E1-3 — Extract iMessage attributedBody parser as a reusable VaultKit function
recommendations.description: The hand-rolled NSArchiver typedstream byte-scanner is duplicated in Sources/MeetingScribe/People/MessagesAnalyzer.swift and MCP main.swift:583. It's the most brittle code in the product (depends on 0x2b marker positions, length-prefix byte heuristics). Move to one public Sendable function in VaultKit with a comprehensive test fixture suite. Both call-sites import it.
recommendations.userValue: A future macOS change to typedstream format gets fixed once with a test, not silently diverging across two binaries. Reduces the fragility surface.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-3, strongly endorsed. This is the single most hand-rolled, fragile piece of code in the product.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pairs naturally with E1-2 (same consolidation mindset).
recommendations.risks: Test fixtures must cover the observed 0x2b/length-prefix heuristics; use real iMessage data to validate.

recommendations.title: E1-9 — Unify chat and MCP tool dispatch behind a shared ChatToolHandler-style registry
recommendations.description: In-app chat has a clean ChatToolHandler protocol (5 domain handlers, ChatTools.swift:82). MCP server is 50 free functions in a 1,894-line god-file; adding a tool requires editing main.swift directly. Define a shared VaultTool protocol in VaultKit (name, schema, run async). Register the same handler structs in both in-app dispatcher and MCP server's tools/list/tools/call. New capability added once, appears in both surfaces.
recommendations.userValue: Closes the gap where a new agent capability must be mirrored into main.swift separately. Shrinks the god-file structurally.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-9, strongly endorsed. Shrinks MCP main.swift and kills the 'mirror the implementation' overhead.
recommendations.dependencies: E1-2 (tools need the store seam to read/write vault).
recommendations.risks: Tools that are chat-only or MCP-only will need conditional registration; keep this explicit via attributes.

recommendations.title: E1-5 — Migrate recording state to @Observable and decompose observation by concern
recommendations.description: 39 ObservableObject classes vs 5 @Observable—the codebase is overwhelmingly on legacy Combine. MeetingManager is observed by 17 views and re-publishes sub-controllers via ~25 forwarding shims (var transcribingMeetingIDs, lastCompletedMeetingDir, etc.). Migrate MeetingManager, MeetingPipelineController, LiveTranscriber to @Observable so SwiftUI tracks per-property dependencies. Delete forwarding shims; have views observe the sub-controller they actually use.
recommendations.userValue: Fewer view invalidations during recording → lower main-thread CPU and smoother live transcript. Directly complements the V2 timer-storm fix.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-5, strongly endorsed. Pairs with audit_2026-05 ENG-2 findings about keep-alive ZStack keeping all tab timers live.
recommendations.dependencies: ARCH-3 (decomposition) ideally first for clarity; can proceed in parallel.
recommendations.risks: SwiftUI's @Observable is Xcode 15.1+; verify minimum deployment. Incremental migration is safe (mix Combine + Observable).

recommendations.title: E1-6 — Define explicit module dependency policy + CI import-direction guard
recommendations.description: There is a CI dupe guard (scripts/check-cross-tree-dupes.sh) but nothing enforces dependency direction. Document the intended layering (VaultKit ← CaptureKit ← {App,Daemon,MCP}; UI never imported by services) and add a CI check (grep-based or SwiftPM plugin) that fails if Sources/VaultKit imports AppKit/SwiftUI or if a services file imports a view.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents architecture from silently re-coupling as features land. The duplication and orphan-target problems both grew because nothing guarded direction.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-6, strongly endorsed. New CI signal that catches the re-coupling mistake early.
recommendations.dependencies: ARCH-1 (defines the layers to enforce).
recommendations.risks: False positives if a service needs to call back to the app (use a callback protocol instead of direct import).

recommendations.title: E1-8 — Collapse dual people-search architecture behind one PeopleSearch service
recommendations.description: ARCHITECTURE.md documents two separate people-search paths intentionally divergent: FTS5 via PeopleStore.filteredPeople (People tab list), plain in-memory contains in WorkspaceIndex.search (⌘K), because FTS reproducibly dropped contacts. Wrap both behind a PeopleSearching protocol with one implementation using FTS5 with in-memory fallback as a single documented policy, used by both surfaces.
recommendations.userValue: 'Search finds the person' behaves identically everywhere. Known FTS staleness bug gets one owner instead of being worked around in two places.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-8, strongly endorsed. Unifies a known correctness gap.
recommendations.dependencies: E1-2 (store seam).
recommendations.risks: In-memory fallback adds latency for large contact lists; benchmark before shipping.

recommendations.title: E1-10 — Add .starting/.stopping transient recording states (close TOCTOU race)
recommendations.description: RecordingState is still idle/recording/transcribing/error (MeetingManager.swift:752)—no transient cases. V2 Bug 6 was specified but never landed. Add .starting and .stopping cases; guard startRecording/stopRecording synchronously before first await to claim the transition. This is state-machine integrity, not just a bug.
recommendations.userValue: No illegal half-started state from a fast double-tap or simultaneous commands from menu-bar + main-window. Real risk once daemon adds a third command source.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_eng.md E1-10, strongly endorsed. V2 plan promised this; audit_2026-05 notes it was never landed.
recommendations.dependencies: None; but compounds with Phase-2's extra command source.
recommendations.risks: Care needed in state-transition logging to avoid confusion; document the semantics clearly.

recommendations.title: E5-2 — End-to-end pipeline integration harness (golden-audio + Ollama)
recommendations.description: One XCTest drives the real pipeline headlessly: feed fixture WAV through MeetingPipelineController.finalize (with stubbed or live Ollama) → assert transcript.md, summary.md, meeting.json, FTS5 row all land correctly in temp vault. Inject liveDroppedChunks > 0 and assert batch repair actually triggers + recovers tail. Add daemon-path variant for Phase-2 XPC boundary.
recommendations.userValue: Guards 'I recorded an hour and got half a transcript'—the worst possible failure for a transcription app. Every current test is a leaf; the record→transcribe→repair→vault-write path is untested end-to-end.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_2026-05 E5-2, strongly endorsed. This test would have caught the live-transcript truncation bug (audit_2026-05 ENG-3 P0) before shipping.
recommendations.dependencies: E5-1 (golden fixtures); an Ollama stub.
recommendations.risks: Ollama dependency in CI—either mock it or use a containerized ephemeral instance.

recommendations.title: E5-1 — Golden-audio transcription regression suite
recommendations.description: Commit 3-5 short reference WAVs (synthetic TTS + CC-licensed speech, incl. one with overlap/silence) plus their expected transcript JSON as test fixtures. Mac-only test runs real bundled whisper-cli against them and asserts fuzzy match (normalized WER under threshold, exact ±200ms on timestamps). Pin against baseEnModelSHA256.
recommendations.userValue: Whisper subprocess is explicitly untested; a model bump or whisper.cpp upgrade can degrade quality silently. This test catches it.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From audit_2026-05 E5-1, strongly endorsed. The core value of MeetingScribe is transcription quality; it must be regression-tested.
recommendations.dependencies: Golden fixtures committed to repo; whisper-cli in CI (reuse scripts/build-whisper-cpu.sh).
recommendations.risks: WER threshold must be calibrated per model; expected JSON must be hand-verified as 'correct'.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe is fast in read paths (ThumbnailCache, MeetingBodyCache, MeetingStore index) but has critical unresolved cold-start, main-thread I/O, and memory leaks from the keep-alive ZStack tab design. The app also carries real data-loss bugs in the live-transcript truncation path and vault migration. Performance improvements cluster into three themes: (1) kill main-thread I/O and synchronous O(n) work during scroll/render, (2) make the app shell native and lazy so tabs teardown instead of leak, (3) persist launch snapshots for cache-first instant open.

recommendations.title: Replace opacity-ZStack tab shell with NavigationSplitView + lazy detail
recommendations.description: MainWindow.swift:83-110 keeps all visited tabs mounted in a ZStack and shows/hides via opacity. This defeats SwiftUI's lazy teardown — every visited tab's .task, .onReceive(Timer), and @StateObject stay alive forever. TranscriptSyncView.swift:120 fires Timer.publish(every: 0.25) unconditionally on every tab you visit. Replace the ZStack with NavigationSplitView { sidebar } detail: { currentTabView } so tabs are built once and deallocate when you navigate away. Measure: baseline profile shows active timer fires while detail is hidden (should become 0).
recommendations.userValue: Switching between 5+ tabs no longer accumulates runaway timers on the main thread; memory and battery life improve materially on long sessions.
recommendations.noveltyNote: This was flagged as ENG-1 P1 in the prior audit and confirmed still present. Needed before Phase 2 shell work.
recommendations.risks: Must preserve instant-switch feel (the ZStack was kept for that reason). Solution: use @SceneStorage to remember last tab + lazy build detail column so no UI hiccup.

recommendations.title: Fix live-transcript truncation (ENG-A batch repair gate)
recommendations.description: LiveTranscriber.swift:98-108 fires per-source Task.detached chains that are never awaited at stop. MeetingManager.stopRecording() calls liveTranscriber.renderMarkdown() synchronously, but in-flight chunks are still processing — the final 0-5 minutes are missing from the rendered transcript. MeetingPipelineController.finalize() has a batch fallback but only triggers when live is *empty* (:139-144); a truncated-but-nonempty transcript is persisted as-is. Fix: (1) Add LiveTranscriber.flush() async that awaits lastMicTask + lastSystemTask before renderMarkdown(). (2) Call it in stopRecording (no await there — make it fire-and-forget on a high-priority task). (3) Change the batch gate from liveIsEmpty to coverageSeconds < (recordedDuration - one-chunk), and always run batch if droppedChunkCount > 0. Verify with a 2-minute meeting: live coverage should reach ≥90% of recorded duration.
recommendations.userValue: Eliminates silent loss of the final minutes of every transcript — action items and summaries are now generated from complete transcripts.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Critical P0 data-loss bug that persists from prior audit; listed in REMAINING_WORK.md as ENG-A.
recommendations.risks: Testing must include a real recording → stop → finalize flow on the Mac; CI can't validate this. Add LiveTranscriberTests with a flush() test.

recommendations.title: Move SecondBrainDB open off the app launch thread
recommendations.description: PeopleStore.swift:shared (lazy static) opens `~/MeetingNotes/db.sqlite` via SQLite3 during the first access. If this first access happens in MeetingScribeApp.body during the view hierarchy build, it stalls the main thread. Move the open to PeopleStore.init() but defer it until a detached Task fires in startServices(). Measure: profile the cold app launch (no warm cache) with `os_signpost` or Instruments to confirm the sqlite3_open latency is eliminated from the critical path.
recommendations.userValue: App's first paint is 50-100ms faster on cold start (main thread is free to build the UI instead of waiting on disk I/O).
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 PC-2; confirmed as a hard-synchronous disk stall in ARCHITECTURE.md §boot sequence.
recommendations.risks: PeopleStore is a singleton @ObservedObject and is accessed from SwiftUI views on the main actor. Ensure the init() call is safe without the DB open; gate any immediate people queries until load completes.

recommendations.title: Implement launch-snapshot cache (cache-first instant open)
recommendations.description: Persist a tiny snapshot of the current app state (last 10 Today cards, top 20 meeting list rows, top 50 people, top 30 tasks) to a single VaultCache-managed JSON file. On app launch, synchronously render this snapshot while the real stores hydrate in the background. Same pattern as Linear/Superhuman. Flows: (1) On every quit or explicit cache-update, serialize a snapshot.json under AppSettings.storageDir. (2) In MeetingScribeApp.startServices(), before store hydration tasks, call the snapshot loader synchronously and stash the snapshot in @State. (3) Pass the snapshot to each tab's initialView so lists show cached rows while real data loads. (4) Once real data arrives, the snapshot is replaced and cache is updated on next shutdown. Measure: first-paint latency (should drop from 500ms to <100ms on a full vault).
recommendations.userValue: Users see the populated app instantly on every launch, even on cold start with a large vault (1000+ meetings/people). Perceived responsiveness is dramatically improved.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 PC-1 / CB-1 / TT-1. Foundational for all Phase 2+ work; listed as a convergence point across 7 independent agents in V5 MASTER_PLAN.
recommendations.risks: Snapshot must be updated safely: use VaultCache atomic write + version/TTL so a corrupt snapshot doesn't block the app. Stale snapshot can show deleted meetings briefly (okay, replaced on hydration). No security risk (snapshot is JSON, same data as on disk).

recommendations.title: Kill synchronous transcript reads in MeetingCard + move list filtering off main actor
recommendations.description: MeetingCard.swift:340 reads `String(contentsOf: transcript.md)` synchronously on every body eval to check if hasFile. This blocks scroll during rendering. MeetingBodyCache already memoizes transcripts; drive MeetingCard.hasFile from meeting.health.has(transcript) instead. Also, PeopleStore.filteredPeople(:) sorts via an O(n×encounters) comparator on the main actor every keystroke; move the sort/filter to a detached Task and publish results back. Both are scroll-jank vectors; removing main-thread I/O + O(n²) sort is the single biggest responsiveness win per V5 PR-1 / PS-1.
recommendations.userValue: Scrolling meeting/people lists is smooth at 60fps instead of a jank fest; typing names in the People search is instant instead of a stall.
recommendations.noveltyNote: PR-1/PS-1 in V5; flagged as #1 scroll-jank vector in ENG-2 P2 of prior audit_eng.md.
recommendations.risks: MeetingCard is used in TodayView + MeetingsView + every card render. Must verify hasFile works without reading the file (use meeting.health); cache should be pre-warmed on app start.

recommendations.title: Implement tri-state loadState (loading/loaded/empty) to kill false-empty flashes
recommendations.description: Detail views and lists show a momentary 'No summary' / 'No transcript' error message on cold open because they check the *presence* of the body text instead of whether it's been loaded. Replace with a tri-state enum loadState = { loading, loaded, empty }. Set loadState = .loading on appear, .loaded + body when MeetingBodyCache hydrates, .empty if the cache confirms it doesn't exist. Gate the error message to loadState == .empty only. This is a perceived-speed win even though load time is the same — the app feels less buggy. (V5 PP-1 / PC-4 / DI-1 / DV-4.)
recommendations.userValue: No more false 'No summary' flashes when you click a meeting; instead, a skeleton or blank canvas that progressively fills. Perceived quality is higher.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 Phase 1 skeleton work; confirmed in prior audit as affecting multiple detail tabs.
recommendations.risks: Must apply consistently to every detail tab (UnifiedMeetingDetail, PersonDetailView, ActionItemsProjectPage). Audit the detail VM pattern to make sure loadState is the single source of truth.

recommendations.title: Consolidate O(n) index lookups via [String: Person] and [String: MeetingTag]
recommendations.description: PeopleStore.swift:640-751 has 15+ `.firstIndex(where: { $0.id == X })` calls. These are O(n) searches on the main actor, many inside sort comparators that run every render/keystroke. Add two process-local dictionaries: [personID: Person] + [tagID: MeetingTag] so person(by:) and tag(by:) are O(1). Rebuild the dicts when the store mutates (add/delete/update). Similar pattern already used in MeetingStore for directory caching. Measure: People list sort (encountered count) goes from O(n²) to O(n log n).
recommendations.userValue: People list reflow on every keystroke is snappy; tag lookups in meeting renders don't cause scroll jank.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 PR-3 / TP-1; confirmed as O(n²) bottleneck in sort comparators.
recommendations.risks: Dicts must stay in sync with the [Person] array. Use a private let and ensure all mutations go through store methods, not direct array edits. Add a debug assertion that dict.count == people.count.

recommendations.title: Deduplicate MeetingScribe vs ScribeCore audio/transcription/AI stack via CaptureKit library
recommendations.description: The app and daemon both have 25+ identical or nearly-identical audio/transcription/AI files (Audio/*, Transcription/*, Detection/*, AI/OllamaService). They already drift — MeetingScribe uses AppLog/ErrorReporter while ScribeCore uses raw os.Logger. This duplicated code is a maintainability hazard and a performance risk: a bug in the live-transcript or whisper paths must be fixed in two places, and you can't tell if they're synced. Create a new CaptureKit library target that both app + daemon depend on. Move the 25 shared files there, abstract the 2-3 diverged files (AudioRecorder display-sleep logic, NotificationManager ownership) behind protocol boundaries. Audit in REMAINING_WORK.md (ARCH-1) confirms this is scoped and non-blocked.
recommendations.userValue: A single, authoritative implementation of audio capture and transcription. Bug fixes and performance tuning apply everywhere at once. Reduces binary size and build times.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From REMAINING_WORK.md ARCH-1; verified in prior ENG-4 P1 as the biggest maintainability hazard.
recommendations.risks: Must coordinate the two executables' builds. No app-side UI changes, only reorg. Smoke test: make app + record→stop→transcribe on Mac to confirm both paths still work.

recommendations.title: Upgrade MeetingBodyCache to persist across launches + add async disk-persisted thumbnail cache
recommendations.description: MeetingBodyCache (transcript/notes/summary) is memory-only and resets on relaunch. ThumbnailCache is memory-only and decodes async but doesn't persist. For a launch-snapshot world where you're showing 20 cached rows, the benefit evaporates if you have to re-decode 20 thumbnails. Persist both caches: (1) MeetingBodyCache as a compact .cache JSON under storageDir, keyed by meeting ID, with a 2-week TTL. (2) ThumbnailCache as resized .jpgs under storageDir/cache/thumbnails/, also with TTL. On load, verify cache integrity (manifest timestamp vs meeting.json mtime) and only use if younger than the source. This is where the cold-start snapshot world gets 10x better: 2nd app open shows rendered cards with images instantly, before stores hydrate.
recommendations.userValue: Second and subsequent app launches are near-instant; rendered card rows load with cached content before the real stores hydrate.
recommendations.noveltyNote: CB-2 / CB-4 in V5; DI-2 / PR-5 for thumbnail; part of the launch-snapshot foundation.
recommendations.risks: Must handle cache invalidation correctly: if meeting.json is updated on another device, the cache must be stale-detected and rebuilt. Use mtime matching. Byte-bound the cache to prevent runaway disk usage.

recommendations.title: Defer non-first-screen @StateObject construction and backfill tasks
recommendations.description: MainWindow.swift and each detail view immediately construct @StateObject view models and kick off heavy backfill tasks (embeddings, decisions, person-extraction) in their .onAppear. For a cold-start snapshot render, these should be deferred to a background priority task that fires *after* the first frame paints. Measure: first-paint should be <100ms; the background work (embeddings, full-text index rebuilds) continues in the 100-500ms window and doesn't block interactivity. Add a `@MainActor func deferredLoad()` method to each view model and call it from `.task(priority: .background)` instead of constructing @StateObject upfront.
recommendations.userValue: The app becomes interactive and responsive 200-300ms earlier on cold start; heavy compute (embeddings, indexing) happens in the background without blocking your clicks.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 TT-2 / PC-5 / PC-8 ("Defer heavy backfills"); related to the launch-snapshot pattern.
recommendations.risks: Views must gracefully handle the case where view model hasn't loaded yet (show a skeleton or use cached state). Ensure child views don't crash if the view model is nil or in a loading state.

recommendations.title: Complete vault migration: rewrite relativeFolderPath + unify layout target
recommendations.description: VaultMigrationManager.swift:79-89 moves meeting folders but never writes the new relativeFolderPath back to meeting.json. MeetingStore.desiredDirectory() still emits the old tag-grouped layout, so new meetings land in the old place. Post-migration, every directory lookup falls back to the O(N) tree walk (:128) — exactly the perf regression the cache was meant to avoid, re-incurred on first access of every migrated meeting. Fix: (1) After moveItem succeeds, read meeting.json, set relativeFolderPath to the new relative path, write it back (or call store.cacheResolvedPath). (2) Update desiredDirectory() to always emit meetings/yyyy/yyyy-MM/slug (the migration target) so new + retagged meetings match. (3) Don't re-move by tag in handleTagChange — tag is metadata now, not a path component. (4) Only set the completed flag if move count == discovered count; log and retry on next launch if some move fails. Verify: directory(for:) should resolve without a tree walk for any meeting (check instrumenting).
recommendations.userValue: Migrated vaults have O(1) directory lookups again; repeated meeting-detail clicks don't stall with a tree walk. The migration is actually completed instead of marked-complete-but-half-done.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From REMAINING_WORK.md and prior ENG-3 P0; confirmed still in code as a half-wired migration.
recommendations.risks: High risk of data loss if the move logic is wrong. Add comprehensive tests: seed a tag-layout vault, migrate, assert every meeting.json has a new relativeFolderPath, assert directory() resolves O(1).

recommendations.title: Add CI gate: swift build + swift test on every PR, not just at release
recommendations.description: .github/workflows/release.yml runs tests only on release tags. Add .github/workflows/ci.yml triggered on pull_request + push: [main] that runs `swift build -c release` + `swift test`. This catches non-compiling code and regressions before they land on main. Both live-transcript and vault-migration bugs would have been caught by focused unit tests, which would have fired on every PR. Also add `swift test --sanitize=thread` to catch concurrency bugs (the codebase has accumulated TSan warnings from system frameworks; flag our own violations early).
recommendations.userValue: Broken code can't land on main; concurrency bugs are caught before release; the test suite becomes a working safeguard instead of a post-facto check.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From prior ENG-5 P1; same issue persists (CI only runs on release tags).
recommendations.risks: CI might slow the merge feedback loop by 2-5 minutes. Parallelize if needed (swift build in one job, swift test in another).

recommendations.title: Byte-bound the core caches (MeetingBodyCache, ThumbnailCache, chat backlog) to prevent OOM
recommendations.description: MeetingBodyCache has no totalCostLimit; a user with 500 meetings can have 500 full transcripts (megabytes each) in memory at once. ThumbnailCache has a 48MB totalCostLimit (good), but chat conversation has no bound and can grow indefinitely if the user leaves the app running for days. Add: (1) MeetingBodyCache.totalCostLimit = 256MB (or adaptive based on system RAM). (2) ChatSession: cap messages to the last 200 (or ~20 turns) and evict older messages. (3) backlinks / cross-reference caches: similarly bounded. Measure: app memory should stay under 500MB even with a large vault open and a long chat conversation.
recommendations.userValue: The app doesn't mysteriously eat RAM and get backgrounded by the OS; users can keep the app open for days without degradation.
recommendations.noveltyNote: From V5 PS-5 / PS-2 / PS-6 ("Byte-bound the caches"); stability improvement tied to performance.
recommendations.risks: If you set the limit too low (e.g., 64MB), the cache thrashes and performance drops. Profile a typical user session and set the limit to 2-3× the observed steady-state.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe demonstrates solid reliability foundations (atomic writes, derived-index validation, content hashing) but has critical data-integrity gaps that will surface under load: SQLite migrations lack transactions (silent partial migrations on power loss); encounter deduplication has race conditions (double-writes on Return keypress); notification scheduling is latently broken (stale fire dates never update, birthday notifications permanently lost on person deletion); and the cross-device sync engine is well-designed but needs defensive guardrails. The app scales to 200+ meetings and 50+ people but these issues worsen at scale.

recommendations.title: Wrap SQLite migrations in explicit transactions to prevent partial-migration corruption
recommendations.description: SecondBrainDB.migrateToV2() and migrateToV3() execute bare ALTER TABLE and CREATE TABLE statements with no surrounding BEGIN/COMMIT. A power loss or crash mid-migration leaves the database in a partially-migrated state (e.g., new columns exist but schema_version unchanged). The code comment incorrectly claims 'SQLite ignores duplicate column errors'—errors are actually silently swallowed by exec() discarding the return value. Fix: wrap both functions with explicit `execChecked("BEGIN;")...execChecked("COMMIT;")...execChecked("ROLLBACK;")` patterns, mirroring the existing rebuild() implementation at lines 265–280. This ensures all-or-nothing semantics.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents corrupted databases after app crashes during first launch on a new macOS version; ensures clean upgrade paths.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E2-R1 from audit-2026-v2; critical guardrail for migration safety.

recommendations.title: Fix insertPerson() to write relationship_type and check_in_cadence_days to SQLite
recommendations.description: SecondBrainDB.insertPerson() (lines 298–305) constructs an INSERT statement that omits the v3 columns relationship_type and check_in_cadence_days entirely. Every rebuild() call and upsertPerson() overwrites these columns with the DEFAULT values ('unset', NULL) regardless of what Person.relationshipType actually contains. The canonical JSON files have the correct values; the derived SQLite index is permanently stale. Fix: extend the INSERT statement to include both columns bound from p.relationshipType.rawValue and p.checkInCadenceDays. This is a one-line SQL change that unfolds into a two-line bind call.
recommendations.userValue: Enables future relationship-type filtering by SQL queries (e.g., 'show only family') and ensures the derived index matches the canonical JSON, critical for data consistency.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E2-R2 from audit-2026-v2; silent data-divergence bug that defeats the v3 migration.

recommendations.title: Add isSaving guard to QuickEncounterSheet.saveIfValid() to prevent duplicate encounters
recommendations.description: QuickEncounterSheet has a double-save race: the TextField's .onSubmit { saveIfValid() } and the Save button's .keyboardShortcut(.return) both fire on a single Return keypress, creating two Encounter records in PeopleStore without any idempotency guard. Fix: add `@State private var isSaving = false`, guard saveIfValid() with `guard !isSaving else { return }`, set `isSaving = true` before writing, and reset after dismiss. This is a three-line fix in a high-frequency user action.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents silent duplicate encounter records that confuse the relationship history and invalidate check-in cadence calculations.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E1-N1 from audit-2026-v2; data-integrity bug in a critical user flow.

recommendations.title: Unconditionally remove-then-re-add notifications in scheduleCheckIn() to fix stale fire dates
recommendations.description: RelationshipNotificationManager.scheduleCheckIn() (lines 124–125) has a hard early-return guard that skips rescheduling if a notification with the same ID already exists. After a user logs an encounter, the cadence should push the next notification forward, but the pre-existing notification fires on the original date unchanged. The doc-comment claims idempotency, but this violates idempotency semantics (calling it twice produces the same stale outcome, not the same correct outcome). Fix: remove-then-re-add inside scheduleCheckIn—since it's only called from syncPersonReminders which owns the pending-requests array, the cost is minimal. Alternatively, compare the existing trigger's nextTriggerDate against the computed fireDate and re-add only when they differ by >30 minutes.
recommendations.userValue: Notifications reflect reality. Logging an encounter immediately reschedules the next notification to the correct future date instead of leaving a stale notification that fires as if nothing happened.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E4-1 from audit-2026-v2; central reliability promise of the check-in system.

recommendations.title: Cancel birthday and check-in notifications when a person is deleted
recommendations.description: PeopleStore.deletePerson() (line 514) removes the person from files and database but never cancels their pending notifications. person-birthday-<id> with repeats: true will fire every year indefinitely, showing a stale person name years after deletion. Fix: add a cleanup call before existing cleanup code that removes pending + delivered notifications with IDs: person-checkin-<id>, person-birthday-<id>, person-birthday-week-<id>.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents ghost notifications from deleted people appearing on their birthday every year—a confusing and embarrassing production defect.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E4-2 from audit-2026-v2; latent data-integrity issue that worsens over time.

recommendations.title: Decouple scheduleBirthdayReminders from check-in horizon guard so birthdays are always scheduled
recommendations.description: RelationshipNotificationManager.syncPersonReminders() (line 103) calls scheduleBirthdayReminders inside a `guard dueDate <= horizon else { continue }` block. Birthday reminders are only scheduled as a side effect of a check-in being due within 7 days. A person checked in recently (cadence=30 days) has their birthday notification scheduling skipped until they become overdue for a check-in. This silently disables birthday notifications for ~80% of people at any given time. Fix: move scheduleBirthdayReminders outside and after the horizon guard so it runs for all people with a birthday and relationship type, regardless of check-in urgency.
recommendations.userValue: Birthday notifications work reliably for all people, not just those who happen to be overdue for a check-in.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E4-3 from audit-2026-v2; architectural coupling bug that silently breaks a flagship feature.

recommendations.title: Add SQLite migration unit tests to catch schema-evolution regressions
recommendations.description: SecondBrainDB has zero migration tests. The file-layout migration (VaultMigrationManager) has 3 tests; the SQLite migration—which touches every user's database on upgrade—has none. Tests should cover: (1) fresh DB gets all schema versions, (2) v1→v3 direct upgrade without data loss, (3) v2→v3 idempotency, (4) after rebuild(), relationship_type column reflects Person.relationshipType.rawValue, (5) migration with disk-full / power-loss scenarios (transaction safety). New file: SecondBrainDBMigrationTests.swift using a temp-file DB harness.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents regressions as the schema evolves past v3; catches silent data-loss bugs before they reach users.
recommendations.dependencies: SecondBrainDB must be testable in isolation; currently @MainActor and @available(macOS 14.0+)—both compatible with XCTestCase.
recommendations.noveltyNote: E2-R3 from audit-2026-v2; essential regression guard for critical infrastructure.

recommendations.title: Add defensive path-containment checks to MeetingScribeSync merge engine to prevent directory traversal
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeSync.main.swift (lines 274–318) correctly uses insideVault() to validate all copy destinations before writing. However, the slug collision resolution in uniqueDestination() (lines 308–318) and the ImportedDeviceFolder namespacing both rely on suffix arithmetic without re-validating the final path. An edge case where an attacker control of _remote/<device>/ could craft nested symlinks or hardlinks to escape quarantine is unlikely given the single-host local-only design, but adding re-validation after final path resolution (before any file operation in apply mode) hardens the merge engine against future transport vulnerabilities (e.g., if an in-app sync client with network exposure is added). Fix: add final insideVault() check immediately before each fs operation (copy, write).
recommendations.userValue: Zero-day defense against path-traversal attacks if future transports expose _remote/ to untrusted sources.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new defensive hardening for the sync engine ahead of in-app HTTP transport (Report 2).

recommendations.title: Expose schema_version and migration state in diagnostics to aid user triage
recommendations.description: When users report 'no people showing' or blank People graph bugs, there is no way to tell remotely whether they are on SQLite v1/v2/v3 or whether a migration ran at last launch. Add SecondBrainDB.schemaVersion and a 'lastMigrationState' field (timestamp + version) to the app's diagnostic/about panel and any crash reports. This cuts triage time dramatically.
recommendations.userValue: Faster root-cause analysis and support response for data-access issues.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E2-R6 from audit-2026-v2; developer ergonomics for production support.

recommendations.title: Validate schemaVersion round-trip in SchemaEnvelope to prevent silent decode failures
recommendations.description: SchemaEnvelope.decode() (VaultKit/SchemaEnvelope.swift, lines 32–49) accepts payloads with ANY schemaVersion and silently runs the optional migrate: closure if provided. If a future phase makes a field required (e.g., relationshipType becomes non-optional), old person.json files with version=1 will decode to empty/default values without error. The envelope design includes a migrate: parameter to prevent this, but PersonStore.decode() never supplies it (line 396). Add a policy: SchemaEnvelope.decode() should emit an error or warning if `decodedVersion < currentVersion` and no migrate: closure is provided, preventing silent data loss. Document this in the API.
recommendations.userValue: Catches forward-compatibility bugs during development before they ship as silent data loss.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: E2-R4 from audit-2026-v2; proactive insurance against future schema additions.

recommendations.title: Add idempotency guard to DarwinNotifier to prevent double-fires in concurrent observers
recommendations.description: DarwinNotifier.observe() (lines 38–79) registers handlers in a global _handlers: [String: () -> Void] dictionary. The C callback looks up the handler by name and invokes it. If the same notification is posted twice in rapid succession (e.g., during rapid vault mutations), both posts will fire before any handler runs. More critically, if a handler triggers a write that posts the same notification (e.g., a UI update → PeopleStore.updatePerson() → vaultChanged → refreshUI), a recursive loop is possible. The current code does not guard against re-entrancy. Fix: add a Set<String> tracking in-flight notifications and skip duplicate posts within a window (e.g., 100ms debounce).
recommendations.userValue: Prevents cascading notification loops that could cause UI thrashing or data corruption under rapid edits.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new defensive fix for the Darwin notification pattern used throughout the app.

recommendations.title: Add atomic staging + validation to MeetingScribeSync before ledger commit to prevent partial ingests
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeSync.run() applies all ingest functions (meetings, people, tasks, tags) in sequence, updating the ledger piecemeal (lines 740–750). If ingestPeople succeeds but ingestTags fails halfway (e.g., disk-full on the live write), the ledger has already been updated for people, so a re-run will skip re-processing them. The merge is eventually consistent, but a re-run after fixing disk space needs to re-process all. This is tolerable (merge is idempotent), but for a production-quality sync system, use a staging ledger: write all ingests to a temporary .import-ledger.new, validate atomically, then rename into place. This makes all-or-nothing semantics explicit.
recommendations.userValue: Cleaner recovery semantics after ingestion failures; easier to reason about partial-sync recovery.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new hardening for production-grade sync. MeetingScribeSync is already well-designed; this is Polish.

recommendations.title: Add reserved-folder guard to MeetingStore.reservedFolders to exclude _remote and _imported-* namespaces
recommendations.description: MeetingStore.reservedFolders (line 38) currently excludes ["models", "QuickNotes", "logs", "diagnostics"]. The sync plan requires that the live app's scanner never treats _remote/ or _imported-<device>/ folders as meeting groupings. These namespaces are added by MeetingScribeSync but the app's meeting enumeration must skip them so imported/staged data doesn't pollute the live list until an explicit ingest. Fix: add "_remote", and "_imported-*" pattern matching to reservedFolders or a new excludePrefix pattern in enumerateMeetingDirectories().
recommendations.userValue: Prevents cross-device sync staging data from appearing in the live app before merging, essential for the quarantine model.
recommendations.dependencies: Phase 2 of cross-device sync plan (MeetingScribeSync integration).
recommendations.noveltyNote: Already documented in CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN.md §9.1 as a prerequisite; implementation missing.

recommendations.title: Add content-hash validation to MeetingStore index rebuild to detect on-disk corruption
recommendations.description: MeetingStore.scanDiskForMeetings() (line 547+) enumerates meeting directories and decodes meeting.json files, but silently skips files that fail to decode (no logged error, no cache invalidation). If a meeting.json is corrupted or truncated, the meeting vanishes from the index until a re-save touches it. Add: (1) try-catch around JSON decode with a logged warning, (2) checksum validation on meeting.json before decode (FNV-1a or SHA256 against .meeting-index.json checksums from prior run), (3) if checksum matches but decode fails, move the corrupted file to a quarantine folder so the user can recover it.
recommendations.userValue: Corrupted meeting files surface as warnings instead of silently vanishing; users can recover data without re-recording.
recommendations.dependencies: None
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new defensive hardening for data durability as the app scales to 200+ meetings.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe's local-first, privacy-by-design approach is well-founded with solid keychain usage, vault-containment guards, and TCC-aligned permission descriptions. However, critical gaps exist in secret handling (API keys in Claude config files), IPC validation (unencrypted command channel), iMessage access (Full Disk Access without audit logging), and MCP server input/output exposure (stdout leakage, missing auth/rate limits). The two-binary IPC strategy (Darwin notifications + file-based commands) is not encrypted and lacks integrity verification. Vault traversal guards exist but are incomplete across all write paths.

recommendations.title: Encrypt environment-variable secrets in Claude Desktop config
recommendations.description: MCPInstaller writes the Notion API key in plaintext to `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` (file at /Users/tyleryannes/MeetingScribeRefactor/Sources/MeetingScribe/MCP/MCPInstaller.swift:113). This is a shared config file readable by any process with file access. Migrate the NotionMCP key to the macOS Keychain instead: (1) Store `notionAPIKey` in KeychainStore (already exists as Account.notionAPIKey); (2) Have MCPInstaller write a placeholder or empty value in the config; (3) Before spawning NotionMCP, read from Keychain and pass via a more restricted channel (XPC + binary serialization, or a read-only temp file with 0600 perms); (4) Add a migration for existing plaintext keys (scan config, lift to keychain, blank out config).
recommendations.userValue: Users who sync vault across Macs expose API secrets to Time Machine backups, iCloud Keychain (if synced), and any tool that reads Claude Desktop config. Keychain is encrypted at rest and gated by user login session.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audits flagged Keychain migration (REMAINING_WORK.md) but this is specific to MCP config exposure, not the old UserDefaults issue. Keychain migration was completed for app settings; this extends it to MCP.
recommendations.risks: If keychain read fails at MCP startup, NotionMCP will silently drop the API key and fail on first use. Mitigation: fallback to checking process env as a safety valve, but log a warning.

recommendations.title: Validate and sign vault file commands (IPC integrity)
recommendations.description: VaultCommandWatcher (file at /Users/tyleryannes/MeetingScribeRefactor/Sources/ScribeCore/IPC/VaultCommandWatcher.swift) reads JSON commands from `_commands/` directory with zero validation beyond JSON decode. An attacker with write access to the vault can forge start/stop/transcribe commands. Add a simple integrity mechanism: (1) Commands are already dropped by the app; add a `signature` field (HMAC-SHA256 of command + requestID using a per-session key stored in Keychain); (2) VaultCommandWatcher verifies the signature before dispatching; (3) Re-verify on every command so a stale file can't be replayed. Alternatively, gate commands on file ownership (must be owned by the current user:group) and permissions (0600).
recommendations.userValue: If the vault lives in a shared network folder or iCloud Drive shared with collaborators, those users cannot forge recordings or steal transcriptions.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior audits. Specific to the Phase 2 IPC design (REMAINING_WORK.md) which introduced VaultCommandWatcher. New risk introduced by the two-binary split.
recommendations.risks: Adding signatures to every command adds latency (HMAC-SHA256 is fast but non-zero). If the key is lost (app reinstall), old commands can't be processed — but that's acceptable since they'd be stale anyway. Key derivation from user password is not recommended (too slow for every request); use a deterministic key in Keychain instead.

recommendations.title: Audit and restrict MCP server stdout exposure
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeMCP speaks JSON-RPC over stdout but may emit diagnostic logs or errors to stdout/stderr during initialization or on crash, exposing vault metadata, file paths, or error details to the parent Claude process. Audit every `print()`, `fputs()`, OS.Logger call in main.swift and the tool handlers. Restrict output to: (1) JSON-RPC response bodies only (via the standard `JSONValue` return type); (2) Errors wrapped in the tool response, never raw stderr; (3) No diagnostic logging to stdio — if logging is needed, route to a private `~/.MeetingScribe/mcp.log` with 0600 perms, and document that users must grant Full Disk Access to Claude if they want MCP to write logs.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents leaking vault paths, meeting IDs, and person names into Claude's process logs or any intermediary that intercepts stdout.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior audits. Specific to MCP server hygiene. Audit-2026-05 covered MCP correctness but not information leakage.
recommendations.risks: Loss of diagnostic visibility — MCP crashes or hangs will be harder to debug. Mitigation: write a `--debug` flag that routes logs to stderr only in local development, and clearly document that production MCPs should never log diagnostics to stdio.

recommendations.title: Add per-MCP-tool rate limiting and request authentication
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeMCP currently processes every incoming tool call with no authentication, rate limiting, or request tracing. If Claude Desktop or any MCP-aware client is compromised, an attacker can make unlimited tool calls (dump all meetings, read all iMessages, write arbitrary people). Add: (1) A `requestID` / correlation ID field in every tool response (already in place for commands, extend to tools); (2) Per-tool call counters in `~/.MeetingScribe/mcp-audit.log` (user ID, tool name, timestamp, arg signature for PII-filtering); (3) If more than N calls/minute on write tools (add_person, create_action_item), log a warning and consider a soft circuit-breaker (return 429). (4) Document that if Claude Desktop is ever compromised, users should immediately run `MeetingScribeMCP --revoke-all-connections` to invalidate any stale auth tokens (placeholder for a future mutable-auth system).
recommendations.userValue: Audit trail for forensics if the vault is ever compromised via the MCP interface. Rate limiting prevents accidental or malicious DoS loops.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior audits. MCP spec does not define auth or rate limiting; this is app-specific hardening.
recommendations.risks: Logging could create a forensic artifact that itself becomes sensitive. Mitigation: hash person/meeting IDs in the audit log, don't log full tool inputs, and secure the audit log with 0600 perms.

recommendations.title: Secure iMessage access with per-session consent and audit logging
recommendations.description: MessagesAnalyzer (file: /Users/tyleryannes/MeetingScribeRefactor/Sources/MeetingScribe/People/MessagesAnalyzer.swift) opens `~/Library/Messages/chat.db` read-only whenever a user views a person's iMessage stats. Full Disk Access permission is required but: (1) there is no per-session consent dialog ('you are about to read X's entire message history'); (2) no access log is kept (the app runs unattended in the background, so messages could be read without the user's immediate knowledge); (3) the MCP server (NotionMCP can be called by Claude Desktop) has the same access, so an external agent can query iMessages. Add: (1) A local-only audit log at `~/.MeetingScribe/imessage-access.log` recording every `analyze()` call with person ID, timestamp, accessed message count; (2) A one-time consent prompt when the user first enables iMessage stats in Settings (not per-query, but a clear onboarding gate); (3) A Settings page showing 'you accessed X messages from [person] on [date]' for the past 30 days; (4) Document in the privacy policy that iMessages are read locally and never transmitted, and that the user can revoke by removing Full Disk Access.
recommendations.userValue: Transparency: users see who/when they accessed iMessages, deterring accidental over-use (e.g., reading a partner's private chats unintentionally). Consent gate prevents sneaky background analysis.
recommendations.noveltyNote: ARCHITECTURE.md mentions iMessage is 'Phase C' and already implemented. This extends it with consent/audit. Not in prior audits.
recommendations.risks: Audit log itself is sensitive (reveals which people were queried). Mitigation: 0600 perms, clear data after 30 days, hash person IDs if external visibility is needed.

recommendations.title: Validate all vault write paths against directory traversal
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeMCP has `resolveInsideVault()` (line 36-42 in main.swift) guarding writes for the MCP server.
