However: (1) The app itself (MeetingManager, PeopleStore) has NO equivalent check before calling `FileManager.default.write()` on paths stored in meeting.json / person.json (e.g., `relativeFolderPath`); (2) `ChatFolderAccess` in FileChatTools does validate, but only for the Chat folders subsystem, not the vault itself; (3) JSON files are decoded tolerantly, so a corrupt `relativeFolderPath` like `../../../../../../etc/passwd` could be written by a future version and then used by MCP. Audit & fix: (1) Extract `resolveInsideVault()` into VaultKit so it's shared; (2) Call it in every MeetingStore write before touching the filesystem (writeMeeting, writeActionItems, writeNotes, etc.); (3) Add a test that a malicious meeting.json with a traversal path is rejected.
recommendations.userValue: Prevents any future bug (or attacker with vault write access) from escaping the vault directory and corrupting system files.
recommendations.noveltyNote: AUDIT_REPORT-2026-05-30.md flagged E4-1 vault containment as a design principle. This is follow-through: ensuring all app paths (not just MCP) enforce it.
recommendations.risks: Over-restrictive validation could block legitimate symlinks (e.g., a user symlinking a vault directory to a network share). Mitigation: validate the *final resolved path*, not the symlink chain, so symlinks are allowed as long as they point inside the vault.

recommendations.title: Encrypt sensitive fields in JSON-persisted records
recommendations.description: MeetingScribe stores all data (meetings, people, action items, memories) as plaintext JSON on disk. If the vault lives on a non-APFS filesystem (e.g., exFAT USB drive, network share), or if an attacker gains read access to the machine's storage, sensitive fields are exposed: person's email/phone, memories, meeting summaries with confidential decisions. Introduce optional field-level encryption for sensitive fields using `CryptoKit.Cipher` (already imported in WhisperRunner): (1) Add a `vaultEncryptionKey` to Keychain (stored once, never changed; user is asked to set it during onboarding); (2) When writing person.json / meeting.json, encrypt fields marked as `@Sensitive`: displayName, emails, phones, memories, summary (keep id, dates, tags in plaintext for search); (3) Decrypt on read (transparent via a CodableWrapper); (4) On export, warn the user that decryption key is not included; (5) For existing vaults, offer a one-time 'encrypt vault' action that rewrites all files in place.
recommendations.userValue: Users with physical access to the machine or network access to vault storage cannot read meeting summaries or person contact info without the encryption key.
recommendations.noveltyNote: ARCHITECTURE.md explicitly states 'No encryption at rest — relies on user's OS-level encryption'. This is a net-new feature that makes the app safer on unencrypted volumes.
recommendations.risks: If the user loses the encryption key (app reinstall, keychain corruption), all encrypted data becomes unrecoverable. Mitigation: offer a recovery-phrase backup during setup, store a salted-hash of the key so the app can verify the user's phrase on recovery. High implementation complexity.

recommendations.title: Implement sandbox rules for the two-binary split
recommendations.description: Currently, both MeetingScribe and ScribeCore are unsigned (self-signed) and have no sandbox entitlements. Once Phase 2 activates the two-binary split (REMAINING_WORK.md), the app should formalize the inter-process trust boundary with App Sandbox entitlements: (1) ScribeCore: request Microphone, Screen Recording, Calendar, Full Disk Access (FDA) — but restrict FDA to `~/Documents/MeetingNotes` and `~/Library/Messages/chat.db` only, not the whole home directory; (2) MeetingScribe: request Calendar, Contacts, and read-only access to the vault (no FDA); (3) Use XPC to communicate between the two, with explicit entitlements (`com.tyleryannes.meetingscribe.xpc`) defining the service provider (ScribeCore) and consumer (MeetingScribe); (4) Audit all entitlements in Info.plist against the principle of least privilege.
recommendations.userValue: If one process is compromised, the attacker cannot immediately access all of the user's disk. ScribeCore is sandboxed to its necessary surfaces (audio, calendar, vault). MeetingScribe is even further restricted (no FDA, no mic).
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior audits. Sandbox entitlements are a native macOS security feature; applying them strengthens the two-binary architecture.
recommendations.risks: Sandbox entitlements are strict: if a third-party library inside MeetingScribe tries to access a file outside the entitlements, the app will crash. Mitigation: test thoroughly on the Mac after enabling sandbox; use Xcode's sandbox violation logs to debug.

recommendations.title: Audit and remediate Keychain data-at-rest exposure
recommendations.description: KeychainStore (file: /Users/tyleryannes/MeetingScribeRefactor/Sources/MeetingScribe/Diagnostics/KeychainStore.swift) uses `kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly` for all secrets (line 131), which is correct but incomplete: (1) Keychain items are NOT gated by a per-app password (the user's login password gates the Keychain unlock, but once logged in, any app with permission can read items); (2) Biometric protection (Face ID / Touch ID) is not required; (3) If a user shares their Mac via fast-user-switching, another user on the same machine can't access the Keychain, but within a single user session, all apps run with the same privileges. Audit what secrets are stored: notionAPIKey, linearAPIKey, googleRefreshToken, anthropicAPIKey. For highly sensitive keys like googleRefreshToken (allows calendar read and Gmail access), consider: (1) Storing a reference/nonce in Keychain and a separate encrypted blob in the app's Application Support directory, locked by an app-provided password (derived from a master password the user enters once per session); (2) Documenting that users should enable FileVault on their Mac for full disk encryption at rest; (3) Adding a 'Revoke all integrations' button in Settings that clears Keychain, useful after device compromise.
recommendations.userValue: Even if an attacker gains local access to the machine (but not the user's account password), they cannot steal integration keys by reading Keychain directly. Users are nudged toward full-disk encryption.
recommendations.noveltyNote: KeychainStore was extracted from the old UserDefaults bug. This is a follow-through to ensure Keychain usage itself is hardened.
recommendations.risks: Requiring a per-session master password creates friction. Mitigation: make it optional, defaulting to the current 'login password unlocks Keychain' model. Advanced users who want per-session keys can opt in.

recommendations.title: Document and enforce TCC/permission lifecycle
recommendations.description: Info.plist (Resources/Info.plist) declares seven TCC-protected permissions: Microphone, Screen Capture, Calendar (read), Contacts, AppleEvents, Full Disk Access (implied by messagesdb read). The app's `designated requirement` in the Makefile is stable (`identifier "com.tyleryannes.MeetingScribe" and certificate leaf[subject.CN] = "MeetingScribe Local Signer"`, from ARCHITECTURE.md), so permissions survive rebuilds. However: (1) There is no user-facing UI explaining which permissions are needed for which features (e.g., 'Full Disk Access is only used to read iMessages if you enable People stats'); (2) No 'permission status' dashboard showing which are granted; (3) No revocation flow (if a user clicks 'Don't Allow' on the Microphone prompt, the app should gracefully disable recording, not hang). Add: (1) A Settings page listing each permission, its purpose, and whether it's granted (use `NSApplicationDelegate.application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:)` to check status via TCC checks); (2) A graceful fallback for each permission: no microphone → disable recording, no calendar → skip auto-scheduling, no FDA → skip iMessage stats and show a warning; (3) A 'Reset all TCC permissions' button that documents how to open System Settings → Privacy & Security and revoke manually.
recommendations.userValue: Users understand what they're granting. Features degrade gracefully if permissions are denied. Privacy-conscious users can revoke permissions knowing what breaks.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audits (ARCHITECTURE.md) documented TCC permissions; this operationalizes them with UI and fallbacks.
recommendations.risks: Checking TCC permission status requires the `com.apple.security.system.privilege` entitlement, which may not be grantable in the current signing model. Mitigation: test on the Mac; if unavailable, fall back to try-and-handle-denial pattern (attempt to use feature, catch error, show fallback).

audits.summary: Testing, CI/CD, and build health are foundational but fragmented. The prior audits caught critical data-loss bugs (ENG-A/B/C around transcript truncation, vault migration, race conditions) and properly documented integration-test gaps. Recent work added golden-audio and pipeline-integration test stubs (33 test files now, up from ~5), plus real CI on PR/push with design and dup guards. But 4 major gaps remain: (1) the golden-audio + integration tests are skeleton-only with no fixtures, (2) the two-binary ScribeCore path ships unfinalized and can silently drop entire meetings, (3) no crash capture or post-publish release verification, and (4) notarization is manual, not automated. Net-new recommendations focus on completing the integration test harness, gating the ScribeCore landmine, and closing the release-integrity gaps so regressions and deployment failures surface automatically.

recommendations.title: Complete the E5-1 golden-audio transcription regression suite with real fixtures
recommendations.description: The test skeleton `GoldenAudioTranscriptionTests.swift` exists and will run WER scoring against `Fixtures/golden-audio/*.wav` files, but the fixtures directory is empty. Commit 3–5 reference clips (20–40s each): one clean CC-licensed speech sample, one with overlapping speakers, one with background noise, one with silence gaps, and corresponding `*.expected.txt` transcripts. Pin each against the `baseEnModelSHA256` to detect model regressions. This is the *only* automated surface for whisper quality drift — a whisper.cpp upgrade or model swap can silently degrade output with zero CI signal otherwise.
recommendations.userValue: A model or whisper.cpp upgrade that degrades transcript quality is caught in CI before it reaches users, not discovered in production.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Builds directly on audit finding E5-1 (existing plan) by implementing the fixture layer that makes the skeleton test runnable.
recommendations.dependencies: None. The test target is already wired; just needs fixture files + one-line README explaining how to add more.

recommendations.title: Implement E5-2 end-to-end pipeline integration test with data-loss assertions
recommendations.description: `PipelineIntegrationTests.swift` has two happy-path test stubs (200 LOC total). Extend with: (1) a test that injects `liveDroppedChunks > 0` and asserts the batch repair path is triggered and the transcript is recovered from `segments/`; (2) a test that stubs Ollama and asserts summary + action items are persisted alongside transcript; (3) a test that kills a thread mid-finalize and verifies the meeting lands in a resumable state (stage journal); (4) a concurrency test driving finalize + transcribeNow simultaneously and asserting only one runs. These assertions specifically would have caught the ScribeCore orphan-folder bug (E3-1) where audio is recorded but never transcribed into the referenced meeting dir.
recommendations.userValue: A crash or race during finalize (the most complex, CPU-heavy phase) no longer silently leaves a meeting with partial data or stranded forever. Silent truncation of the final minutes is caught before release.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends audit finding E5-2 with concrete test cases for the three most dangerous paths (dropped chunks, race condition, crash recovery) that prior leaf-unit tests don't cover.
recommendations.dependencies: GoldenAudioTranscriptionTests fixtures (optional, for batch repair variant); an Ollama stub or mock.

recommendations.title: Gate the ScribeCore daemon recording path behind a disabled-by-default feature flag (SHORT-TERM data-loss prevention)
recommendations.description: The daemon-recording path in `MeetingManager.startRecording` (line 232-233, `tryStartViaScribeCore()`) is live and auto-registered on every launch via `registerScribeCoreLoginItem()` (MeetingScribeApp.swift:166). When the daemon is up first, it records audio into `meetings/scribecore-<date>/` — a folder with no `meeting.json`, divorced from the UI's `activeMeeting`, and never finalized (no merge, no transcript, no summary, no action items). The UI's `recordingStopped` handler (lines 135–149) writes an empty live transcript to the orphaned folder and goes idle, leaving actual audio unindexed and invisible. This is silent, total loss of a meeting, nondeterministic (depends on daemon boot timing). Minimum fix: add a `usingScribeCoreRecording` feature flag (default false) to `AppSettings`, gate the daemon path on it, and make `tryStartViaScribeCore()` return false while disabled. Document why in a comment and link to REMAINING_WORK §2 (Phase 2 proper fix). This is a shipping bug with no user-facing feature gate.
recommendations.userValue: Stops the silent, nondeterministic loss of an entire meeting that can occur if the daemon happens to start before the user hits record. Unblocks Phase 2 when ready.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E3-1 flagged this as a critical live landmine in the shipping binary. This is the short-term fix; the proper fix (having the daemon finalize) is ARCH-1 + Phase 2.
recommendations.dependencies: None (pure feature-flag gating).

recommendations.title: Add crash-report capture into the app-log and diagnostics bundle (E5-4 from audit)
recommendations.description: Currently crashes leave no trace because there's no exception/signal handler. Add an uncaught-exception + signal handler (or integrate PLCrashReporter / MetricKit) that writes a `crashes/crash-<timestamp>.log` next to `app.log` in the vault directory. On next launch, scan for new crash logs and surface a prompt ("MeetingScribe quit unexpectedly — include the crash report in diagnostics?"). Fold crash logs into the `DiagnosticsExporter` bundle so a user can attach them to an issue. This turns an invisible crash into reproducible data.
recommendations.userValue: A crash on a user's Mac becomes actionable instead of invisible. Crashes are the one class of bug users can't describe.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Builds on audit finding E5-4; app has solid local diagnostics but no crash capture. macOS 13+ has crash.log in ~/Library/Logs/ but waiting for the user to find it is unreliable.
recommendations.dependencies: None (optional: a lightweight crash reporter framework like PLCrashReporter).

recommendations.title: Implement `make doctor` pre-flight self-test to surface environment drift
recommendations.description: Most "it's broken" reports are really environment drift (whisper binary missing, Ollama down, model SHA-256 mismatch, TCC permissions revoked, storage dir unmounted). Add a bundled `make doctor` target and a "Run self-test" button in Settings → Diagnostics that verifies: (1) whisper binary at the configured path exists and matches the pinned SHA-256, (2) Ollama is running and has the required model pulled, (3) TCC permissions granted (mic, screen, calendar), (4) signing identity valid, (5) storage dir writable, (6) Sparkle feed reachable (200). Output results to `app.log` and include them in the diagnostics bundle. Reuses the pinned SHA so install.sh and runtime agree.
recommendations.userValue: A vague "transcription failed" becomes "whisper model is missing — reinstall via Homebrew" or "Ollama server not running." Users can self-diagnose instead of opening an issue.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-3. Composes existing checks (WhisperRunner SHA validation, OllamaService connection probe, FileManager checks) into one user-facing button. Improves UX for the 80/20 case where the app isn't broken, the environment is.
recommendations.dependencies: None (reuses existing validation code).

recommendations.title: Automate notarization in the release workflow (E5-6 from audit)
recommendations.description: The release pipeline (`release.yml`) currently produces an ad-hoc-signed, unnotarized build. Notarization is spelled out in `NOTARIZATION.md` but is a manual ritual (steps 94–99) that will be skipped under deadline pressure. Add Apple Developer ID credentials as encrypted CI secrets, and insert `notarytool submit --wait` + `stapler staple` before the GitHub-Release publish (reuse the logic from NOTARIZATION.md). Fall back to ad-hoc only if secrets are absent (local builds, forks). This turns unnotarized → quarantined → manual `xattr` dance into "update just works."
recommendations.userValue: Users installing an update via Sparkle don't hit Gatekeeper quarantine warnings and don't need to run `xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine`. Updates "just work."
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-6. Apple Developer ID cert enrollment is the blocker (external), but the CI integration is straightforward and removes the manual ritual.
recommendations.dependencies: Apple Developer Program enrollment + Developer ID code-signing certificate (external; one-time setup). Once available, the CI wiring is self-contained.

recommendations.title: Fix the official `clean-reinstall.sh` recovery script to target the refactor repo and assert the remote
recommendations.description: The canonical recovery script (`clean-reinstall.sh:21`) has `REPO_DIR="$HOME/MeetingScribe"` — the **legacy repo**, not the refactor. So the official "fix your install" script actively rebuilds and installs the deprecated app, recreating the exact wrong-repo bug the audit told users to run it to fix. Change `REPO_DIR` to `$HOME/MeetingScribeRefactor`, add a guard that `git remote get-url origin` contains `meetingscribe-refactor` (abort loudly if not), and stop hardcoding `STORAGE_DIR=~/Documents/MeetingNotes` — let users keep their vault. This is a release-integrity bug in the tool meant to fix release integrity.
recommendations.userValue: Users running the recovery script can't accidentally downgrade to the deprecated app. The script actually fixes what it claims to fix.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-7 (existing plan). Tiny effort, huge leverage — this is the script users run when "my app is broken." Shipping the wrong target is a release integrity bug.
recommendations.dependencies: None.

recommendations.title: Add CI sanitizers (TSan) and code-coverage reporting for data-integrity files
recommendations.description: `ci.yml` runs `swift build -c release` + `swift test` on every PR, but does NOT run `swift test --sanitize=thread` (the race-condition detector that `AudioCountersTests.swift:6` documents but CI never executes). Add a parallel CI job running TSan tests. Also add `swift test --enable-code-coverage` and publish an `llvm-cov` summary as a PR comment, with a floor on the critical files: `MeetingPipelineController`, `WhisperRunner`, `LiveTranscriber`, `MeetingStore`, `VaultMigrationManager`. The races that ENG-C fixed (finalize vs transcribeNow, dropped chunks) only reliably surface under TSan.
recommendations.userValue: A concurrency regression in the finalize path is caught on the PR, not discovered when a user hits record during a specific timing window.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-9. Completes the CI setup by adding sanitizers (documented but not run) and coverage reporting for the highest-risk subsystems.
recommendations.dependencies: None (standard CI machinery).

recommendations.title: Extend CI dup-guard to detect diverging paired files (not just new dupes)
recommendations.description: `check-cross-tree-dupes.sh` prevents *new* duplication between `Sources/MeetingScribe/` and `Sources/ScribeCore/` but is blind to *divergence within allowlisted pairs*. For example, `WhisperRunner.swift` and `NotificationManager.swift` differ between the two trees (documented in REMAINING_WORK §1), but the guard doesn't catch it. Enhance the guard to hash each allowlisted pair and fail if a file that was byte-identical diverges without an explicit `# diverged:` marker in the baseline. Surface the diverged set (currently ~2 files, ~150 lines total) as a tracked debt list. This matters because ENG-A and ENG-D fixes currently live in *both copies*, and a fix in one silently doesn't apply to the other.
recommendations.userValue: A bug fix applied to the app's copy of WhisperRunner is visible as a regression if the daemon's copy is left unchanged, preventing silent divergence.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-10. Pairs well with ARCH-1 (CaptureKit extraction); until the audio/transcription stack is shared, this guard prevents regression by making divergence explicit.
recommendations.dependencies: None (extends the existing bash guard).

recommendations.title: Add release-artifact post-publish verification job to CI (E5-8 from audit)
recommendations.description: The `release.yml` workflow verifies inputs (feed slug, key placeholder) but never confirms the *published* artifact is actually installable. After `softprops/action-gh-release`, add a verification job that: (1) downloads the published `appcast.xml` + zip from the release URL, (2) verifies the EdDSA signature with the public key from `Info.plist`, (3) confirms `sparkle:version` matches the tag, (4) HEAD-checks that `SUFeedURL` resolves 200. Fail the workflow if any check fails. This catches a signing/upload glitch that ships a release no installed copy can apply.
recommendations.userValue: A broken release (corrupted archive, signature mismatch, unreachable feed URL) is caught in CI before it's published, not discovered by users stuck on an old version forever.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E5-8. Verifies the release *after* it's published; complements the pre-release checks already in `release.yml`.
recommendations.dependencies: None (uses standard GitHub Actions and CLI tools).

recommendations.title: Implement E3-3 — pipeline write-ahead journal + finalize resume on crash
recommendations.description: Currently finalize is a linear sequence of independent writes (transcript → summary → meeting.json → markdown → FTS index). A crash between steps (e.g., mid-summarize, which takes minutes on M1/M2) leaves a meeting in a partial state with no record of where it stopped. The existing crash-recovery path covers *capture* (`.recording.inprogress` markers) but not *finalize*. Add a tiny `pipeline-state.json` per meeting (`{stage: merged|transcribed|summarized|indexed, updatedAt}`) written synchronously before each stage. On launch, scan for meetings whose stage < `indexed` and resume finalize from the last completed stage (analogous to the audio crash sweep). This turns "crashed during summarize → meeting stranded forever" into "resumes on next launch, auto-completes."
recommendations.userValue: A crash during the CPU-heavy finalize phase (especially summarize, which can take 1–2 minutes) no longer strands a meeting with a transcript but no summary/action-items forever.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E3-3 (existing plan). Extends the existing crash-recovery pattern (which covers capture) to finalize. High impact because finalize is the longest-running, most CPU-intensive phase.
recommendations.dependencies: None (low-risk file format addition).

recommendations.title: Implement E3-4 — SQLite integrity check + auto-rebuild on open
recommendations.description: The People graph / search index lives in `SecondBrainDB.db` (SQLite). Currently `SecondBrainDB.open()` opens WAL and runs `ensureSchema` but never validates integrity. A corrupted/truncated DB (power loss during checkpoint, disk full, quota exceeded) silently returns empty search results — the People graph goes dark with no signal and no auto-recovery. On `open()`, run `PRAGMA quick_check` (and detect `SQLITE_CORRUPT` from any query); on failure, delete the DB and trigger a full `rebuild()` from the canonical `person.json` files (which is safe because the DB is derived). Also wrap `rebuild()` so a failed step does `ROLLBACK` instead of leaving a committed half-index. This matches the philosophy of the crash-recovery path: prefer availability + regeneration over silent data loss.
recommendations.userValue: The People graph / global search self-heals instead of going dark after a bad shutdown. A single bad bit can't permanently blind the app.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E3-4. The DB is derived (not canonical), so corruption is recoverable via rebuild. This is a high-leverage, low-risk reliability upgrade.
recommendations.dependencies: None (uses standard SQLite pragmas).

recommendations.title: Create atomic-write helper for all canonical artifacts (merges, audio imports, index files)
recommendations.description: Currently several canonical writes are non-atomic: `mergeSegments` (`AudioRecorder.swift:367`) removes the old file then writes the new one; audio imports (`MeetingManager.convertToM4A:834`) do the same; index writes (`.meeting-index.json`, `_recent.json`) are best-effort on a detached task. A crash or permission error mid-write leaves a missing/truncated canonical file. Add a single `writeAtomicallyReplacing(tempDir:finalURL:body:)` helper that writes to `.<name>.tmp` in the same directory, then `FileManager.replaceItemAt` to swap atomically. Route merged audio, afconvert output, and index writes through it. This is the single biggest reliability improvement after crash recovery.
recommendations.userValue: No half-written canonical file (transcript, merged audio, index) can ever be observed by the app, even after a crash or permission error mid-write.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Audit finding E3-7. Extends the pattern already used in `coordinatedWrite` (atomicity via coordination + exclusive access) to file-system-level atomic operations. High leverage for low effort.
recommendations.dependencies: None (pure FileManager utility).

audits.summary: MeetingScribe is feature-rich but misses critical PKM power-user needs: cross-entity linking and bidirectional queryability are under-invested; the vault is not directly queryable or scriptable; meeting↔task↔person↔note relationships lack fast forward/back navigation; and there's no external access or bulk operations for daily knowledge workflows. The app has strong foundations (MCP, local search, markdown links) but needs 6-8 focused improvements to become a true PKM engine for knowledge workers who live in cross-vault operations.

recommendations.title: Backlinks & Bidirectional Query Panel
recommendations.description: Every entity (meeting, task, project, person, note) needs a right-sidebar backlinks panel showing what links TO it. Currently backlinks exist only for meetings via file-scan; they need to be persistent, indexed, and available everywhere. Add a `BacklinkIndex` actor (built at write-time) + a `.backlinksShown` toggle on every entity detail view. When viewing a task, users instantly see "linked from 3 meetings + 2 projects" and can jump between them. References: `WorkspaceIndex.swift:61-85` (async file-scan is too slow for daily use); `WorkspaceLinks.swift` (link parsing infrastructure exists).
recommendations.userValue: Power users trace ideas across the vault in 1-2 clicks instead of searching + combining results manually. A task becomes a hub that shows its full context (which meetings mention it, which projects depend on it).
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plans mention backlinks for meetings only; this makes them universal + indexed + fast.
recommendations.risks: Requires write-time index maintenance; must handle orphaned links gracefully.

recommendations.title: Global Forward/Back Navigation Stack
recommendations.description: Currently ⌘[ and ⌘] are undefined. Implement a persistent `NavigationStack` in `WorkspaceRouter` that tracks every entity open/close event and allows stepping backward/forward through the browsing history (like a web browser). Emit events on every entity selection in any tab, coalesce rapid clicks. Show breadcrumb spine at top of detail panes. References: `WorkspaceRouter.swift` (exists but is minimal); `Models/WorkspaceLinks.swift:100-120` (notification skeleton exists). Prior plan flagged in MASTER_PLAN_V5 as `DN-1/CP-5`.
recommendations.userValue: When jumping between linked tasks→people→meetings→notes, users can instantly jump back to any previous context with ⌘[ instead of re-searching or navigating menus.
recommendations.noveltyNote: V5 plan includes this; endorsing it from PKM lens because it directly enables the exploration workflows power users depend on.
recommendations.risks: Stack can grow large; implement bounds (e.g. last 50 items) to avoid memory creep.

recommendations.title: Transitive Linking: Meeting → Task → Person Graph Queries
recommendations.description: The app can search meetings, tasks, and people independently, but there's no query that asks "which people are involved in tasks I extracted from meetings tagged 'Product'?" or "show me all action items from meetings with Sarah in the last month." Add transitive-query MCP tools that follow edges: `list_related_people(meeting_id)` (people mentioned + attendees), `list_meeting_decisions(person_id)` (decisions involving them), `list_task_stakeholders(task_id)` (people linked to the task's meeting). Requires: indexed edges (person↔meeting attendance, task↔meeting source, decision↔person). Build on the `EntityGraphIndex` from MASTER_PLAN_V5 (`SD-3`). References: `PeopleChatTools.swift:82-98` (existing tools are simple lists; need to add graph traversal).
recommendations.userValue: Users ask Claude "Who should I brief on the Product roadmap?" and get back people from relevant meetings + their contact info, eliminating manual cross-tab searches.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plan includes `EntityGraphIndex` infrastructure; this builds queryable MCP tools on top of it for PKM workflows.
recommendations.risks: Graph can be sparse on first use; gracefully handle missing edges.

recommendations.title: Bulk Link Creation & Editing
recommendations.description: Linking is currently 1-item-at-a-time: add one task to a project, link one person to one meeting. For bulk operations ("link all these attendees to the project", "tag 5 meetings with 'Q3-Planning'"), users need multi-select + batch actions. Add to `MSList` (the planned shared list component): multi-select mode (checkboxes), a batch-action toolbar ("Link to…", "Tag as…", "Assign to…"), and drag-to-link in the list. Store link edits batched off-main per `TK-2` performance plan. References: `ActionItemsChrome.swift` (already has table/board view with multi-row visibility); no drag-to-link exists yet.
recommendations.userValue: Instead of clicking 12 times to link 12 people to a project, users select them and do it in 2 clicks + 1 drag.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; addresses the PKM user's need for fast bulk operations without sacrificing UI simplicity.
recommendations.risks: UX complexity; must keep single-item workflows fast and obvious.

recommendations.title: Vault Query API: CLI + JSON Schema Export
recommendations.description: Power users want to export subsets of the vault ("all meetings from Q2 with budget role", "every decision linked to Sarah") for external analysis or to feed into other tools. Currently only exports are full markdown dump (Google Drive) + CSV (tasks only). Add: (1) a simple read-only CLI entry point (`meetingscribe query --tag=Product --since=2026-01-01 --format=json`) that reads the vault and outputs JSON; (2) a schema doc describing the vault structure (entities, edge types, standard fields); (3) in the UI, a one-click "Export filtered list" button in every tab that generates a timestamped JSON file. Reuse existing `MeetingStore`/`PeopleStore` read paths + the `Codable` DTO types. References: `MeetingManager.swift` (already loads from disk); `MeetingScribeShared/` (DTOs exist); no CLI entry point exists yet.
recommendations.userValue: A user can query the vault from a terminal script, use jq to slice and combine data across entity types, pipe into other analysis tools. Non-destructive, read-only. Enables no-lock-in: the vault is queryable and portable.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; core to the power PKM ethos of "your data is yours." Builds on existing portable-file design.
recommendations.risks: CLI needs to carefully handle concurrency (app may be modifying vault at same time); use file locks or a simple server-side gate.

recommendations.title: Meeting↔Task Bidirectional Linking & Inline Creation
recommendations.description: Currently: a task can link to a meeting (via `meetingID` field), but there's no way to see "which tasks came from this meeting?" or to create a task inline from the meeting detail and have it auto-linked. On a meeting's Summary tab, action items are shown inline with a 1-tap complete toggle, but there's no "+ Add action item" button. Add: (1) a persistent `linkedMeetingID` field on `ActionItem` (inverse of current `meetingID`); (2) a "Tasks sourced from this meeting" section on meeting detail (below action items) that shows related tasks even if not extracted by AI; (3) a "+ Add action item" button on the Summary that creates a task, auto-links to the meeting, and opens the task page for editing. References: `ActionItem.swift` (already has `meetingID`; add inverse index); `UnifiedMeetingDetail.swift:200-300` (action items rendered here; add section + button); `TaskPageView.swift` (open-for-edit already works).
recommendations.userValue: After a meeting, users create follow-up tasks inline without switching tabs. The task stays linked to its source meeting forever, enabling later queries like "which meetings led to Q3 engineering work?"
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior plan includes inline action-item editing (TDY-4, PPL-1); this adds the reverse link + persistence.
recommendations.risks: Data model change; requires migration for existing tasks.

recommendations.title: Smart @-mention Completions in Notes & Tasks
recommendations.description: Markdown links (`[title](meetingscribe://…)`) exist in theory, but there's no UI to insert them. When editing a note or task body, users should be able to type `@` to trigger a completions menu showing recent/related entities (meetings, people, tasks, projects). Selecting an item inserts the markdown link. Implement: (1) a `@` detector in `MarkdownEditor.swift` that triggers a popover completion menu (reuse `GlobalSearchView` or make a simpler `@Completions` view); (2) prioritize entities by recency + relevance to the current context (if editing a task linked to meeting X, boost meeting X); (3) cache the list of completions so typing is instant. References: `MarkdownEditor.swift:1-50` (editor exists, no @ support); `GlobalSearchView.swift` (can adapt its search logic); `WorkspaceIndex.swift:11-49` (catalog of entities).
recommendations.userValue: Linking becomes as easy as typing `@meeting` instead of manually constructing URLs or copy-pasting IDs. The vault becomes a web of interconnected notes, like Obsidian.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; makes the link infrastructure (WorkspaceLinks.swift) user-facing and reduces friction from current 5 clicks to 2 keystrokes.
recommendations.risks: Completion menu must be non-intrusive; only show on explicit @ keystroke to avoid noise.

recommendations.title: Queryable People Tag System: Hierarchy & Faceted Search
recommendations.description: Tags on people exist (e.g. "Collaborator", "Friend") but are flat strings with no hierarchy or semantic meaning. Power users want to organize people by context trees (e.g. Collaborators → Product → [Sarah, Horst]; Collaborators → Engineering → [Ana, Dev]). Currently tags are display-only on the person detail chips. Add: (1) a tag tree editor in Settings (drag to nest, edit labels); (2) on People list, show a hierarchical filter pill strip (click "Collaborators" → see sub-tags → click "Product"); (3) expose tags in backlinks/queries ("all meetings with tagged Collaborators:Engineering"); (4) in chat, add `list_people_by_tag_path(path)` tool (e.g. "Collaborators/Product"). References: `PersonDetailView.swift:474-490` (tags rendered as chips today); `PeopleListView.swift` (search/filter exists, tags are flat); `PeopleChatTools.swift` (could add hierarchy-aware tools); prior plan mentions `TagRegistry` unification (`SD-5`) but not hierarchy.
recommendations.userValue: A founder managing 40+ contacts can organize them mentally: "Who did I hire last quarter?" becomes a 1-click filter. Claude can answer "who are my top 5 most-engaged collaborators in Product?" by traversing the hierarchy.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not explicitly in prior plans; adds queryable structure to the relationship CRM, enabling context-aware reconnect & collaboration features.
recommendations.risks: Tag migration needed if hierarchy is added; must remain backward-compatible with flat tags.

recommendations.title: Vault as a Read-Only Git-Like Append Log
recommendations.description: The vault is a folder of JSON + markdown files, which is good for portability but has zero audit history. A power user who deletes a meeting or edits a summary can't recover it. Implement: (1) an optional `vault/.scribelog/` directory that stores immutable JSON records of every write (meeting created/updated/deleted, task changed, person added, decision captured) with timestamp + source (app vs. MCP vs. CLI); (2) a `scribelog` MCP tool `get_audit_trail(entity_id)` that shows the history; (3) in Settings, a "Undo recent changes" UI that lets users revert specific changes up to N days back. The log is append-only (never modified), so it survives crashes and can be synced separately from the vault. References: `VaultMigrationManager.swift` (writes are currently unlogged); `MeetingManager.swift:339-380` (write points); no history system exists yet.
recommendations.userValue: Users have an immutable record of what changed when, enabling accidental-delete recovery and audit for team settings later. The log also enables multi-device sync (send just the log to other devices, they replay).
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; aligns with the local-first philosophy and no-lock-in promise. Essential for teams/multi-device.
recommendations.risks: Append log can grow large; implement rotation (e.g. trim entries >90 days). Must be strictly append-only to be trustworthy.

recommendations.title: Decision Ledger: Capture & Retrieve Org Memory
recommendations.description: Great meetings produce decisions ("We're moving to SaaS", "Sarah owns Q3 roadmap"). Decisions are extracted from meeting summaries but they're scattered and unlinked. Create a dedicated "Decisions" ledger (like action items): (1) one-click capture from meeting Summary (alongside "+ Add action item"); (2) a Decisions tab in the main nav (between Meetings and People) showing all decisions sorted by recency/importance; (3) each decision links to the meeting + attendees + optionally a "decision owner"; (4) on a person's detail view, show "Decisions [this person] made" or "Decisions [this person] is affected by"; (5) MCP tools: `list_decisions(since=date, person=)`, `get_decision(id)`, `search_decisions(query)`. Implementation: `Decision` struct already exists (`Models/Decision.swift`); wire up UI in Summary tab + create the tab + backlinks. References: `Decision.swift` (model exists but is unused in UI); `MeetingSummaryTab.swift` (where decisions are rendered as prose; add extraction + capture button); MASTER_PLAN_V5 flags this as `TM-4` (Decisions section + 1-click capture).
recommendations.userValue: 6 months later, "Did we decide to use vendor X?" becomes a search instead of a team meeting. A new hire can read the decision ledger to understand why choices were made.
recommendations.noveltyNote: V5 plan includes Decisions section; this makes it a first-class, searchable, linked entity.
recommendations.risks: Decisions need high quality extraction; fallback to manual entry if AI misses them.

audits.summary: A new, non-technical user is scared of permissions and local AI setup. The rebuild respects this with skip-friendly onboarding, one-tap AI setup, and a sample tutorial meeting—but three jargon gaps and missing first-run guidance block confident adoption: "vault" is unexplained, three recording entry points are undifferentiated, and empty tabs don't explain the auto-flow that powers the app's core value.

recommendations.title: De-jargon 'vault' throughout onboarding and first-run copy
recommendations.description: Replace 'vault' with 'notes and recordings folder' in OnboardingSheet.swift:60 (heading), :62 (body), and wherever else it appears in first-run surfaces. Change 'Where to store your vault?' → 'Where should we save your notes and recordings?' and 'MeetingScribe keeps all recordings, transcripts, and notes in a single folder' stays clear. The sample seeder and Settings can keep 'vault' for power users, but first-run must use plain language. Also soften SetupCheckSheet.swift:76 from 'Local AI engine (Ollama)' → 'Meeting summaries' or 'Summary AI'—Ollama is technical jargon.
recommendations.userValue: A non-technical user won't feel confused or left behind by unexplained jargon. The app feels designed for them, not for engineers.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces audit_users.md USER-5 ask #1 (de-jargon 'vault') and MASTER_PLAN_V3.md §5 (onboarding clarity). Net-new: expand to SetupCheckSheet copy.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pure copy changes in OnboardingSheet.swift, SetupCheckSheet.swift, and any other first-run text.

recommendations.title: Disambiguate 'Record Meeting' vs 'Voice note' vs 'Ad-hoc recording' with one-line descriptions
recommendations.description: Add a one-time disambiguation sheet OR a tooltip row in TodayView after onboarding completes. The three entry points exist (MainWindow.swift toolbar, TodayView.swift:411, :421, :638) but a new user has no idea which to use when. Show: 'Record Meeting' (for calendar events), 'Voice note' (quick dictation, no transcript), 'Ad-hoc meeting' (manual recording without a calendar link). Also surface in a Settings subsection 'Recording types' so users can re-read it. The sample seeder already hints at 'Ad-hoc' but not visibly in the UI.
recommendations.userValue: Users confidently pick the right recording type the first time instead of trying all three and deleting experiments. Reduces doubt about which button to press.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces audit_users.md USER-5 ask #2 (disambiguate three recording entry points). Net-new: propose a specific UI (one-time sheet or tooltip grid, not copy-only change).
recommendations.dependencies: OnboardingSheet completion (show after hasCompletedOnboarding = true but before showing Today). Reusable in Settings → Help/Recording types.

recommendations.title: Add a one-time 'How it works' tour after onboarding
recommendations.description: After OnboardingSheet dismisses and SetupCheckSheet closes, show a 3-step visual guide in a light sheet or on Today itself: (1) 'Record a call using the Record Meeting button'; (2) 'MeetingScribe transcribes on your Mac'; (3) 'A summary and action items appear in the Tasks tab.' Show a GIF or annotated screenshot sequence if possible; text-only is acceptable. Dismiss with a 'Got it' button. Flag this as a first-run-only event (`@AppStorage("hasSeenHowItWorks")`) so returning users don't see it again. This closes the gap between 'the app is set up' and 'I know what to do now.'
recommendations.userValue: New users understand the end-to-end flow (record → transcribe → summarize → extract tasks) before they record their first call, setting correct expectations. Reduces confusion when transcribing happens in the background.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces MASTER_PLAN_V3.md §5 'A one-time how it works overview after onboarding.' Net-new: specific file placement (HowItWorksSheet.swift or similar) and integration into MainWindow.swift after onboarding/setup complete.
recommendations.dependencies: OnboardingSheet and SetupCheckSheet. Fire after both dismiss (hook in MainWindow.swift:533). New file HowItWorksSheet.swift.

recommendations.title: Update empty-state copy for Tasks/People/Meetings tabs to explain auto-population
recommendations.description: The Tasks, People, and Meetings tabs show empty states on first launch. Add a secondary line of copy explaining the auto-flow: e.g., on Tasks empty state, add 'Action items from your recorded meetings will appear here automatically.' On People, 'Attendees from your meetings will be listed here—add notes and tags to keep in touch.' On Meetings, 'Your recordings will appear here once transcription completes.' These are in TasksTab/PeopleTab/MeetingsView empty states (find via `ContentUnavailableView` calls). The copy should reassure that the app is working, not broken.
recommendations.userValue: New users won't think the app is broken when tabs are empty. They'll understand that recording a meeting is the trigger that populates everything.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends audit_users.md USER-5 ask #2 (first-run clarity) with a concrete surface: empty-state secondary copy. Prior plans didn't specify this detail.
recommendations.dependencies: None; pure copy updates in the relevant tab views.

recommendations.title: Add a 'Start recording' affordance to the first-run empty state when onboarding is fresh
recommendations.description: When a user completes onboarding and the vault is empty (before SampleMeetingSeeder runs), highlight the 'Record Meeting' button with an animated pulse or glow effect (1-2 sec, then fade) so their eye goes there immediately. Add a one-line hint: 'Ready to record? Press here or use ⌘R.' The sample seeder will seed a tutorial meeting 0.4 seconds after MainWindow appears (TodayView.swift:40), so by the time a user looks around, they'll see both the seeded example AND the encouragement to record their own. This is purely UX—no backend change.
recommendations.userValue: The first 10 seconds of using the app are unambiguous: 'I should record something.' Reduces the "I don't know what to do" friction that defeats onboarding.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends the sample-seeding pattern (SampleMeetingSeeder.swift) with a visual nudge. Pure UX, not new.
recommendations.dependencies: SampleMeetingSeeder (already in place). Add a @State flag in TodayView to track first-paint, then apply a conditional `.scaleEffect` + `.animation` pulse to the Record Meeting button on first load.

recommendations.title: Surface live recording feedback (mic + system audio status, audio levels) during a live recording
recommendations.description: When the user hits 'Record Meeting' and the button changes to 'Stop recording,' add a small HUD or expansion to the live recording card (MeetingRecordDock.swift or the 'Recording now' card in TodayView.swift:477–488) that shows: (1) 'Listening to: Microphone + System audio' or similar plain text, (2) A live audio-level meter (green bars rising/falling as sound comes in). The audio-level meter already exists for voice notes in the same file—reuse it. This reassures a non-technical user that the app is actually capturing what they think it is, and if the meter stays silent they'll know something's wrong while they can still re-record.
recommendations.userValue: Users instantly know 'the app is listening' and can verify mic/system capture is working before the meeting ends. Prevents the 'I recorded a meeting and got nothing' surprise.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces MASTER_PLAN_V3.md 'live audio-level / silence indicator' (ENG-D). Applies specifically to meeting recording, not just voice notes. Net-new: specify it as first-run confidence-builder, not just feature parity.
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingRecordDock.swift (audio meter code reuse), TodayView.swift live card section, and the audio-capture state from MeetingManager. Should show only when isRecording = true.

recommendations.title: Add a post-recording toast/card explaining 'transcription in progress'
recommendations.description: When the user stops recording, the live recording card closes. Add a brief contextual toast or a 'Finalizing' card in the Today feed that says: 'Meeting recorded! Transcribing on your Mac... this usually takes a few minutes.' Then as the status progresses (Transcribing → Summarizing → Ready), update the card copy. The meeting card already shows a status pill ('Ready' / 'Transcribing' / etc.), but a fresh user won't know to look there or what the status means. A one-time toast at the moment of stop + a persistent card in the feed until completion removes the 'is anything happening?' doubt.
recommendations.userValue: Users understand that the app is working after they stop recording, rather than wondering if they need to do something else or if the app is stuck.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends the post-recording UX. Not explicitly in prior plans, but aligns with 'reassure the user' theme from audit_users.md USER-5.
recommendations.dependencies: TodayView.swift past/upcoming section, ToastCenter (already integrated per MASTER_PLAN_V3.md). Hook into MeetingManager.lastStoppedMeetingID or the same place where MeetingCard status is sourced.

recommendations.title: Explain Chat sidebar purpose and permissions in onboarding
recommendations.description: The Chat sidebar is off by default (chatRailVisible = false per MainWindow.swift:67), which is correct for first-run. But when a user has recorded a meeting and the sidebar is still hidden, they have no idea what the 'Assistant' button does. Add a one-line explanation in the toolbar button's `.help()` text: 'Ask questions about your meetings, tasks, and notes.' Also add a contextual tip on first sidebar toggle: 'Chat runs locally. You can ask about your vault or let it read files in your sandboxed Chat folders.' This removes the mystery around 'is it safe? does it upload?' for a privacy-conscious new user.
recommendations.userValue: New users understand the Chat sidebar is safe (local), optional, and useful (can ask about meetings/notes) before they turn it on. No fear of privacy leakage.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends audit_users.md USER-5 ask #3 (Chat context) with plain-language guidance. Reinforces the local-first trust narrative.
recommendations.dependencies: MainWindow.swift toolbar button (.help() text) and ChatPanel/ChatSidebar.swift (optional first-show toast). Pure text updates + one optional toast.

recommendations.title: Create a 'First recording' success card on Today after completing the first transcription
recommendations.description: Track whether the user has completed a full record → transcribe → summarize cycle (`@AppStorage("hasCompletedFirstRecording")` or similar in MainWindow/TodayView). On the moment the first real recording (not the sample) becomes Ready, show a celebratory card or toast: 'Your first meeting is ready! Check out the summary → tasks tab. Want to record another, or explore People?' with a button to jump to the sample meeting's summary as an example, or a 'Done' button. This celebrates the first win and nudges them to the next action.
recommendations.userValue: A first-time user sees their work immediately rewarded ('the app works!') and gets clear next-step options instead of wondering 'what now?'
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: uses the existing status-tracking infrastructure to celebrate a milestone and guide next actions.
recommendations.dependencies: MeetingManager state (track real vs. sample meetings), TodayView (new card/toast display), MainWindow (flag storage).

recommendations.title: Add optional guided tour / tooltips for the 5-item nav rail on first app open
recommendations.description: After 'How it works' tour, and once the user is on Today, show a light overlay with 5 tooltips (one per nav item): 'Today — see your schedule and live recordings; Meetings — review past calls; People — manage relationships; Tasks — track action items; Notes — quick voice memos.' This is a one-time-per-session visual tour (`@AppStorage("hasSeenNavTour")` + a 0.5-sec delay for the nav to render). Can be as simple as 5 translucent labels with a 'Next' button, or a Spotlight/Onboard-style library if you want polish. The goal is to prevent the new user from clicking through the tabs in confusion; they'll know what each tab does without trial-and-error.
recommendations.userValue: New users don't wander aimlessly through the app. They know the 5 main sections upfront, so they can focus on using the app instead of discovering it.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends onboarding with a spatial/visual tour of the main UI. Not in prior plans; net-new quality-of-life.
recommendations.dependencies: New file NavTourView.swift or NavTourOverlay.swift. Integration into MainWindow.swift after 'How it works' tour completes. Optional external library (Spotlight, Onboard).

audits.summary: A manager running 6+ calls/day across direct reports, skip-levels, and peers faces three core jobs-to-be-done: (1) walk into every 1:1 already knowing last time, open commitments, and growth themes; (2) track bidirectional commitments ("what do I owe each report, what do they owe me") across meetings; (3) notice drifting relationships before they become problems. The rebuilt app has solved #1 partially (calendar meetings now show, pre-meeting brief synthesizes) but #2 and #3 remain friction. Core gaps: action items lack direction (iOwe/theyOwe), growth themes aren't tracked over time, notes lack visibility controls for candid manager assessments, and 1:1 templates don't capture non-recorded meetings that most managers rely on.

recommendations.title: Directed commitment tracking: add iOwe/theyOwe to action items
recommendations.description: Extend ActionItem (Sources/MeetingScribe/ActionItems/ActionItem.swift:23) with an optional `direction: enum {iOwe, theyOwe, mutual}` field and a `personID: String?` to link commitments to specific people, not free-text owner strings. On the Commitments card (TodayView.swift:211), split items into a per-person "Commitments" sidebar showing "I owe Priya" vs "Priya owes me" instead of the binary isMine() filter. This makes the accountability loop concrete and queryable.
recommendations.userValue: Managers track delegation and delivery; today the app can't answer "what have I promised each report and not delivered" or "what does each team member owe back." This is the core management feedback loop.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-3 from prior audit was correctly scoped but not yet built; now confirmed by actual PersonDetailView code showing ownerPersonID already exists as foundation.
recommendations.risks: Schema migration for existing action_items.json; backward compat via optional + default. No breaking changes if direction is nil.

recommendations.title: Growth-theme threads: time-series tracking of 1:1 arc per report
recommendations.description: Add a GrowthTheme model (like AttachedNote but with dated entries and trend metadata) to Person (Sources/MeetingScribe/People/Person.swift:244). Each theme ("delegation", "public speaking", "code reviews") gets timestamped notes and a trend indicator (improving/stuck/regressed). Render on the person detail as a mini-timeline per theme, and include in the 1:1 prep digest (PreMeetingBriefView.swift). This replaces the blank 1:1 template with data-driven context.
recommendations.userValue: Managers write reviews quarterly by scrolling six months of scattered notes; growth themes turn that into a queryable, trendable arc. A manager can see "delegation improved over 6 weeks then plateaued" without reconstructing by hand.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-4 from prior plan; growth tracking was described as needed but never built. Code inspection shows Memory is freeform, AttachedNote is kind-tagged but undated. GrowthTheme closes the gap.
recommendations.risks: Requires schema migration. UI complexity: rendering mini-timelines per theme on person detail. Fallback to AttachedNote if GrowthTheme is nil.

recommendations.title: Private vs shareable note visibility: gate MCP reads on candid assessments
recommendations.description: Add `visibility: enum {private, shareable}` to AttachedNote (Person.swift:26) and Memory. When an agent or MCP tool reads person data (PeopleChatTools.swift), filter to shareable content only unless the user explicitly opts in to share candid notes. Add a visual indicator (lock icon) on private notes in the UI. This prevents accidental leak of "not ready for staff" assessments into a shared summary draft.
recommendations.userValue: Managers keep two registers: private candid assessments and shareable summaries. Today there's no boundary; one bucket is a leak risk if agents ever draft shareable content. This is table-stakes for trusting the system with sensitive people data.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-5 from prior plan; exact scope confirmed by code inspection showing no visibility field on notes and write-capable MCP already shipping.
recommendations.risks: MCP tool layer needs gating; add an is_private flag in tool schemas. Default: all existing notes are shareable (backward compat), users tag private as needed.

recommendations.title: Quiet 1:1 capture without recording: post-meeting template log for unrecorded talks
recommendations.description: Add a "Log a 1:1" quick-action button (next to Record Meeting on TodayView) that opens a lightweight encounter-capture sheet: date, person, 3-5 bullet points of notes, and optional commitments. Create a non-recorded Meeting stub (meetingID, startDate, attendees, transcript=nil) and link a Memory to the person. This backfills the Meetings tab and timeline for unrecorded 1:1s (the norm for most managers). Wire it into the calendar-aware 1:1 prep digest so last-time context includes walked talks, not just recordings.
recommendations.userValue: Managers do ~60% of 1:1s talked, not recorded. Today unrecorded 1:1s don't populate the person timeline, the brief reads "first meeting," and growth context is missing. This makes the People CRM actually match reality.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-10 from prior audit; pairs with U2-1 (calendar meetings now showing) to make the timeline complete.
recommendations.risks: Non-recorded meetings are created with no transcript; summarization/analysis skipped. UI must be very lightweight or managers skip it. Template-driven entry (one bullet per key point) keeps friction low.

recommendations.title: Per-report 1:1 prep digest: synthesized brief with last meeting, commitments, themes
recommendations.description: Extend PreMeetingBriefView (Sources/MeetingScribe/UI/PreMeetingBriefView.swift) to read the person's bio, recent growth themes (U2-4), and open commitments with direction (U2-3) into the synthesized LLM brief. When a manager opens an upcoming 1:1, the brief should say: "Last 1:1 was X, you're tracking their Y theme (improving), they committed to Z by now, you committed to W." Today it pulls only meetings and action items, ignoring the person record's richest context.
recommendations.userValue: A manager preps in 30 seconds instead of five minutes reading scattered notes. The brief is the highest-frequency moment where context matters most — pre-meeting anxiety is real and directly tied to whether you feel ready.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-2 from prior audit, already partially implemented (brief synthesizes) but missing person record reads. Depends on U2-1 (calendar meetings), U2-3 (commitments), U2-4 (themes).
recommendations.risks: Ollama latency if synthesis is slow; cache per-person briefs and invalidate on person edit. Fallback to static list if LLM unavailable.

recommendations.title: Team view + org rollup: my directs, skip-levels, and cross-team patterns
recommendations.description: Add a typed "directReport" + "manager" relationship (alongside the existing freeform Relationship, Person.swift:172). Render a "My Team" filter on the People list and a simple org-chart view showing directs + their open commitments, overdue items, and growth-theme status. Add a cross-report pattern aggregator (U2-11) that surfaces "4 of 7 reports raised on-call load this month" — turning 1:1 notes into org signal.
recommendations.userValue: A manager answers "how's my team doing?" in one view instead of 7 separate person pages. Recognizing patterns across reports (shared pain, shared wins) is the job that the app isn't built for yet.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-9 from prior plan; requires growth-theme aggregation infrastructure (U2-4, U2-11).
recommendations.risks: Relationship typing requires migration; infer from existing freeform labels via RelationshipType.inferred (Person.swift:144). Team view rendering can start minimal.

recommendations.title: Performance-review compilation: one-click draft from 6 months of data
recommendations.description: Add a "Compile review" button on a person's detail that pulls: date-range of 1:1 points (from Memories), completed commitments, growth-theme deltas (U2-4), and shareable notes (U2-5, visibility=shareable). Render as a structured Markdown draft (title, intro, growth themes with evidence, completed milestones, forward look). This is the single most painful recurring task managers have — turning scattered notes into a defensible review.
recommendations.userValue: Writing quarterly reviews is a 2-3 hour task per report × 7 reports. A structured draft cuts that to 30 minutes of polish, and ensures nothing is forgotten.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-6 from prior audit; depends on U2-3, U2-4, U2-5 being built first.
recommendations.risks: Privacy: must filter to shareable notes only; add explicit "share candid notes in this review?" confirm. Markdown rendering must be Notion-compatible for copy-to-doc.

recommendations.title: Sentiment/morale sparkline: early warning for disengaging reports
recommendations.description: Extend AttachedNote "sentiment" kind (Person.swift:32) to track a dated scalar (-2…+2) and render as a sparkline on the person-detail header. Add a quick-log button after each 1:1 ("How's Priya doing?") to capture sentiment score, or infer from recorded 1:1 transcript (Ollama can estimate engagement tone). This is early-warning for attrition before HR notices.
recommendations.userValue: Spotting a report's morale decline week-by-week is the difference between proactive retention and losing someone. Today the signal is invisible.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-8 from prior audit; shares time-series infrastructure with U2-4 (growth themes). Low-hanging optimization of existing AttachedNote.
recommendations.risks: Sentiment inference from text can be wrong; always show the raw note alongside the score. Manual override easy. Sparkline rendering simple (Charts framework).

recommendations.title: Calendar-aware stay-in-touch cadence: don't silently drop a report
recommendations.description: Extend StayConnectedSection (TodayView.swift:96) to compute overdue 1:1s from **both** recorded meetings and calendar events, deriving lastInteractionAt from calendar + recordings + messages, not just recordings. A weekly 1:1 that wasn't recorded still bumps the person's lastInteractionAt (or create a new `lastMetAt` field). This fixes the silent-ghosting bug where a manager had a 1:1 yesterday but the app says the person is overdue because it only counts recordings.
recommendations.userValue: Managers have dozens of unrecorded check-ins; today the app tells them they're drifting from people they saw yesterday. A truthful signal is the difference between the feature being helpful vs actively wrong.
recommendations.noveltyNote: U2-7 from prior audit; confirmed by code inspection showing calendar meetings are now fetched but lastInteractionAt still only counts recordings (WorkspaceIndex.swift).
recommendations.risks: Performance: deriving lastInteractionAt from all signals (meetings, calendar, messages) every time is expensive; pre-compute and cache. Schema: add lastMetAt (calendar-only) alongside lastInteractionAt for clarity.

recommendations.title: Decision & Commitment Ledger: queryable who-owes-what across all meetings
recommendations.description: Add a DecisionStore (parallel to ActionItemStore, Sources/MeetingScribe) that extracts structured decisions + commitments from every meeting summary: who decided what, by when, with what owners. Expose as a queryable "Decision" ledger in TodayView and a "What were we committed to last month?" search. This closes the loop between "the meeting happened" and "something changed because of it."
recommendations.userValue: A manager needs to know: "Did we actually commit to the Q3 roadmap?" "Who owns the hiring plan?" Decisions are the atomic unit of leadership, and today they're buried in summaries with no way to track follow-through.
recommendations.noveltyNote: P1-1 / U2-3 convergence from prior audit; DecisionStore already partially started (decisions: DecisionStore in TodayView, Sources/MeetingScribe/Decisions/DecisionStore.swift exists). Wire it into the extraction pipeline.
recommendations.risks: LLM extraction quality (what counts as a decision vs a note?). Start with high-confidence signals ("decided", "will", "committed", "owner:"). Manual tagging available as fallback.

audits.summary: A personal relationship/second-brain user needs the app to actively support daily maintenance habits (check-ins, logging interactions, drift alerts) and to unlock emotional depth when they track partners, family, and friends. RelationshipType is in place, but the notification/quick-log/coaching layers are incomplete, leaving the relationship-tracking features 40% built out. The highest-leverage gaps are: (1) making encounter logging frictionless enough for daily use, (2) adding proactive notifications so drift detection actually surfaces in the user's workflow, and (3) surface relationship health as visible feedback on Today so logging becomes a habit with measurable progress.

recommendations.title: Implement encounter quick-log widget (kind picker + mood) on person profile
recommendations.description: The current AddEncounterSheet requires typing an 'event name' field, which is friction for daily logging ("grabbed coffee" becomes "Coffee 2026-06"). Add a horizontal strip of kind buttons (call, coffee, dinner, quality-time, difficult-conversation) on the person's profile or in a small popover. Each button auto-generates an encounter with kind set and eventName prefilled. Stores kind as a new optional `EncounterKind` enum on the app-side Encounter model (matching VaultKit.Encounter.Kind). No changes needed to data layer — just wire VaultKit's Kind field into the UI. Files: /Sources/MeetingScribe/People/Encounter.swift (add kind field), PersonDetailView.swift (add quick-log strip above encounters section), AddEncounterSheet.swift (optional simplification for detail-mode).
recommendations.userValue: Logging an interaction drops from 5 taps + required text to 1 tap. This is the difference between a daily habit and a forgotten feature. Direct impact: cadence inference gains real data, drift detection becomes accurate, AI analysis has rich input.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audits (U1-5, U2-2, U3-3) all identified encounter friction as the #1 blocker. This is a direct implementation of those findings. The model layer is ready; this is UI + light model wiring.
recommendations.risks: None. Additive feature, backward-compatible schema change (kind is optional). Test: verify encounter.kind roundtrips through JSON and MCP tools expose it.

recommendations.title: Add relationship check-in notifications (per-person cadence + banner on Today)
recommendations.description: Extend NotificationManager with a new `RELATIONSHIP_CHECKIN` category and a scheduled task that fires daily for each person with relationshipType in [.romanticPartner, .familyMember, .closeFriend]. For each person, check lastInteractionAt vs. checkInCadenceDays (or type's defaultCheckInDays). If overdue, post notification: "Haven't logged time with [Name] in N days — tap to log now." Each notification deep-links to opening the person's profile with the quick-log widget focused. Also add a dismissible banner on TodayView above the upNextCard: "No log with [Name] in 4 days — tap to add." Banner dismisses for 24h via UserDefaults. Files: NotificationManager.swift (add category + scheduler), TodayView.swift (add banner), PersonDetailView.swift (handle deep-link focus).
recommendations.userValue: Without notifications, the entire drift-detection system is passive — only visible if the user opens the app. A push notification is the only signal that works when the user is busy. This closes the habit loop: proactive nudge → one-tap logging → visible streak → dopamine reward.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audits (U1-3, U2-3, U3-4) identified notifications as the missing piece. Notification infrastructure is already solid; this is a new category + scheduler. M effort for high retention impact.
recommendations.risks: Notification spam if checkInCadenceDays is too short (default 1 day for partners could be noisy). Mitigation: respect user's checkInCadenceDays setting, add a Settings toggle to disable, default to conservative cadence.

recommendations.title: Compute and surface relationship health score on person profile and Today strip
recommendations.description: For each person with relationshipType in [.romanticPartner, .familyMember, .closeFriend], compute a lightweight RelationshipHealthScore: (1) Recency component: days since last encounter (lower=better, 0–30 scale); (2) Depth component: avg length of recent encounter notes + memory count (0–20 scale); (3) Streak component: consecutive days with any logged interaction (0–20 scale). Total 0–100. Compute on-the-fly (no stored score) to avoid stale data. Surface: (a) a colored circle arc (green >70, yellow 40–70, red <40) on the person's identity panel, (b) a Today strip below 'up next' showing partner/family contacts ordered by health score (red first). Tap opens the person. Files: PersonDetailView.swift (add computed healthScore property + visual on identity panel), TodayView.swift (add strip), Person.swift (no schema changes).
recommendations.userValue: Visible progress feedback. Users log encounters and immediately see the score move — quantified relationship progress becomes a motivator. This is the reward signal that makes daily logging sustainable.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audits (U1-4) called this out. Health scores are a well-tested retention pattern in habit-tracking apps. Unique here: grounded in real encounter data, not arbitrary achievements.
recommendations.risks: Score computation logic could feel arbitrary or demotivating if poorly tuned. Mitigation: start with transparent formula, make weights tunable in settings, gather user feedback.

recommendations.title: Enable inline quick-edit for relationship type and check-in cadence in person identity panel
recommendations.description: Currently relationship type is a dropdown in the identity panel (good). Check-in cadence is likely buried or requires opening edit sheet. Make both directly editable inline: relationship type as a quick picker (already done), check-in cadence as an editable stepper or text field right below it (e.g., "Check in every 2 days"). Files: PersonDetailView.swift (relationshipTypePicker already exists, extend with inline cadenceField adjacent to it).
recommendations.userValue: Quick adjustments without opening a modal. For a partner, tweaking the cadence from 2 days to 3 days before a trip should be a 2-second edit, not a sheet.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Minor polish on already-implemented feature. Makes the UI cohesive with the inline-edit philosophy already present in identity panel.
recommendations.risks: None. Cosmetic enhancement, no schema or logic changes.

recommendations.title: Reorder person profile sections by relationship type (partner/family first show encounters + health, professional-type hides them)
recommendations.description: PersonDetailView currently renders sections in a fixed order for all relationship types. For partners/family/close-friends (where relationshipType.supportsDepthContent==true), reorder so: identity panel → relationship health score (if present) → encounters → memories → favorites → AI suggestions → messages → relationships → tags → contact (collapsed by default). For colleagues, keep current order. Files: PersonDetailView.swift:241–258 (the body rendering logic) — conditionalize section order on current.relationshipType. The section nav jump-chips (lines 334–351) already support dynamic sectionNavItems.
recommendations.userValue: Emotional relationships use a different mental model than work CRM. Showing encounters and memories first instead of meetings and tasks makes the interface feel like it's designed for you, not a generic contact manager.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U1-2) identified this. No schema changes needed — leverages existing supportsDepthContent flag.
recommendations.risks: Could feel jarring if the reordering is too aggressive. Mitigation: test with actual users, provide an option to customize section order if needed.

recommendations.title: Add relationship-type-aware AI analysis presets (partner: conflict patterns, celebrations, date ideas)
recommendations.description: ConversationAnalysisPreset (PersonDetailView.swift:58–148) has six presets. When relationshipType==.romanticPartner, swap presets: replace 'pending action items' with 'Conflict patterns' and add 'Celebrations & milestones' + 'Date ideas from favorites'. For .familyMember, add 'Life updates' + 'Open loops they've asked me to remember'. Also fix the AI preamble (line 121) from hard-coded "adult professional named Tyler" to be type-aware — for partners say "romantic partners", for family "family members", etc. This ensures the model frames its analysis correctly. Files: PersonDetailView.swift (template method + preset enum).
recommendations.userValue: AI assistance feels personal and grounded in the relationship type, not generic work CRM advice. A prompt to 'suggest a date idea' is something you'd actually use after a hard week; 'suggest pending action items' is noise for a partner.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U1-6) identified this. Low code effort (enum cases + template string changes).
recommendations.risks: None. Pure additive, backward-compatible.

recommendations.title: Add 'On this day' resurface for partner/family memories and encounters
recommendations.description: TodayView already has onThisDaySection (line 87–96) resurfacing past meetings from the same calendar date. Extend this to resurface partner/family encounter memories: "One year ago today: Dinner at [place]. What was going on then?" This surfaces logged encounter notes from M-D matching today's date in prior years. Bonus: if a memory is attached to that date, resurface that too. Files: TodayView.swift (extend onThisDaySection logic to filter encounters by person.relationshipType and month-day match), add a new onThisDayMemoriesSection.
recommendations.userValue: Serendipitous, emotionally rewarding. You log a dinner, a year later the app nudges you to reflect on that moment. Increases engagement and makes the relationship logging feel meaningful beyond the moment.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U1-8) identified this. Straightforward feature, leverages existing encounter data.
recommendations.risks: None. Cosmetic feature, read-only.

recommendations.title: Surface partner/family people as pinnable 'Inner Circle' on Today view
recommendations.description: Add a new 'Inner Circle' persistent Today strip showing the user's partner + close family/close-friends (filtered by relationshipType) in a horizontal scroll, ordered by health-score recency (red/overdue first). Each shows avatar + name + colored status ring (green=recent, yellow=due-soon, red=overdue). Tap opens the profile. This gives a glanceable daily reminder of who to stay connected with. Files: TodayView.swift (add innerCircleSection above or below upNextCard), Person.swift (no changes).
recommendations.userValue: A daily ritual. Open the app, see your partner is overdue (red ring), one tap to log quality time. Without this glanceable surface, drift happens silently.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U3-6) identified this for close-friends; extending to partner/family. Builds on health-score feature.
recommendations.risks: Could feel intrusive if the rings update too frequently. Mitigation: compute health scores once per session, cache the results.

recommendations.title: Implement structured reflection templates for relationship check-ins (weekly, monthly, conflict debrief)
recommendations.description: For partners/family, add a 'New reflection' button that opens a popover with templates: 'Weekly check-in' (What went well / What was hard / What I appreciate / Next week's intention), 'Monthly review' (same 4 + bigger wins/learning), 'Conflict debrief' (What happened / My role / Partner's perspective / Repair action). Each template is a prefilled AttachedNote with kind='weekly-checkin'/'monthly-review'/'conflict-debrief'. Saves as a memory/note on the person. Files: PersonDetailView.swift (add button in memories section for relationshipType.supportsDepthContent), a small new view for the template picker and form.
recommendations.userValue: Structured reflection without requiring a separate journaling app. Builds emotional intelligence and self-awareness in the relationship. These notes become searchable AI context later.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Unique to relationship-focused apps. Prior audit (U1-10) identified this. Coaching frameworks (Gottman repair, attachment theory context) could be layered in later.
recommendations.risks: Could feel overly intrusive or clinical if templates are poorly written. Mitigation: get relationship coach feedback on template language, gather user feedback before shipping.

recommendations.title: Add 'initiated-by' direction and interaction mode to encounters (to/from, duration)
recommendations.description: Encounter model currently has no 'direction' (did the person call you or did you call them?) or duration (how long?). For family/partners, these are relationship-health signals. Add optional fields: initiatedByMe: Bool?, durationMinutes: Int?, interactionMode: enum(phone, video, in-person, text, email). Update AddEncounterSheet to expose these as optional fields. Files: Encounter.swift (add fields), AddEncounterSheet.swift (expose in UI), PersonDetailView.swift (surface direction badge in encounter list).
recommendations.userValue: Over time, spotting patterns ("I always call first", "we video every Sunday", "texts are weekday-only") becomes AI-queryable insight that informs relationship coaching analysis.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Minor data enrichment. Prior audit (U2-12) identified direction as a signal; U2-2 identified interaction mode.
recommendations.risks: Adding optional fields to core model is safe with tolerant decoder already in place.

recommendations.title: MCP tool: get_relationship_health(person_id) returning health score + overdue status + coaching context
