recommendations.description: The MCP server (Sources/MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift) has get_check_in_status (line 909) and log_encounter (line 895). Add or extend get_relationship_health(person_id) to return: {health_score: 0–100, last_encounter_date, days_since, cadence_days, overdue: bool, recent_encounters: [encounter summary], upcoming_events: [birthdays, anniversaries], coaching_context: string}. This exposes the health computation to Claude so the AI can proactively say "You're overdue with your mom" or "Your partner seems stressed lately based on your notes." Files: Sources/MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift (implement tool handler + DTO).
recommendations.userValue: Claude becomes a relationship coach. The AI can surface insights and nudge you proactively based on your own vault data, without needing to open the app.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U1-7, U2-9) identified MCP extensions for relationship data. MCP infrastructure is mature; this is a new tool handler.
recommendations.risks: None. Read-only tool, additive.

recommendations.title: Fix ConversationAnalysisPreset preamble to use actual user identity instead of hard-coded 'Tyler'
recommendations.description: PersonDetailView.swift line 121–134 has prompt templates that hard-code "adult professional named Tyler" in the preamble. This is both unsafe (leaks a name into every local LLM call) and wrong (doesn't match the user's actual name or the relationship type context). Add an AppSettings field for userName (populated on onboarding), and pass it + relationshipType to template(). Template should render: "You are an adult discussing [their partner/family member/friend] named [PersonName]. Your role is to help reflect on [relationship-specific coaching goal]." Files: AppSettings (add userName field), PersonDetailView (pass userName + relationshipType to template), ConversationAnalysisPreset.template method.
recommendations.userValue: Correct AI framing + privacy. The AI should reason about YOUR relationships using YOUR name (or generic), not a baked-in name from the developer.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U1 & U5) flagged this as both a safety and correctness issue. Quick fix, high precedent.
recommendations.risks: None. Pure improvement, backward-compatible.

recommendations.title: Add relationship-type-aware color/emoji accent throughout UI (badges on people list, Today strip, graph clusters)
recommendations.description: RelationshipType already has emoji and colorName. Lean on these throughout: (a) Person list rows: prepend emoji badge (💑 for partner, 👨‍👩‍👧 for family, etc.) before the name; (b) Today strip: color the inner-circle cards by type; (c) People graph: add layout mode or node styling by type (all partners cluster with color rose, families with color amber, etc.). Files: PersonRow (prepend emoji), TodayView.innerCircleSection (color cards), PeopleGraphView (type-aware node styling).
recommendations.userValue: Visual differentiation so you instantly know a person's role in your life. Faster cognitive load when scanning the people list or Today.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Design polish leveraging existing relationship type infrastructure.
recommendations.risks: None. Cosmetic.

recommendations.title: Create onboarding/tutorial overlay explaining relationship types and the check-in habit loop (record → log → drift alert → reconnect)
recommendations.description: For first-time users, after the permissions onboarding, add a 2–3 screen post-onboarding tour: Screen 1 — "MeetingScribe tracks two things: meetings (auto-recorded) and relationships (you log). This is the relationship side."; Screen 2 — "Add your partner, family, and close friends. The app will remind you if you drift."; Screen 3 — "Every day, check in: tap a quick-log button (coffee ☕ call 📞 quality-time 🛋️) and the app learns your cadence. No writing required." Then dismiss. This only shows on first launch. Files: OnboardingSheet (extend with relationship tour), AppSettings (track hasSeenRelationshipTour).
recommendations.userValue: Prevents the 'What am I supposed to do with the People tab?' confusion. Sets correct mental model: this is a relationship habit-tracker, not a CRM.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Pure UX onboarding. Prior audit (USER-5) flagged onboarding clarity gaps; this is the relationship-specific context.
recommendations.risks: None. Optional, dismissible.

recommendations.title: Add weekly digest notification for family/partner check-in cadence compliance (Sunday evening, opt-in)
recommendations.description: Extend NotificationManager with a weekly family check-in digest (Sunday 6pm, configurable). Content: list of family/partner people overdue for contact (by checkInCadenceDays), their last contact date, upcoming birthdays this week, and a single "Catch up" button that opens a 'check-in wizard' sheet listing all overdue people with quick-log buttons. This is a ritual-building nudge, not a reactive alert. Files: NotificationManager (add weekly digest scheduler), TodayView or new CatchUpSheet (add the wizard).
recommendations.userValue: A Sunday evening habit: open notification, spend 30 min catching up with 2–3 people. This is the habit-loop closure that turns relationship-tracking from passive to proactive.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior audit (U2-10) identified weekly digest as a habit-formation tool. Extends existing notification infrastructure.
recommendations.risks: Weekly digests can feel spammy. Mitigation: make it opt-in, default off, respect user's notification settings.

audits.summary: A cross-device/on-the-go user needs vault + capture from phone, quick voice notes, review away from the Mac, sync trust, and glanceable status. MeetingScribe has a working web UI on the same WiFi and Tailscale, one-way sync infrastructure in place, but lacks intuitive phone affordances, mobile-optimized voice capture, a reliable "what's new" glance mechanism, and missing integrations (iPhone Shortcuts, system-wide quick actions). The prior plans address transport and backend ingestion; this audit surfaces the friction points a real mobile-first user would hit daily.

recommendations.title: iPhone Shortcuts for vault capture (Voice Note, Quick Note, Action Item, Add Person)
recommendations.description: Implement the four iOS Shortcuts documented in MASTER_PLAN_V2.md and REMAINING_WORK.md §3. Each writes a timestamped JSON envelope to iCloud Drive / MeetingScribeVault / _inbox/ (the contract is already in VaultKit and wired via iCloudInboxWatcher). Shortcut 1: 'Quick Note' (Siri voice → transcribe, title, create vault note); Shortcut 2: 'Action Item' (voice → parse as task, assign to person if named); Shortcut 3: 'Add Person' (name + email + phone fields + create); Shortcut 4: 'Voice Note' (raw 15-min Siri recording → vault). Each gets a Siri phrase (especially 'Note that' and 'Add person'). Export .shortcut files to Shortcuts/ directory in repo for reproducibility. Test via iCloudInboxWatcher log to confirm _inbox/processed ledger updates.
recommendations.userValue: User is in a meeting or at a coffee table: speaks 'Hey Siri, note that we need to fix the signup flow' → it lands in their vault as an inbox item, picked up on the Mac within 15 min when they next open MeetingScribe. No need to open the web app or dig for notes. Removes the friction of 'I heard something important and have no hands to capture it.'
recommendations.noveltyNote: This closes a gap in the prior CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN.md which handles Mac↔Mac sync but leaves mobile capture to the web UI (limited, no Siri). Shortcuts are native iOS, frictionless, and reuse existing _inbox infrastructure.
recommendations.dependencies: iCloudInboxWatcher already wired; needs iPhone Shortcuts app authoring (client-side, not in this Swift codebase).

recommendations.title: Quick-access 'What's new' dashboard on the phone web UI (glanceable sync status)
recommendations.description: Add a new `/api/dashboard/whatsnew` endpoint (WebAPI.swift) returning the last 24h of: newly transcribed meetings, new action items marked in last sync, updated people matched to work device, new vault entries. Render on phone's home tab with a sticky time-since-last-sync badge at the top. Include: meeting title + 2-line summary (clipped), unchecked action items count, 'X new people' chip, and sync freshness (e.g., '5 min ago' or 'Synced 14:32'). The badge changes color if synced >1h ago (amber) or never (red). Mobile user pulls up the phone, glances at the summary, sees 'Synced 5 min ago · 2 new meetings · 7 action items', immediately knows whether they're caught up.
recommendations.userValue: On-the-go user needs 1-second awareness of 'is my Mac's work in here yet?' and 'what happened since I last checked?' Without this, they have to drill into Meetings or Tasks to confirm—friction. The glance is the job-to-be-done.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior docs (CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN) assume the merge is silent; this surfaces it as a first-class 'inbox' concept on the phone. Reuses the existing `/api/health` ledger pattern.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (rsync/Syncthing) to deposit files; Phase 2 merge engine optional but makes 'new people' meaningful.

recommendations.title: System-wide quick actions for voice capture (mac + iOS Shortcuts integration)
recommendations.description: Expose iPhone Shortcuts via iOS Quick Actions (long-press on MeetingScribe app icon) and macOS Services (⌘K command palette + 'Quick Capture' menu). iOS: register the four Shortcuts as Quick Actions (iOS 17+); macOS: add a 'Voice Note' and 'Action Item' service in the app menu so a user in a full-screen app can hit a hotkey (e.g., ⌃⌘N) and post a note without switching windows. Wire to the existing QuickNotesController. For the voice case, open the Whisper-style F5 dictation or a floating input sheet if Accessibility is enabled.
recommendations.userValue: User is in a Slack or email, hears something important: on iOS hit long-press → 'Quick Note' → done in 5 sec, no app switch. On Mac, a single hotkey captures a voice note from anywhere without leaving the current window. Removes the friction of app-switching or context-loss when inspiration strikes.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Complements the Shortcuts; makes Shortcuts + app integrated instead of siloed. Not in prior plans.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires iPhone Shortcuts feature (above) + QuickNotesController (already exists, Sources/MeetingScribe/Meeting/QuickNotesController.swift).

recommendations.title: Phone web UI: streamlined async task capture with voice input (mobile-first flow)
recommendations.description: Enhance the web UI's task creation (`/api/tasks POST`, WebAPI.swift) to accept audio chunks streamed from the browser's MediaRecorder API. Add a mobile-only UI in WebAssets.swift: floating microphone button (similar to MeetingRecordDock) visible on the phone when viewing the Tasks tab. Tapping it opens a 30-sec countdown + waveform visualization; releasing posts raw audio to `/api/tasks/from-audio?project=<id>`, which the backend transcribes via the existing Whisper + summarizes via Ollama, then creates the task with auto-title. No server-side Whisper needed on the phone; the audio ships to the Mac's server, processed in-place, and the task appears in the phone's list within 5 sec.
recommendations.userValue: User is at the gym, parking lot, or waiting in line, opens the phone app, taps the microphone, says 'Schedule 1:1 with Sarah for next week' — task is created and linked to Sarah's contact. No typing, no context-switching, works offline until sync.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Extends the web API to handle audio (already capable via the token auth); requires browser-side code (WebAssets HTML/JS) but no new Swift endpoints. The CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC plan focuses on sync; this is about *capture*—a distinct job.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires working phone web UI (PHONE_ACCESS.md is done); Whisper + Ollama already available on the Mac running the server.

recommendations.title: Trust dashboard: cross-device sync health & conflict quarantine UI
recommendations.description: Add a Settings section (SettingsView.swift) mirroring PhoneAccessSection that shows sync health: 'Last sync 2h ago from work-macbook (87 files)' with a progress bar, and a clickable 'Conflicts' link that opens RemoteImportSettingsView (from Phase 2 merge engine). The phone web UI mirrors this in the dashboard header: '[Hub] Last received: work-macbook 3h ago' + link to inspect import ledger (if merge enabled). The goal is to surface what's happening in `_remote/` so the user never wonders 'did it actually sync?' or 'why isn't my work meeting showing up?' Implement via the existing CrossDeviceSyncController (already reads `.last_sync` heartbeat) and extend it to surface ledger stats (conflict count, items merged).
recommendations.userValue: On-the-go user needs to trust the sync. They walk into a meeting with their phone, want to glance and know 'my Mac's work is already in here' or 'it's still uploading, refresh in 5 min.' Without trust, they'll manually re-sync or keep both tabs open, losing the point of the feature.
recommendations.noveltyNote: The CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN documents the transport layer (rsync, Syncthing) but leaves observability to command-line logs. This is the user-facing equivalent: 'is it working?' at a glance.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (rsync/Syncthing) + Phase 2 (merge engine for ledger stats). CrossDeviceSyncController already has the scaffold.

recommendations.title: Mobile-optimized meeting review flow: inline summary + next action button
recommendations.description: Rework the phone web UI's UnifiedMeetingDetail to match the Mac's summary-first, inline action-item pattern. Currently (WebAPI.swift + WebAssets), the phone UI shows tabs (Summary/Transcript/Chat) but the action items are buried at the bottom of the Summary tab. Move them to a sticky action-items pane above the summary on mobile (<600px), with a floating 'Add action item' button. Add a 'Next steps' section (people to follow up with, tasks created from this meeting). Add a 'Share / Draft reply' button that POSTs to `/api/tasks` with `fromMeeting=<id>` and pre-fills the task with the summary (reuses the existing task API). This mirrors the Mac's 'Draft follow-up' button but designed for mobile: one tap to add a task, one more to edit it inline.
recommendations.userValue: User walks out of a meeting, pulls out phone, wants to: (1) glance at the summary, (2) check the action items, (3) create a follow-up task if needed. Today they have to scroll through the summary to find action items and then navigate to Tasks to create a task—three context switches. With this, it's one view with everything visible.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Adapts the Mac's UX (which UX audit confirmed is valuable) to mobile dimensions. Not a separate feature, but a mobile-first layout of existing endpoints.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires existing web API endpoints; changes to WebAssets.appHTML layout only.

recommendations.title: Bidirectional sync option: work Mac ↔ hub for live editing on the go
recommendations.description: Extend the CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN Phase 1 (currently work → hub one-way) to optionally support hub → work pulls, gated by a config flag `ALLOW_HUB_TO_WORK=true` (off by default). When a user edits a person, completes a task, or adds a note on the phone (via web UI), those changes land on the hub's live vault. Add a daily pull-back to the work Mac (e.g., a second rsync pass that mirrors `~/Documents/MeetingNotes/<hub-specific-tags>/` back down, or a Syncthing receive folder for hub-only updates). Document the merge rules (work changes to shared records are preserved, hub-only tasks/people are new). This lets a user trust the phone as a real editing surface, not just a read-only viewer.
recommendations.userValue: User is on the go, opens phone web UI, marks a task done that they just completed in the meeting. Today: it marks done on the phone but the work Mac still has it open. With bidirectional sync, the check-off flows back to the work Mac within an hour, and the two stay in sync. The user's second brain feels unified instead of fragmented.
recommendations.noveltyNote: The CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC plans are fundamentally one-way (protect the hub). This is an optional, lower-friction variant for users who trust their work Mac and want mobile editing. Opt-in flag means no risk to the default install.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (rsync transport) and Phase 2 (merge engine dedup logic). Adds a second launchd job on work Mac (pull every 12h) and a second transport pass.

recommendations.title: Notification relay for cross-device events (meeting ready, task assigned, person added)
recommendations.description: When a Mac detects a 'meeting ready' (transcription done) or 'person matched from work device' or 'task assigned to you', if the work Mac is configured for sync, push a summary to the hub's notification ledger. The iPhone can poll that ledger (or subscribe via WebSocket) and post iOS notifications. Implement via a new `/api/notifications/pending` endpoint (GET) and optional long-poll or WebSocket upgrade for real-time. The Mac MeetingManager posts to the ledger when it fires a local `NotificationManager.postMeetingReady()`; the phone queries every 30 sec. Include a deep link so tapping the notification opens the meeting/task in the web UI. This closes the job-to-be-done: 'I left my MacBook at the office, now I'm in another meeting—I want to know that the recordings are done transcribing so I can review them before standup.'
recommendations.userValue: On-the-go user is in a different meeting, phones notifies them 'Meeting with Product is ready (summary + 5 action items)' → tap → opens the summary on phone. No need to ask 'did it finish?' or dig into settings.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Not in prior plans; extends the phone-as-viewer model to push events. Requires a modest new API endpoint and browser-side polling but reuses existing notification logic from the Mac.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (sync established); the web server (HTTPServer.swift) already supports long-polling patterns via the existing 16 MB request handling.

recommendations.title: iPad-optimized web UI layout (landscape + split-view ready)
recommendations.description: Adapt WebAssets.appHTML for iPad's larger screen and landscape orientation. Currently the phone web UI is a single-column bottom-nav design; iPad can use a two-pane layout: sidebar (Meetings/People/Tasks) + detail. Render a landscape-aware Flexbox layout in CSS (check viewport width / orientation, hide bottom nav if width >900px and show a sidebar instead). Add responsive touch targets (48pt minimum) for iPad's less-forgiving touch. Test on iPad Pro and iPad Air to confirm readability and usability. This is a CSS-only change to WebAssets, no server changes needed.
recommendations.userValue: User has an iPad and wants to use it away from the Mac: today they get a phone-sized interface crammed into a 11-inch screen with wasted space. With iPad layout, they get a real two-pane experience and can see meetings + details side-by-side.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Low-hanging fruit; the web UI is already responsive-capable. Just needs responsive CSS and testing. Not in prior plans because they assume iPhone-first.
recommendations.dependencies: None; CSS changes only.

recommendations.title: Offline-first mobile web app (service worker + local IndexedDB cache)
recommendations.description: Register a service worker in WebAssets.appHTML to cache the app shell (HTML/CSS/JS) and API responses. When the phone loses connectivity (e.g., between meetings, spotty conference WiFi), the app remains usable: user can still browse cached meetings/tasks/people, mark tasks as done (optimistically, queued for sync when connection returns). Implement via a simple service worker that: (1) caches the app HTML on first load, (2) mirrors GET responses to localStorage/IndexedDB keyed by URL + timestamp, (3) on POST (task updates), stores them locally and re-sends when online. The backend already handles idempotent updates (schema version + hashing), so re-sends are safe. This removes the friction of 'I'm reviewing a meeting and my WiFi dropped.'
recommendations.userValue: User is on a flight, subway, or in a Zoom waiting room with patchy WiFi: they open the phone web app, can still read their meetings and mark tasks done offline. When they reconnect, changes sync silently. The app feels reliable instead of brittle.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Service workers are standard web tech; this is an enhancement to the existing web UI. Not in prior plans because cross-device docs focus on sync, not offline UX.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires minimal server changes (the existing /api/* endpoints already support idempotent updates via their schema design). Mostly browser-side code in WebAssets.

recommendations.title: macOS Focus mode integration: silence transcription notifications during deep work
recommendations.description: Hook into macOS Focus modes (System Settings → Focus) so users can create a 'Deep Work' or 'Focus' focus with MeetingScribe's notifications silenced. Currently (NotificationManager.swift), meetings-ready notifications post unconditionally, interrupting a user mid-task. Add a check: before posting a notification, query `NSWorkspace.shared` for the active focus mode (macOS 12+); if the user is in a focus that excludes MeetingScribe, post a silent notification (badge-only, no sound/banner) or queue it for later. Also add a Settings toggle: 'Respect macOS Focus modes' (default on). This is a small UX polish that feels native.
recommendations.userValue: User sets a 'Deep Focus' mode for deep work. A meeting finishes transcribing while they're focused; instead of an interrupting banner, it posts a silent notification that lands in Notification Center. When they exit Focus, they see 'Meeting with Product is ready' and can dive in. Removes friction caused by well-intentioned notifications at the wrong time.
recommendations.noveltyNote: A small, polishing feature that modern Mac apps support. Not in prior plans but fits 'best-in-class' category.
recommendations.dependencies: NotificationManager.swift already exists; requires macOS 12+ (app already targets 14+).

recommendations.title: Phone web UI dark mode awareness + system color scheme sync
recommendations.description: Update WebAssets.appHTML to respect the user's system dark/light preference (prefers-color-scheme media query). Currently the web UI hardcodes dark mode (CSS `color-scheme: dark`). Add a light-mode color palette and toggle in the web UI settings, with persistence via localStorage. Also add a 'Match system' default so the phone's web app switches to light or dark based on iOS Settings → Display & Brightness. This is a pure CSS + localStorage change, no server-side logic needed.
recommendations.userValue: User prefers light mode on their iPhone; the web app is blinding in daylight. With system awareness, the app matches the rest of their phone's UI and they can actually see it outdoors.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Standard modern web practice. Not in prior plans but expected by modern users.
recommendations.dependencies: None; CSS + localStorage only.

recommendations.title: Merge engine: fuzzy dedup on person names and emails for cross-device matches
recommendations.description: Enhance the Phase 2 merge engine (RemoteVaultIngestor, CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC_MASTER_PLAN §9) to use PeopleStore's existing fuzzy-match logic when merging work-device people into the hub. Currently the engine matches on exact email/phone/name (strong) and fuzzy ≥0.85 (auto-merge). Extend to offer fuzzy-scored suggestions (0.6–0.85) when a work contact is similar-but-not-quite to a hub contact (e.g., 'John Smith' vs 'J. Smith', or 'john@company.com' with hub's 'john.s@company.com'). Queue these as PersonSuggestion tasks on Today so the user can manually confirm the merge before it happens. This prevents silently creating duplicate 'John Smith' records when one already exists.
recommendations.userValue: Work Mac records a meeting with 'john-s@company.com'; hub has 'john.smith@company.com' already linked to a Person with 15 memories. Without fuzzy matching, a duplicate person is created. With it, the user sees a suggestion: 'Merge work-recorded John Smith with existing John Smith?' → confirm → they're one record with 30 memories. Ensures the 'second brain' stays unified.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Improves the Phase 2 merge engine from the prior CROSS_DEVICE_SYNC plan. Is a deepening of existing logic (PeopleStore already has fuzzy match), not a new concept.
recommendations.dependencies: Requires Phase 2 (merge engine) to be built; reuses PeopleStore.fuzzyMatch and PersonSuggestion UI (already exist).

audits.summary: MeetingScribe has quietly closed its single biggest 2026 parity gap: cross-meeting "ask your vault" RAG now exists (ChatSession.groundedSystemPrompt + searchVaultHybrid + GlobalSearch "Ask Chat"), and speaker-labeled transcripts are surfaced. But it is still grounded only on summaries, and it misses the in-meeting layer the whole category now competes on — Granola's hybrid scratchpad (your typed notes, AI-enhanced) and mid-call "catch me up / recap last 5 min" — plus conversation intelligence (talk-time), recurring-series memory, calendar auto-record, and a unified review-then-execute action fan-out. The net-new bets below turn the local-first/no-bot moat into capabilities the cloud field structurally can't match.

recommendations.title: In-meeting hybrid scratchpad: type during the call, AI enhances after
recommendations.description: Add a live note-taking surface to the active-meeting dock/overlay (MeetingRecordDock.swift today only offers 'Open & add notes'; FloatingOverlay.swift is voice-note only). Give the user a TextEditor scratchpad while recording; persist keystrokes with rough timestamps alongside the transcript. On finalize, the OllamaService summary pass merges the user's typed bullets (treated as priority/emphasis signals) with the AI notes — render the user's words and AI-added context distinctly in MeetingSummaryTab (Granola shows user notes in black, AI in gray). This is the single most-copied Granola mechanic and MeetingScribe has no equivalent.
recommendations.userValue: Lets users stay present and jot only what matters; AI fills the rest. The hybrid (human emphasis + AI completeness) beats pure-AI or pure-manual notes, which is exactly why Granola users stick.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: every prior comp doc (C1, C4) focused on POST-meeting RAG/recall and explicitly noted live capture but never proposed the in-meeting scratchpad. The app's dock has no scratchpad field today (verified MeetingRecordDock.swift).

recommendations.title: Mid-call 'catch me up' recap on the active meeting
recommendations.description: During a live recording, add a one-tap 'Recap last 5 min' / 'What did I miss?' action in the dock that runs the local Ollama model over the rolling 5-min-chunk transcript already produced by the live pipeline and shows a concise summary inline — without stopping capture. Reuse OllamaChatClient + the existing chunked transcript. Granola and Notion both shipped mid-call recap in 2026; MeetingScribe does live chunk transcription but surfaces no live insight (confirmed: prior C1 doc flagged 'no live insight surface').
recommendations.userValue: Walk into a call late or zone out and instantly catch up — the assistant-on-the-call feeling, fully on-device with zero bot in the room.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: prior docs noted the absence of live assistance but proposed no concrete mechanic. Builds on the existing 5-min live transcription chunks.

recommendations.title: Ground vault-chat on transcripts + people, not just summaries, with scope chips and citations
recommendations.description: ChatSession.groundedSystemPrompt() already retrieves via searchVaultHybrid but only injects meeting *summaries* (prefix 1200) for top-5 meetings — it drops transcript bodies, attached notes, voice notes, and people. Extend retrieval to chunk and rank transcript/notes/person passages, add scope chips in the chat UI (this meeting · this person · this tag · everything), and render each answer's source links inline (meetingscribe:// already produced). This converts the half-built RAG into the full Otter-CMI / Granola-folder-chat experience, local-only.
recommendations.userValue: 'Where did we decide X?' archaeology returns exact, cited answers across the whole vault — and uniquely without a byte leaving the Mac.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-1/C4-1 but those are now PARTIALLY DONE (C2-2 retrieve-then-ground shipped); this is the specific, verified next step (summaries-only + no scope chips, per ChatSession.swift:243-260).

recommendations.title: Conversation intelligence: talk-time ratio + topic timeline (on-device)
recommendations.description: Diarization is now surfaced as speaker-labeled, color-coded transcript (MeetingTranscriptTab.swift:87 'Me'/other), but nothing quantifies it. Add an analytics tab to UnifiedMeetingDetail computing talk-to-listen ratio per speaker from the diarized segments, a per-segment word/time breakdown, and a topic timeline (cluster segments via on-device NLEmbedding). No talkTime/sentiment computation exists today (verified grep). This is the conversation-intelligence layer Read.ai/Fireflies/tl;dv gate behind paid tiers.
recommendations.userValue: 'Did I dominate that 1:1?' / 'How long on pricing?' — insight competitors charge for, delivered privately from data already on disk.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C1-2 proposed this when diarization was UNSURFACED; diarization is now shipped, so this is the cheap next unlock (just the analytics computation), materially smaller than originally scoped.

recommendations.title: Recurring-meeting series memory: thread context across sessions
recommendations.description: PreMeetingBriefView pulls prior meetings by shared *attendees* (priorMeetings, attendee-based), not by recurring series. Use EKEvent recurrence/series identity (CalendarService) to group a recurring meeting's instances, then auto-carry forward open action items, recurring topics, and last-meeting decisions into the pre-meeting brief and into a per-series rollup view. Granola explicitly 'tracks conversation threads across recurring sessions' in 2026; MeetingScribe has the calendar data but no series grouping.
recommendations.userValue: Weekly standups/1:1s become a continuous thread — 'last week you committed to X, still open' — instead of disconnected notes.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: C1-6 proposed cross-recurring *digest reports*; this is the operational series-identity grouping + brief carry-forward, a different and prerequisite mechanic. No prior doc used EKEvent recurrence.

recommendations.title: Calendar-driven auto-record (opt-in) with pre-roll buffer
recommendations.description: AmbientMeetingDetector only *prompts* after sustained mic use (AmbientMeetingDetector.swift:83), and CalendarService marks events isImpromptu:false but never auto-starts. Add an opt-in 'auto-record my calendar meetings' mode: when a calendar event with a video link starts (and the user has armed it), begin capture automatically with a short rolling pre-roll buffer so the first 30s aren't lost. Granola/Notion both auto-open on calendar-detected meeting start in 2026; this is now table-stakes for a frictionless notetaker.
recommendations.userValue: Never forget to hit record — coverage of the meetings users currently miss — while keeping it strictly opt-in and bot-free.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: prior docs covered ambient *detection/prompting* and continuous always-on (C4-3), but not calendar-event-triggered auto-start with pre-roll. Distinct from C4-3's life-bounded mode.

recommendations.title: Unified review-then-execute action fan-out card after finalize
recommendations.description: The primitives are scattered: FollowUpGeneratorService drafts email/Slack, Linear/Notion sync exists, EventKit + People writes exist. Compose them into ONE post-finalize 'action card' in UnifiedMeetingDetail: proposed action items (push to Linear/Notion), a follow-up email draft (open in Mail), a 'schedule next' EventKit event, and new-attendees→add-to-People — each a checkbox the user approves, then one-click executes. Zoom 3.0/Fathom auto-fan-out without consent; MeetingScribe's local LLM proposes, the human approves.
recommendations.userValue: Collapses ~15 min of post-meeting busywork into one approval — agentic, but consent-safe and local.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-3; still not built (FollowUpGeneratorService drafts only; no unified card verified). Worth keeping — now with the exact existing services to orchestrate.

recommendations.title: Per-tag / per-meeting-type summary templates wired into the summary pass
recommendations.description: NoteTemplate.swift exists (id/name/body, NoteTemplate.all) but is a manual scratch-note template, NOT bound to the AI summary pass (no template reference in MeetingPipelineController or OllamaService, verified). Add a Template model that maps a tag or auto-detected meeting type (1:1/sales/interview/standup) to a structured summary prompt, store templates as editable .md files in vault/_templates/ so they're shareable, and pick the right one automatically from calendar metadata. Granola's 29+ templates + Recipes is its most-cited stickiness feature.
recommendations.userValue: Right, repeatable output format per meeting type with zero manual selection — and templates are vault-native, version-controllable, shareable.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-7/C3-9 (file-based shareable templates) — verified still unbuilt for the SUMMARY pass (NoteTemplate is scratch-note only). Adds auto-selection from calendar (C1-8) as one item.

recommendations.title: Slide/screen-frame capture into the transcript timeline
recommendations.description: MeetingScribe already holds Screen Recording permission via ScreenCaptureKit for audio. On screen-share/slide-change (frame-diff heuristic), grab a frame, OCR locally with Vision, and pin image+text into the meeting at its timestamp; make it searchable via the existing FTS index. OtterPilot 3.0 captures slides/whiteboards into notes — audio-only notetakers lose decks entirely. The capability is one frame-grab away.
recommendations.userValue: 'What was on slide 4?' — shared decks and whiteboards become searchable artifacts, not lost context.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-4; verified still unbuilt (no Vision/frame-capture in pipeline). Kept because the permission is already held, making it unusually cheap.

recommendations.title: Provable privacy panel + copy-paste consent helper
recommendations.description: Build a Settings 'Privacy' surface that states the moat concretely: '0 bytes left this Mac · transcribed on-device (whisper.cpp) · summarized on-device (Ollama) · vault at <path> · audio retained N days'. Add a one-click meeting-consent script the user can paste into a calendar invite or say aloud. The 2026 market reorganized around bot-free/all-party-consent (NYC Bar Formal Op 2025-6, Brewer v. Otter.ai); MeetingScribe is MORE private than Granola (which still transcribes in the cloud) yet says so nowhere.
recommendations.userValue: Turns the invisible local-first advantage into the explicit reason to choose this over the entire cloud field — and gives the user a defensible consent record.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-5/C4-7. Still unbuilt and now even more pointed given Granola's cloud-transcription stance — low effort, high positioning leverage.

recommendations.title: Retention & 'right to forget': audio-vs-transcript TTL + hard-delete
recommendations.description: The vault grows unbounded. Add per-vault retention controls ('delete raw audio after N days, keep transcript', 'auto-purge meetings older than 1 year') and a one-click 'Forget this meeting/person' that hard-deletes across the vault folder, the FTS5 index (searchVaultHybrid source), and the people graph. Limitless ships custom audio-retention windows; a forgetting primitive is both a privacy differentiator and storage hygiene, and a prerequisite for any future always-on mode.
recommendations.userValue: Genuine privacy guarantee competitors mostly fake, plus reclaimed disk — 'your data, deletable on demand'.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C4-5; verified no retention/forget primitive exists. Pairs naturally with the privacy panel above.

recommendations.title: On-device meeting coaching (opt-in): monologue, filler, question ratio
recommendations.description: Building on the new talk-time analytics, add an optional post-meeting local coaching pass: monologue detection (longest unbroken speaker run), filler-word count, question-asked ratio, and a one-line nudge ('you spoke 78% of your 1:1'). Entirely on-device — the privacy-safe version of Fireflies Live Assist / tl;dv Coaching Hub / Read.ai meeting coach, none of which can run without uploading your voice.
recommendations.userValue: Self-improvement insight users currently can't get without sending their voice to a vendor.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-9 but now sequenced on top of the just-shipped diarization + the talk-time item, making it a focused, cheap add rather than a from-scratch module.

recommendations.title: Decision & commitment ledger across the vault
recommendations.description: Add a dedicated extraction pass that pulls *decisions* and *commitments* (distinct from action items, which already exist via ActionItem.swift) into a vault-wide, searchable ledger with source-meeting citations. Surface as a 'Decisions' view and expose via the MCP server and the grounded chat. Otter CMI advertises 'consensus on Q3 budget'; Fathom 'locate past decisions instantly' — this is the persistent structured artifact, not just an ephemeral chat answer.
recommendations.userValue: The institutional-memory layer — 'every decision about pricing this quarter' as a durable, citable list.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces prior C1-11; verified no decisions/commitments extraction exists (only ActionItem). Distinct from action items and complements the now-shipped RAG.

recommendations.title: Pull web answers into chat via an optional local search tool
recommendations.description: Add an opt-in 'web lookup' tool to the chat tool set (ChatTools.swift) so a user can ask a question that mixes their vault with a current web fact, gated behind an explicit toggle that makes the single outbound call legible (preserving the local-first default). Fireflies shipped 'Talk to Fireflies' (Perplexity-powered in-meeting web Q&A) in early 2026 and it's becoming an expectation; MeetingScribe's chat is currently vault-only.
recommendations.userValue: Answers that blend 'what we discussed' with 'what's true now' without leaving the app — with privacy kept as the explicit default.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: no prior doc proposed web-augmented chat (they all leaned purely local). Grounded in Fireflies' 2026 Perplexity integration; designed to keep the outbound call opt-in and visible.

audits.summary: Since the prior C2 audit, MeetingScribe shipped most of the recall moat: EmbeddingService + hybrid semantic/FTS search, per-meeting embeddings, related-meeting discovery, a cross-meeting DecisionStore ledger, "On this day" resurfacing, and crucially an Obsidian-native vault (YAML frontmatter + [[wikilinks]] + #tags + a Daily/ note writer). That makes it competitive on capture AND on basic recall. The remaining gaps vs Tana/Capacities/Mem/Reflect (2026): a still people-only knowledge graph, MCP that exposes write tools but zero recall/graph/backlink primitives, no [[ autocomplete or in-app daily-note journal, decisions parsed only by markdown-heading scrape with no speaker attribution, no standing/structured query layer, and an unexploited "your vault IS an Obsidian vault" interop story no AI notetaker competitor can match.

recommendations.title: Promote meetings, projects & topics to first-class graph nodes
recommendations.description: PeopleGraphViewModel.buildGraph (Sources/MeetingScribe/People/Graph/PeopleGraphViewModel.swift:38) still builds nodes exclusively from Person and edges only from shared meetings/tags (var nodes: [PersonNode]). Generalize PersonNode to a GraphNode with a kind enum (person/meeting/project/topicTag/decision), feed in meeting nodes (attendance edges), project nodes (ActionItems/Project.swift), and topic/tag nodes. Reuse the existing force layout + BFS find-path unchanged. Add a node-kind toggle row to GraphFilterBar.swift (which today only has resetFilters). DecisionStore and per-meeting embeddings give you ready-made mention edges.
recommendations.userValue: See the whole cluster around a project or topic in one canvas — who, which meetings, which decisions — not just person-to-person. The Capacities/Tana 'knowledge network' view; the connection layer alone no longer differentiates without it.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-5 (verified still people-only) but now far cheaper because embeddings + DecisionStore + DailyNoteWriter wikilinks already exist to supply edges.
recommendations.dependencies: PeopleGraphViewModel, Project.swift, DecisionStore
recommendations.risks: Force layout perf with many node kinds; cap node count / cluster by kind.

recommendations.title: Expose recall to Claude: semantic-search, backlinks & find-path MCP tools
recommendations.description: MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift ships 23 tools that are all write/structured-lookup (add_person, log_encounter, get_summary, list_meetings...) and ZERO recall primitives. Add: search_vault(query, k) routing to PeopleStore's hybrid FTS+embedding path (EmbeddingService.embed + cosine) returning ranked passages with meetingscribe:// + [[wikilink]] citations; get_backlinks(entity) over WorkspaceIndex.backlinks; find_path(personA, personB) over the graph BFS; get_decisions(filter) over DecisionStore.
recommendations.userValue: 'Claude, what's the through-line across my last five Acme calls?' or 'who connects me to Priya?' works from any MCP client with cited answers — turning the write-capable MCP into a read/synthesize brain.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-10 (verified still missing in main.swift) but now buildable as thin wrappers since EmbeddingService/DecisionStore/graph BFS all landed since the prior audit.
recommendations.dependencies: EmbeddingService, PeopleStore hybrid search, DecisionStore, PeopleGraphViewModel
recommendations.risks: Long passages blow context; cap k and snippet length.

recommendations.title: Daily Note as a live in-app journal, not just a markdown file
recommendations.description: DailyNoteWriter.swift only appends meeting wikilinks to <vault>/Daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md between HTML guards; nothing in Sources/MeetingScribe/UI references it. Add a DailyNoteView rendering today's Daily/*.md in MarkdownEditor with a free-write journal area above the managed meeting block, auto-populated with open action items and the 'On this day' resurfacing already computed in TodayView.swift:257. Make Today's hero open into this editable note; index it in vault_content so it's searchable.
recommendations.userValue: A durable, searchable, linkable journal of each day — the habit loop Reflect/Tana/Capacities center on. Today the file exists but the user can never see or write in it inside the app.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-4 but the gap narrowed to a precise cheap fix: the file format and writer already exist (DailyNoteWriter) — only the editable in-app surface is missing.
recommendations.dependencies: DailyNoteWriter, MarkdownEditor, TodayView, ActionItemStore
recommendations.risks: Two-way edit must respect the HTML-comment managed block so regeneration doesn't clobber free text.

recommendations.title: [[ wikilink and @person autocomplete in the notes editor
recommendations.description: MarkdownEditor.swift has checkbox handling (regex at :290) but no link autocomplete — grep for [[ in the editor finds nothing. Add an inline popover triggered on '[[' (and '@') that fuzzy-matches WorkspaceIndex entities (meetings, people, projects, tags) and inserts a real [[slug]] wikilink resolving both in-app and in Obsidian (on-disk markdown is already Obsidian-native via ObsidianExporter). Makes the graph self-assemble from authoring, not extraction only.
recommendations.userValue: Type [[acm and link to the Acme meeting in two keystrokes; the link is bidirectional and shows as a backlink on Acme's page and in Obsidian. Reflect cmd+J / Tana mentions are the bar.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: prior C2-3 focused on AUTOMATIC pipeline backlinks; this is the manual-authoring counterpart ([[ autocomplete) which is still entirely absent and is PKM table-stakes.
recommendations.dependencies: MarkdownEditor, WorkspaceIndex entity enumeration
recommendations.risks: NSTextView popover positioning; debounce the fuzzy query.

recommendations.title: Lean into 'your vault IS an Obsidian vault' as a positioning moat
recommendations.description: ObsidianExporter already writes every meeting as Obsidian-native markdown (frontmatter, [[wikilinks]], #tags, ## People) into the vault, and DailyNoteWriter writes Obsidian periodic notes. No AI notetaker competitor (Granola, Otter, Fathom, Fireflies) gives a plain-markdown, Obsidian-openable archive — they lock notes in their cloud. Surface it: an onboarding/Integrations step 'Open my vault in Obsidian' that writes a starter .obsidian config + vault README, plus a Settings toggle to mirror notes.md edits back from Obsidian (watch the folder) for a two-way loop.
recommendations.userValue: Total data ownership, zero lock-in: meetings, decisions, daily notes and the people graph are queryable in Obsidian's plugin ecosystem too — a story Granola/Otter structurally cannot tell.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new angle: prior comp docs treated Obsidian only as a competitor to copy FROM; this flips it — the app's existing Obsidian-native output is an unexploited positioning weapon vs cloud notetakers.
recommendations.dependencies: ObsidianExporter, DailyNoteWriter, IntegrationsView
recommendations.risks: Two-way file watching needs conflict handling; keep mirror opt-in, one-directional by default.

recommendations.title: Smart Collections: saved, live-updating semantic+structured queries
recommendations.description: No saved-search/collection concept exists (grep for SmartCollection/SavedSearch returns nothing). Add a SmartCollection model (query string + filters: entity kind, tag, person, date range) materialized as a pinnable sidebar item (WorkspaceRouter / ActionItemsSidebar), resolving live against the hybrid FTS+embedding index already wired into GlobalSearchView. Seed with 'Open commitments to me', 'Pricing decisions (90d)', 'People overdue for check-in'.
recommendations.userValue: A self-maintaining 'Pricing decisions' or 'Commitments to me' page that's always current — Tana 'live searches' / Mem Collections. Turns one-off search into standing topic views.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-7 (still entirely unbuilt) but now low-risk because the ranked hybrid query already exists in PeopleStore/GlobalSearchView.
recommendations.dependencies: GlobalSearchView hybrid search, WorkspaceRouter, DecisionStore
recommendations.risks: Live re-query cost; cache and refresh on vault-change notifications.

recommendations.title: Upgrade decision/commitment extraction to attributed, LLM-backed atoms
recommendations.description: DecisionStore.parseDecisions (Decisions/DecisionStore.swift:61) only scrapes bullets under a '## Key Decisions' heading the summary may not contain — no speaker attribution, no transcript fallback, brittle to wording. Extend the Ollama summary pipeline (TranscriptPolisher / MeetingPipelineController) to emit typed atoms: Decisions, Commitments (who -> what -> by-when, attributed to a PeopleStore person), Open Questions, persisted as first-class indexed entities. Owner attribution lets commitments show on each person's page and become graph edges.
recommendations.userValue: 'Who committed to what, by when?' becomes a queryable, attributed ledger across all meetings — the highest-value recall query, the one raw transcript search fails at. Tana auto-extracts decisions/follow-ups from transcripts.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-8 but sharpens it: the ledger landed yet is markdown-heading-fragile and unattributed — the net-new work is robust LLM extraction + person attribution, not the store itself.
recommendations.dependencies: DecisionStore, TranscriptPolisher, OllamaService, PeopleStore
recommendations.risks: LLM extraction adds latency; run async post-finalize like embeddings already do.

recommendations.title: Active-recall resurfacing: weekly 'Brain Bits' digest of YOUR notes
recommendations.description: TodayView has 'On this day' (TodayView.swift:257) but no scheduled, intent-based resurfacing of decisions/notes the way Mem 'Heads Up'/Daily Digest and 'Daily Brain Bits' do for original notes. Add a scheduled job (reuse Notifications + RecordingMonitor scheduling) that picks N aging-but-relevant items (decisions, open commitments, notes semantically near upcoming-calendar topics via EmbeddingService) and delivers a 'Brain Bits' card on Today plus an optional notification, on a spaced-repetition cadence so the same item resurfaces with decreasing frequency.
recommendations.userValue: Your own past decisions and commitments resurface before they're forgotten, tied to what's coming up this week — the 'compounding knowledge' behavior Mem/Reflect are known for, applied to meetings.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new vs C2-6 (pre-meeting-brief resurfacing): this is a scheduled, spaced, vault-wide digest of the user's OWN decisions/notes, grounded in current Mem Heads Up / Daily Brain Bits 2026 patterns.
recommendations.dependencies: EmbeddingService, DecisionStore, Notifications, Calendar
recommendations.risks: Resurfacing the wrong things erodes trust; allow thumbs-down to suppress, bias toward overdue commitments.

recommendations.title: Per-tag structured summary templates (supertag-style schemas)
recommendations.description: NoteTemplate.swift offers static editor templates (meeting-notes, 1:1, standup, decision-log) but they don't drive the AI summary and there's no per-tag schema. Let a tag (#1on1, #sales-call, #standup) bind a summary template that steers the Ollama prompt to emit a consistent structured shape (1:1 -> Wins/Blockers/Feedback/Commitments). Tana's supertag idea applied to meetings: the tag IS the schema. Consistent summaries also feed Smart Collections and decision extraction cleanly.
recommendations.userValue: Every 1:1 summarizes into the same shape automatically; sales calls always surface next-steps + objections. Consistency makes recall, collections and graph topic-nodes far more reliable.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces a prior 'per-tag templates' line item but reframed through the 2026 Tana-supertag-schema lens and wired to the AI summary (not just editor insertion, which NoteTemplate already does).
recommendations.dependencies: NoteTemplate, TagStore, OllamaService summary prompt, DecisionStore
recommendations.risks: Too many templates to maintain; ship 3-4 built-ins + one custom slot.

recommendations.title: Generalize custom properties from tasks to people, meetings & projects
recommendations.description: PropertyDefinition/PropertyValue + CustomPropertyRow.swift already give tasks typed user-defined columns — a real structured-object primitive. Extend the same property system to Person, Meeting and Project (VaultKit.Person has only fixed fields: emails/company/role/tags). Render a typed property block on PersonDetailView and UnifiedMeetingDetail. Turns entities into Capacities/Tana-style typed objects without a new framework.
recommendations.userValue: Track 'where we met', 'kids names', 'renewal date', 'deal stage' as typed fields on a person or meeting — the object-first modeling Capacities/Tana users want, reusing UI you already have.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new: prior comp docs never noticed the task property system (CustomPropertyRow/PropertyDefinition) could be lifted into the app's general typed-object layer — a cheap path to Capacities-style entities.
recommendations.dependencies: PropertyDefinition/PropertyValue, PersonDetailView, UnifiedMeetingDetail, VaultKit.Person
recommendations.risks: Schema/migration for stored properties; keep values optional and additive.

recommendations.title: Render typed backlinks ('Mentioned in') on People & Project pages
recommendations.description: WorkspaceIndex.backlinks (WorkspaceIndex.swift:61) only resolves backlinks TO a meeting by scanning notes/summary/project bodies for literal meetingscribe:// URLs, rendered only in UnifiedMeetingDetail (backlinks state at :55). People and Projects have no backlinks panel. Compute and render a 'Mentioned in' section on PersonDetailView and ActionItemsProjectPage from (a) attendance, (b) [[wikilink]]/@mention authoring, (c) embedding-similar meetings (relatedMeetingIDs already exists).
recommendations.userValue: Open a person or project and immediately see every meeting and note referencing them, zero manual tagging — the bidirectional-link payoff currently only half-present on meetings.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces C2-3's render half (auto-extraction partly landed via relatedMeetingIDs); verified gap is that backlinks are meeting-only and URL-scrape-only — people/projects get none.
recommendations.dependencies: WorkspaceIndex, PersonDetailView, ActionItemsProjectPage, PeopleStore.relatedMeetingIDs
recommendations.risks: URL-scan path is slow; move to the index rather than re-scanning files.

recommendations.title: Topic & person timelines: 'everything with Acme, chronologically'
recommendations.description: No chronological topic/person view exists — TodayView's 'On this day' is date-pivoted, not entity-pivoted. Add a timeline tab on PersonDetailView and a topic/tag timeline from GlobalSearchView listing meetings, decisions, encounters and notes for that entity in date order, backed by the date_epoch the vault index already stores.
recommendations.userValue: Rediscover the full arc of a relationship or topic — every Acme touchpoint and decision in order — which keyword search alone can't reconstruct. Reflect's temporal patterning applied to entities.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces the temporal half of C2-9 but distinguishes entity-timelines from the 'on this day' date-resurfacing that already shipped — per-entity chronology is still missing.
recommendations.dependencies: vault_content date_epoch index, PersonDetailView, DecisionStore, Encounter
recommendations.risks: Merging heterogeneous item types into one sorted stream; define a common timeline-item protocol.

audits.summary: Since the last competitive audit, MeetingScribe has quietly shipped the category's hardest features: local RAG grounding with citations (ChatSession.groundedSystemPrompt), on-device semantic embeddings (EmbeddingService + vault_embeddings table), daily-note rollups (DailyNoteWriter), and a 23-tool MCP including get_coaching_context and log_encounter. That puts it ahead of Granola/Dex/Limitless on the local-first axis exactly as Meta's Limitless shutdown, Amazon's Bee buy, and Pieces' "drop Ollama for a custom local stack" pivot made on-device trust the decisive moat. The remaining gaps are not capability gaps but surfacing, recall-breadth, retention/forgetting, MCP discoverability/transport, and a missing Apple Foundation Models fallback — the moves that would let MeetingScribe own the "second brain you actually own" position outright.

recommendations.title: Broaden RAG grounding beyond meetings to the whole vault (voice notes, encounters, people, daily notes)
recommendations.description: ChatSession.groundedSystemPrompt() (Sources/MeetingScribe/Chat/ChatSession.swift:245) only grounds on results where entityKind == "meeting" and caps at prefix(5). PeopleStore.searchVaultHybrid already returns mixed entity kinds. Extend the grounding builder to also render voice notes (QuickNotes), encounters, person bios/attachedNotes, and DailyNoteWriter day-rollups into the RETRIEVED VAULT CONTEXT block, each with its own meetingscribe:// citation deep-link. Add per-kind snippet formatters and raise the cap to a token-budgeted ~8 passages.
recommendations.userValue: Today 'what did Sarah say about Q3?' can miss the voice note or encounter where it actually lives. Whole-vault grounding is the difference between 'meeting search' and a real second brain — the exact gap Granola's 'super-human memory' is racing to fill, but here 100% on-device.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-1 ('Ask My Memory') has SHIPPED as the C2-2 retrieve-then-ground loop; this is the net-new next step — the shipped version is meeting-only and under-retrieves.

recommendations.title: Retention & Right-to-Forget: audio-vs-transcript TTL plus one-click 'forget' across vault + FTS5 + embeddings
recommendations.description: There is no retention or forget primitive anywhere (grep for retention/purge/ttl in AppSettings + SettingsView returns nothing). Add AppSettings fields (deleteRawAudioAfterDays, purgeMeetingsAfterMonths) honored by a sweep in MeetingPipelineController, plus a 'Forget this meeting/person' action that hard-deletes the folder, the SecondBrainDB rows, the FTS index, AND the new vault_embeddings vectors (SecondBrainDB.upsertEmbedding has no delete counterpart — add deleteEmbedding(entityID:)). Surface controls in SettingsView under a Privacy/Storage section.
recommendations.userValue: The vault grows unbounded forever and embeddings/FTS rows are never cleaned. Limitless ships custom audio-retention windows; a genuine forget primitive is both storage hygiene and a privacy guarantee competitors fake. It is also a hard prerequisite for any always-on mode.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-5 proposed retention; still NOT built. Reinforces it, but now load-bearing because embeddings exist and currently leak orphaned vectors on delete.

recommendations.title: Add a unified search_everything MCP tool so Claude Desktop gets the hybrid recall the app already has
recommendations.description: MCP dispatch (Sources/MeetingScribeMCP/main.swift:1782+) has 23 tools but NO search tool — Claude must call list_meetings + get_meeting per result to answer 'what did we decide about pricing?'. The app already has searchVaultHybrid (semantic + FTS) in PeopleStore. Expose a search_everything(query, kinds?, limit) MCP tool that reads the same SecondBrainDB (FTS5 + vault_embeddings) and returns ranked cross-entity hits (meetings, voice notes, people, encounters, action items) with deep-links. The DB path is already known to the bundled binary.
recommendations.userValue: Granola's MCP killer feature is 'search all my notes' (read-only). MeetingScribe can match it AND extend across its richer people/encounter graph in one call — a single 'when did I last discuss Stripe with Jordan?' instead of N round-trips.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-3 proposed this; the underlying hybrid search has SINCE been built in-app but is still absent from the MCP surface — so it's now low-risk to expose.

recommendations.title: MCP resources/list with an auto-injected 'today brief' (overdue people, due tasks, recent meetings)
recommendations.description: The MCP server only handles initialize / tools/list / tools/call (main.swift:1862-1868) — no resources primitive. Add resources/list + resources/read exposing a 'relationship-brief' / 'today' resource computed from existing tool_listOverdueCheckIns, action-item due dates, and recent meetings. Claude Desktop reads resources at session start, so personal context flows WITHOUT the user asking — turning the server from a tool Claude calls into context Claude already has.
recommendations.userValue: Every competitor's MCP (Granola, Dex) requires the user to ask before any personal data flows. Proactive session-start context is one architectural step ahead and makes MeetingScribe feel like a coach, not a database.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-1 (MCP resources brief) still NOT built — server has no resources method at all. Endorse and carry forward.

recommendations.title: Embed local-first privacy guarantee in MCP server/info and tool descriptions
recommendations.description: The initialize/server info response and tool descriptions in main.swift contain no privacy statement (only an OS-permission error string mentions Privacy). Add a one-line 'All data is read from this Mac's local storage; nothing is transmitted to any server' to the serverInfo and to the highest-traffic tool descriptions. When MeetingScribe appears alongside Granola/Dex in Claude's connected tools, this line is the differentiator users see.
recommendations.userValue: After Meta's Limitless shutdown and Amazon's Bee acquisition, 'local-first' is now an explicit purchase criterion (LMCP, Screenpipe market on it). MeetingScribe's strongest claim is currently invisible at the protocol layer.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-2; trivially small, still unshipped, high signal-to-effort. Carry forward.

recommendations.title: On-device Recall Timeline: a day-partitioned 'your recorded life' view as the anti-Recall
recommendations.description: There is no timeline/recall surface (UI dir has only ActionItemsCalendarView + TodayView). Build a scrollable day/week/month timeline aggregating meetings, voice notes, encounters, action items, and the new DailyNoteWriter rollups into one chronological 'memory' surface, each row deep-linking via meetingscribe://. Explicitly framed as the anti-Microsoft-Recall: only things the user chose to record, no screenshots, no plaintext snapshot store, no cloud.
recommendations.userValue: Reframes the app from 'a list of meetings' to 'your recorded life on a timeline you own' — the recall/lifelog UX people wanted from Rewind/Recall, minus the surveillance that got Recall banned at institutions.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-2 (repurpose dead CalendarTabView) — that view is now gone, so this is a net-new build; DailyNoteWriter (newly shipped) gives it a ready data spine.

recommendations.title: Apple Foundation Models as a zero-setup local fallback when Ollama is absent
recommendations.description: OllamaService and the new EmbeddingService both hard-depend on a running Ollama (EmbeddingService returns nil if the endpoint/model is missing; summaries fail similarly). macOS 26 Tahoe ships a 3B on-device LLM via the FoundationModels framework (no code path references it). Add a FoundationModelsService conforming to the same summarize/chat interface, selected automatically when Ollama is unreachable, so polish/summary/grounded-chat degrade to Apple's on-device model instead of failing — still fully local.
recommendations.userValue: Removes the single biggest onboarding cliff (install + pull a multi-GB model) for the exact privacy-conscious Mac demographic, while keeping the 'nothing leaves your device' promise. Pieces just rebuilt its stack to drop the Ollama dependency for the same reason.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new — no prior audit considered Apple's FoundationModels framework (it post-dates them); turns a macOS-26 platform capability into a differentiator.

recommendations.title: Capture provenance & consent receipt stamped on every recording
recommendations.description: meeting.json carries no record of HOW a recording was captured (manual vs AmbientMeetingDetector-prompted vs AppDetector-triggered) or under what consent. Add a small provenance block (captureMethod, triggeredBy, detectedSpeakers, jurisdictionAtRecord) written at finalize in MeetingPipelineController, and surface a one-line 'how this was captured' note in UnifiedMeetingDetail. Directly answers the Brewer v. Otter.ai class of risk.
recommendations.userValue: Auditability and legal defensibility — the user can prove how and under what consent each artifact was created. Reinforces the trust brand that is MeetingScribe's whole moat.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-7; still unbuilt (no provenance fields in the schema). Cheap, stands alone, carry forward.

recommendations.title: Speaker-attributed recall: cite WHO said it, not just which meeting
recommendations.description: groundedSystemPrompt cites a meeting but not a speaker; transcripts carry diarization but the grounded context flattens to summary.prefix(1200). When a retrieved passage has speaker labels, render 'Sarah: …' lines and let the local model attribute answers ('Sarah committed to X on <date>'). Pull from the diarized transcript rather than the summary when answering attribution questions.
recommendations.userValue: Limitless's headline is 'who said what.' MeetingScribe records diarized audio but throws that signal away at recall time. Speaker-attributed answers are the difference between a summary and a record you can act on.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new at the recall layer — prior plans treated diarization as transcript polish; this makes it load-bearing for grounded Q&A.

recommendations.title: Streamable HTTP transport for the MCP server (alongside stdio) for Claude.ai web
recommendations.description: The server is stdio-only (readLine loop at main.swift:1891), so only Claude Desktop can reach it; Claude.ai web and remote agent workflows cannot. Add an optional Streamable HTTP transport (Nov-2025 spec standard) behind a launch flag, with a localhost bind + token auth so the local-first guarantee holds, reusing the existing JSON-RPC dispatch.
recommendations.userValue: As Claude.ai's web MCP support matures, stdio-only servers are locked out. Granola's personal/enterprise APIs are HTTP. This keeps MeetingScribe reachable from where users increasingly run Claude.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-4; still stdio-only. Lower urgency than search/resources, but carry forward.

recommendations.title: Publish the MCP server in the public registry as a standalone discovery channel
recommendations.description: The 23-tool server is only discoverable after buying/downloading the app — zero organic reach. Extract MeetingScribeMCP build artifacts + a claude_desktop_config.json snippet and README, and list on the Anthropic MCP registry / mcpservers.org / mcpmarket.com (where Granola, Dex, Screenpipe already appear). Package via a Homebrew tap pointing at the bundled binary path.
recommendations.userValue: The 10k+-server MCP ecosystem is a distribution channel, not a feature. Every Claude power user searching 'local personal data MCP' should find MeetingScribe — it's the only one with write tools + a people graph + on-device privacy.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-6; unshipped and now stronger because the server has grown to 23 tools including coaching/encounter tools no competitor lists.

recommendations.title: Personal-AI profile object that personalizes recall and coaching context
recommendations.description: groundedSystemPrompt and get_coaching_context have no standing user profile — every query starts without knowing the user's role, key projects, preferred terminology, or core people. Build an editable, user-owned profile (assembled from the People graph + recurring tags + the user's name) injected as standing context into both the grounded chat prompt and the MCP coaching tool. Surface it in Settings so it's legible and editable (transparency), and make it queryable ('who is my AI built from?').
recommendations.userValue: Personalized recall the way Personal.ai promises, but legible and owned rather than an opaque cloud model 'trained on you.' Makes both Ask-My-Memory answers and coaching context sharper with zero off-device data.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-6 (persona) — still unbuilt; now has two concrete consumers (the shipped RAG loop and get_coaching_context) to feed, raising its leverage.

recommendations.title: Memory-coverage honesty surface: tell the truth about gaps in recall
recommendations.description: Add a small surface (in SettingsView or a Recall/Timeline header) reporting recall integrity: meetings with transcript vs audio-only, items missing embeddings in vault_embeddings, finalize failures, total coverage — with a one-click 'repair/re-embed' action. The personal-AI promise collapses silently the moment recall has holes the user can't see.
recommendations.userValue: Trust through honesty. 'You have 142 meetings, 9 missing transcripts, 4 un-indexed — repair?' turns invisible integrity debt into a user-visible, fixable feature, and protects the grounded-chat quality that now depends on the embedding index.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Prior C4-8; now more concrete because there's a real embeddings index (vault_embeddings) that can silently fall behind the vault.

audits.summary: MeetingScribe's local-first moat is real, but it is conspicuously under-exploiting the macOS 26 Tahoe platform it ships on: the on-device Apple Foundation Models LLM (free, zero-install, in every Tahoe Mac) is unused while the app forces an Ollama install; App Intents are a single stubbed intent so the app is invisible to Tahoe's new Spotlight Actions; there is no real WidgetKit/Control Center surface, no Spotlight index, no EventKit/Reminders/Notes write-back, and no Continuity/Handoff. Competitors (Granola, Otter, Apple's own apps) are racing into exactly these native hooks; closing them turns "private notetaker" into "the Mac's native AI memory layer."

recommendations.title: Add Apple Foundation Models as a zero-install on-device LLM backend
recommendations.description: Sources/ScribeCore/AI/OllamaService.swift is the only LLM backend, forcing every user to install Ollama + pull a model during onboarding (OnboardingSheet/SetupReadiness gate on it). macOS 26 Tahoe ships Apple's on-device Foundation Models LLM in every Apple-Silicon Mac. Introduce an `LLMProvider` protocol and a `FoundationModelsService` conforming to it (import FoundationModels, SystemLanguageModel.default, guided `@Generable` structs for action-item/people/summary extraction — exactly the summarization/extraction/classification tasks Apple's model is tuned for). Make it the default backend when available, with Ollama as the heavier-reasoning fallback. Route the existing polish/summarize/extract calls through the protocol.
recommendations.userValue: Onboarding goes from 'download a 4GB model + install Ollama' to 'works out of the box', the single biggest setup-friction killer. Still 100% on-device, so the privacy moat is intact while the install moat disappears.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Prior comp docs (C4, G5_personalai) treat Ollama as fixed and never mention Apple Foundation Models; this removes the app's #1 setup barrier using a framework that did not exist when those plans were written.
recommendations.dependencies: Define LLMProvider protocol; gate on SystemLanguageModel.availability; keep Ollama path for long-context jobs.
recommendations.risks: Apple's model has small context window and weaker reasoning; need length-aware routing (short extraction → Apple, long transcript synthesis → Ollama).

recommendations.title: Turn the stubbed App Intent into a real Spotlight/Shortcuts action suite
recommendations.description: Sources/MeetingScribe/Widgets/QuickAddWidget.swift has exactly one AppIntent (QuickAddMeetingIntent) and it is a stub — perform() just returns a dialog with a TODO; it never creates anything. In Tahoe, Spotlight executes app actions via App Intents with inline parameter fields. Wire QuickAddMeetingIntent to actually post a draft (NotificationCenter → MeetingManager, since intents run out-of-process) and add a real intent suite: StartMeetingRecordingIntent, CaptureQuickNoteIntent (text param), AddActionItemIntent, AskMyVaultIntent (query param → returns an Ollama/FoundationModels answer as dialog), LogEncounterIntent(person). Expose AppEntity for Meeting/Person/ActionItem so they're parameterizable. Register all via MeetingScribeAppShortcuts.
recommendations.userValue: Type 'add action item…' or 'ask my vault…' straight into Spotlight or run it from Shortcuts/Action button without opening the app — the app becomes a system-level verb, matching how Tahoe users now expect every app to behave.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new and high-leverage: the intent is already half-built (a stub with a TODO) but no prior plan calls out finishing it or that Tahoe's Spotlight Actions make it suddenly table-stakes.
recommendations.dependencies: AppEntity/EntityQuery for Meeting/Person; reuse the deep-link router in MeetingScribeApp.onOpenURL.
recommendations.risks: Intents run outside the app lifecycle; must persist via the iCloud inbox/vault path rather than touching live in-memory state.

recommendations.title: Ship a real WidgetKit + Control Center extension (Needs-Attention / Quick Capture)
recommendations.description: ActionItemsWidget.swift and NeedsAttentionWidget.swift are plain SwiftUI Views embedded in TodayView — there is no WidgetKit extension (no TimelineProvider, no Widget protocol, no widget bundle target). So none of this is reachable from Notification Center, the desktop, the macOS menu-bar widget area, or Control Center. Add a widget extension target with: a Needs-Attention widget (overdue tasks + people to reconnect from RelationshipNotificationManager), an Action-Items-due-today widget, and Tahoe Control Center controls (ControlWidget) for Start Recording and Quick Note that fire the new App Intents.
recommendations.userValue: Today's open tasks and 'who to reconnect with' live on the desktop/Notification Center, and one-tap Start-Recording / Quick-Note from Control Center — the always-glanceable surface every Mac productivity app now ships.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. The in-app 'widgets' exist but no prior doc notices they are not WidgetKit widgets and thus invisible to the OS widget surfaces.
recommendations.dependencies: App Group + shared snapshot store so the extension reads vault state; the App Intent suite for Control Center actions.
recommendations.risks: Widget extension needs an App Group container; vault data must be mirrored into a lightweight shared file the sandboxed extension can read.

recommendations.title: Index meetings, people, and action items into Spotlight (CoreSpotlight)
recommendations.description: There is no CoreSpotlight usage anywhere (only comments mention Spotlight). The vault already has an FTS5 index (WorkspaceIndex/SecondBrainDB) and a meetingscribe:// deep-link scheme routed in MeetingScribeApp.onOpenURL. Add a CSSearchableIndex pass on finalize/update: index each meeting (title, date, summary, attendees), person, and action item as CSSearchableItem with a relatedUniqueIdentifier carrying the meetingscribe:// URL, so a system Spotlight hit deep-links straight into UnifiedMeetingDetail. Donate NSUserActivity for the currently-open meeting so it's continuable/searchable.
recommendations.userValue: Find any meeting or person from the system Spotlight bar (or the new Tahoe Spotlight) and jump right into it — the vault becomes part of the Mac's universal search instead of a walled garden.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new. Prior plans wire FTS5 into the app-internal GlobalSearchView; none push the index out to the OS-level Spotlight where users actually start searches.
recommendations.dependencies: Reuse existing meetingscribe:// scheme + WorkspaceLink.parse for result routing.
recommendations.risks: Must purge index entries on delete/trash to avoid dangling Spotlight hits; respect the privacy posture (allow opt-out).

recommendations.title: Write action items to Apple Reminders and 'Schedule next' to Calendar via EventKit
recommendations.description: EventKit is used read-only (CalendarService/CalendarStoreActor only convert EKEvents to read upcoming meetings; there is no EKEventStore.save, no EKReminder anywhere — the 'Reminder' hits are local UserNotifications, not Reminders.app). Add an optional EventKit write path: mirror an action item into Apple Reminders (EKReminder with due date + the meetingscribe:// URL in the notes), and a 'Schedule follow-up' button that creates an EKEvent for the next meeting. Two-way: completing in Reminders flips ActionItem.status on next sync.
recommendations.userValue: Action items land in the Reminders app the user already lives in (and on iPhone/Watch via iCloud) instead of being trapped in MeetingScribe — and 'schedule next meeting' is one click, the first agentic post-meeting action competitors all ship.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Reinforces the agentic-fan-out idea from G5_comp_notetakers C1-3, but adds the specific, missed observation that EventKit is currently read-only and Reminders is entirely unused — the native write surface is the net-new part.
recommendations.dependencies: requestFullAccessToReminders/Events; an ActionItem↔EKReminder id map for two-way sync.
recommendations.risks: Calendar/Reminders write needs new TCC prompts; pin to the existing self-signed identity so it doesn't re-prompt on rebuild.

recommendations.title: Slide/screen-frame capture with on-device Vision OCR into the meeting note
recommendations.description: The app already holds Screen Recording permission and uses ScreenCaptureKit for system audio (2 files import ScreenCaptureKit), but there is zero Vision usage. On a meeting, periodically (or on detected slide-change) grab a frame via SCScreenshotManager, OCR it locally with Vision (VNRecognizeTextRequest), and pin the image + recognized text into the meeting note at its timestamp. Optionally trigger off AmbientMeetingDetector/AppDetector when Zoom/Meet is frontmost.
recommendations.userValue: 'What was on slide 4?' becomes answerable and searchable; shared decks/whiteboards stop being lost. Audio-only notetakers structurally can't do this, and it's fully on-device.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Endorses G5_comp_notetakers C1-4 (OtterPilot Visual Context) but my contribution is the concrete native stack (SCScreenshotManager + Vision VNRecognizeText) and that the screen-record permission is already held.
recommendations.dependencies: ScreenCaptureKit frame grab; Vision OCR; slide-change heuristic to avoid frame spam.
recommendations.risks: Frame capture cadence/storage growth; must respect retention policy and let users disable per-meeting.

recommendations.title: Apple Notes / Reminders two-way bridge (replace AppleScript-only import)
recommendations.description: AppleNotesImporter.swift reads people from Apple Notes one-way via AppleScript (Automation permission). Extend to a write path: on finalize, optionally push the polished summary into an Apple Notes folder (so notes sync to iPhone/iPad/Watch for free), and a 'meeting digest' note. Pair with the Reminders write above so the People-graph 'stay connected' nudges (StayConnectedSection/RelationshipNotificationManager) can become real Reminders the user sees on all devices.
recommendations.userValue: Summaries and reconnect nudges show up in Apple Notes/Reminders the user already syncs everywhere, giving the Mac-only app an iPhone presence with zero new mobile code.
recommendations.noveltyNote: Net-new direction: prior docs push Obsidian/markdown portability; none propose using Apple's own Notes/Reminders as the cross-device surface, which is the lighter path for an Apple-ecosystem user.
